name: REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB

topics.title: Tutorials on Air Refrigeration Cycle Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Tutorials on Air Refrigeration Cycle Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) An **Air Refrigeration Cycle** is a vapor-compression-like refrigeration system in which the working fluid remains in the **gaseous phase throughout the entire thermodynamic cycle** — it never condenses into a liquid. The working fluid is atmospheric air (or any other permanent gas), which acts both as the refrigerant and the heat-transfer medium. Because the system operates on a gas-cycle, the energy transfer occurs via **sensible heat exchange** (temperature change at constant phase), rather than the latent heat exchange used in conventional vapor-compression refrigeration. In a KTU laboratory context (PCAGL507 – Module Experiment 2), the student studies the **Bell-Coleman Air Refrigeration Cycle**, which is the practical, open-type realization of the **Reversed Carnot Cycle** applied to air as the working substance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The "Tutorials on Air Refrigeration Cycle Systems" experiment in PCAGL507 expects the student to (i) identify each process on the P-V and T-S diagrams, (ii) compute the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** and **Compressor Work (W)**, and (iii) interpret the influence of pressure ratio and temperature limits on cycle performance. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Firefighter's Blower" Imagine a **firefighter holding a high-powered electric blower in front of a burning room**. The blower pulls the hot, smoky air out, compresses it (pushing it harder), and forces it outside. To cool the room, the cycle is *reversed*: a separate blower draws the cold outside air in, compresses it (which actually *heats* it), then runs it through a long metal pipe (the cooler) so it dumps the heat of compression to the atmosphere, and finally expands it back through a nozzle — making it **colder than the outside air** — before blasting it into a cold storage room. This is exactly how the Bell-Coleman cycle works on a ship or in an air-craft refrigeration unit. **Air is the "firefighter's air", the cooler is the long metal pipe, and the cold storage is the room we want to freeze.** ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants for Air (to be remembered) | Property | Symbol | Value (at STP) | Units | |----------|--------|----------------|-------| | Specific heat at constant pressure | $c_p$ | **1.005** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Specific heat at constant volume | $c_v$ | **0.718** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Ratio of specific heats | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | dimensionless | | Characteristic gas constant | $R$ | **0.287** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Universal gas constant | $\bar{R}$ | **8.314** | kJ/(kmol·K) | > [!NOTE] > **Why these values matter:** Because the air cycle is *isentropic* in compression and expansion, $\gamma = 1.4$ is the single most-cited constant in all numerical problems of this experiment. A wrong value of $\gamma$ gives wrong $T_2/T_1$ ratios, and the entire COP calculation collapses. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Reference Cycles The tutorials are built on **two idealized cycles**. Every Bell-Coleman problem is solved by relating it to the Reversed Carnot framework and then applying the pressure-ratio constraint. #### A. Reversed Carnot Cycle (The Ideal Benchmark) A completely reversible cycle in which a *gas* (here, air) undergoes: - **Process 1 → 2:** Reversible *isothermal* compression (heat rejected at $T_H$). - **Process 2 → 3:** Reversible *isentropic* (adiabatic) compression. - **Process 3 → 4:** Reversible *isothermal* expansion (heat absorbed at $T_C$). - **Process 4 → 1:** Reversible *isentropic* expansion back to state 1. **Limitation:** It is impossible to build a real machine that performs true isothermal compression/expansion of a gas (you cannot simultaneously compress and reject heat in a finite-time stroke). Hence, the Reversed Carnot is *theoretical only* — its sole job is to set the *upper limit* of COP. #### B. Bell-Coleman Cycle (The Practical Air Cycle) A four-process, **open-type** (or closed-type) gas cycle: - **Process 1 → 2:** Reversible *adiabatic (isentropic) compression* in a **compressor**. Temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_2$. - **Process 2 → 3:** *Constant-pressure* heat rejection in an **after-cooler** to the atmosphere. Temperature drops from $T_2$ to $T_3$. - **Process 3 → 4:** Reversible *adiabatic (isentropic) expansion* in an **expander** (or a throttling nozzle in real ships). Temperature drops from $T_3$ to $T_4$. - **Process 4 → 1:** *Constant-pressure* heat absorption in the **cold chamber (refrigerated space)**. Temperature rises from $T_4$ to $T_1$, producing the **refrigerating effect**. ### 2.2 The Governing Physics — "Why" and "How" - **Why isentropic compression?** Real reciprocating compressors are rapid; there is insufficient time for heat to leave the gas. So we model the process as adiabatic and reversible → **isentropic**. - **Why constant-pressure heat exchange?** Air moves through the cooler and the cold chamber as a steady stream. The ducts are large enough that frictional pressure drop is neglected. - **Why expand instead of throttle?** Theoretically, an isentropic expander recovers work. In cheap realizations (like a ship's cold-air machine), a throttling nozzle replaces the expander, and the cycle becomes a *Joule (gas) refrigeration cycle* with reduced COP. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Realization:** In the KTU lab, the experimental Bell-Coleman rig uses a **reciprocating compressor** and a **piston expander** coupled on the same shaft. The expander delivers work back, reducing the net shaft work input — a unique feature no vapor-compression system can offer. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units | |---|----------|---------|--------------------| | 1 | Isentropic temperature relation | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | $r_p = p_2/p_1$ is the pressure ratio | | 2 | Isentropic temperature relation (expansion) | $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Same pressure ratio as compression | | 3 | Combined identity | $T_2 / T_1 \;=\; T_3 / T_4$ | Because both processes share $r_p$ | | 4 | Compressor work (per kg of air) | $W_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg, isentropic compression | | 5 | Expander work (per kg of air) | $W_e = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg, isentropic expansion | | 6 | Net shaft work | $W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$ | kJ/kg, positive when $W_c > W_e$ | | 7 | Heat rejected in cooler | $Q_{rej} = c_p (T_2 - T_3)$ | kJ/kg, hot side (positive number) | | 8 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = c_p (T_1 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg, cold side (positive number) | | 9 | **COP of Bell-Coleman** | $\displaystyle COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | COP of Reversed Carnot | $\displaystyle COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ | For ideal benchmark | | 11 | Mass flow rate (capacity) | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\text{Capacity (TR)} \times 3.517}{c_p (T_1 - T_4)}$ | kg/s, where 1 TR = 3.517 kW | | 12 | Volumetric efficiency relation | $\dfrac{p_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{p_4 V_4}{T_4}$ | Ideal gas; gives $V_4/V_1$ | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Safety Note:** In the markdown table above, every vertical bar `\vert` or `\mid` has been used instead of the raw `|` character. Do not use `|` in your answer sheets to avoid breaking the renderer — KTU does not penalize you for this, but it keeps the formulas clean. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Air refrigeration systems dominated the field before vapor-compression technology matured. They are still preferred in: - **Air-craft air-conditioning packs** (because the working fluid is readily available at altitude). - **Marine cold-storage on cargo ships** (Bell-Coleman was invented by Bell & Coleman in 1902 precisely for this). - **Pneumatic tools and mining equipment** where any leakage of refrigerant is harmless. - **Gas liquefaction plants** (reverse-stirling and Linde-Hampson cycles). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Isentropic Temperature–Pressure Relation for Air **Statement to prove:** For an ideal gas undergoing a reversible adiabatic (isentropic) process, $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ **Step 1 — Start with the first law for a closed system undergoing a reversible adiabatic process.** For an adiabatic process, $\delta Q = 0$. The first law becomes $dQ = du + \delta W$, so $\delta W = -du$. For a reversible process in a closed system, $\delta W = p \, dv$. **Step 2 — Substitute $du = c_v \, dT$ for an ideal gas.** $$p \, dv = -c_v \, dT$$ **Step 3 — Apply the ideal-gas equation $pv = RT$, differentiated.** Differentiating $pv = RT$ gives: $$p \, dv + v \, dp = R \, dT$$ **Step 4 — Solve the two equations for $v \, dp$.** From Step 2, $p \, dv = -c_v \, dT$. Substituting into Step 3: $$-c_v \, dT + v \, dp = R \, dT$$ $$v \, dp = (R + c_v) \, dT$$ $$v \, dp = c_p \, dT$$ (Because for an ideal gas, $c_p - c_v = R$, so $R + c_v = c_p$.) **Step 5 — Form the ratio $\dfrac{v \, dp}{p \, dv}$.** Dividing the two key equations: $$\frac{v \, dp}{p \, dv} = \frac{c_p \, dT}{-c_v \, dT} = -\frac{c_p}{c_v} = -\gamma$$ **Step 6 — Rewrite the differential equation.** $$v \, dp + \gamma \, p \, dv = 0$$ **Step 7 — Integrate from state 1 to state 2.** Using the integrating-factor technique (divide by $pv$): $$\frac{dp}{p} + \gamma \, \frac{dv}{v} = 0$$ $$\int_{p_1}^{p_2} \frac{dp}{p} + \gamma \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \frac{dv}{v} = 0$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right) + \gamma \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) = 0$$ $$\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right) \left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)^{\gamma} = 1$$ **Step 8 — Use the ideal-gas law to eliminate $v$.** From $pv = RT$, $\dfrac{v_2}{v_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \dfrac{p_1}{p_2}$. Substituting: $$\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right) \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma} \left(\frac{p_1}{p_2}\right)^{\gamma} = 1$$ $$\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1-\gamma} \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma} = 1$$ **Step 9 — Solve for $T_2/T_1$.** $$\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\;}$$ This is the master relation used in *every* Bell-Coleman numerical problem. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Refrigerating Effect and COP of Bell-Coleman We derive the COP from the first law applied to the *cyclic* working fluid. **Step 1 — Apply steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to the compressor (1 → 2).** $$h_1 + W_c = h_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_c = h_2 - h_1 = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$ **Step 2 — Apply SFEE to the after-cooler (2 → 3) at constant pressure.** $$h_2 = h_3 + Q_{rej} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{rej} = c_p(T_2 - T_3)$$ **Step 3 — Apply SFEE to the expander (3 → 4).** $$h_3 = h_4 + W_e \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_e = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$$ **Step 4 — Apply SFEE to the cold chamber (4 → 1) at constant pressure.** $$h_4 + Q_{abs} = h_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{abs} = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = RE$$ **Step 5 — Net work is the difference (the expander recovers part of the work).** $$W_{net} = W_c - W_e = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$$ **Step 6 — Form the COP ratio.** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{BC} &= \frac{RE}{W_{net}} \\ &= \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]} \\ &= \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Apply the identity $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ to simplify.** Let $r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = k$. Then $T_2 = k T_1$ and $T_3 = k T_4$. Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= c_p[kT_1 - T_1 - kT_4 + T_4] \\ &= c_p[(k-1)(T_1 - T_4)] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute back into COP.** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{BC} &= \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p(k-1)(T_1 - T_4)} \\ &= \frac{1}{k-1} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 9 — The cleanest closed-form result.** $$\boxed{\;COP_{BC} = \frac{1}{r_p^{\,(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}\;}$$ This is the formula you quote directly in the answer sheet — it depends *only* on the pressure ratio, which makes physical sense: the bigger the pressure ratio, the hotter $T_2$ and the colder $T_4$, but the compressor work grows much faster than the refrigerating effect. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Flow Rate from Given Tonnage **Given:** A Bell-Coleman machine has a refrigerating capacity of $N$ tonnes of refrigeration (TR). Compute the mass flow rate of air. **Step 1 — Recall the definition of 1 TR.** $$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 3.517 \text{ kJ/s}$$ **Step 2 — The total cooling load (in kW).** $$\dot{Q}_{abs} = N \times 3.517 \;\; \text{kW}$$ **Step 3 — Equate to the per-kg refrigerating effect.** $$\dot{m} \times c_p (T_1 - T_4) = N \times 3.517$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\dot{m}$.** $$\boxed{\;\dot{m} = \frac{3.517 \, N}{c_p (T_1 - T_4)} \;\; \text{kg/s}\;}$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** Air enters the compressor of a Bell-Coleman cycle at $T_1 = 290$ K and $p_1 = 1$ bar. The pressure ratio is $r_p = 4$. The air leaves the cooler at $T_3 = 310$ K and enters the cold chamber at $T_4 = 270$ K. Calculate (i) $T_2$, (ii) refrigerating effect per kg, (iii) net work per kg, and (iv) COP. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$. **Solution:** **(i) Compute $T_2$:** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 290 \times 4^{0.4/1.4} = 290 \times 4^{0.2857}$$ $$4^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 4} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.3961} = 1.4860$$ $$T_2 = 290 \times 1.4860 = 430.94 \text{ K}$$ **(ii) Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $$RE = c_p (T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (290 - 270) = 1.005 \times 20 = 20.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(iii) Net work per kg:** $$W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] = 1.005 \times [(430.94 - 290) - (310 - 270)]$$ $$= 1.005 \times [140.94 - 40] = 1.005 \times 100.94 = 101.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(iv) COP:** $$COP = \frac{20.10}{101.45} = 0.1981 \approx 0.198$$ **Cross-check using closed-form formula:** $$COP = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1} = \frac{1}{1.4860 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.4860} = 2.058$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** The two COP values do **not match** because the given data is *inconsistent* with an ideal Bell-Coleman. The closed-form formula assumes $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4$, but here $T_3/T_4 = 310/270 = 1.148 \neq 1.486$. In a real exam, prefer the direct formula $COP = (T_1-T_4)/[(T_2-T_1)-(T_3-T_4)]$ when temperatures are given numerically. The closed-form is valid *only* when the cycle is internally consistent. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Reversed Carnot vs Bell-Coleman | Feature | Reversed Carnot (with gas) | Bell-Coleman (Real Air Cycle) | |---------|----------------------------|-------------------------------| | Compression | Isothermal (ideal, impossible to realize) | Isentropic (realistic, by reciprocating piston) | | Expansion | Isothermal | Isentropic | | Heat exchangers | Infinitely slow, infinite area | Finite time, finite area, finite effectiveness | | COP | Highest possible | Always less than Carnot COP | | Practical use | None — theoretical benchmark only | Aircraft ECS, marine cold rooms, gas liquefaction | | Working fluid phase | Gas (does not condense) | Gas (does not condense) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Schematic of the Bell-Coleman Air Refrigeration Test Rig (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Atmospheric Air

RE divided by Wnet] S1 -- Pressure Ratio Only --> S3[Use Closed Form COP
1 divided by rp to power gamma minus 1 over gamma minus 1] S1 -- Capacity in TR --> S4[Compute Mass Flow Rate First
then Per kg Quantities] S2 --> S5[Calculate T2 from Isentropic Relation] S3 --> S5 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6[Compute RE cp T1 minus T4] S6 --> S7[Compute Wnet cp times T2 minus T1 minus T3 minus T4] S7 --> S8[COP RE divided by Wnet] S8 --> S9[Tabulate Results and Check Units] style S0 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600,color:#000 style S5 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000 style S8 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style S9 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — With Model Answers --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator and write the COP expression for the Bell-Coleman air refrigeration cycle.** **Model Answer:** The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired output (refrigerating effect) to the required input (net work supplied): $$COP = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}}$$ For the **Bell-Coleman air refrigeration cycle**, the COP is: $$COP_{BC} = \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$$ Equivalently, in terms of pressure ratio $r_p$: $$COP_{BC} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}$$ COP is a *dimensionless* number; values typically lie between 0.2 and 0.7 for practical Bell-Coleman machines. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any four advantages and two disadvantages of an air refrigeration system over a vapor-compression system.** **Model Answer:** **Advantages:** 1. **No phase change** — the working fluid (air) does not condense, so there is no evaporator or condenser of the latent-heat type; construction is simple and lightweight. 2. **Leakage is harmless** — air leakage causes no environmental damage or toxicity risk, unlike CFCs/HFCs. 3. **Suitable for aircraft** — atmospheric air is freely available as the working fluid at all flight altitudes. 4. **Expander recovers work** — a portion of the compression work is returned by the expander, reducing net power. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Very low COP** (0.2 to 0.5) compared to vapor-compression systems (2 to 4) because the cycle operates on sensible heat only. 2. **Large mass flow rate** of air is required to produce the same cooling capacity, leading to bulky ducts and high fan power. --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice 1 **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice A, June 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **COP of a Bell-Coleman air refrigeration cycle** in terms of the pressure ratio $r_p$ and ratio of specific heats $\gamma$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Bell-Coleman machine compresses air from $T_1 = 295$ K and $p_1 = 1$ bar to $p_2 = 4$ bar. The air is then cooled at constant pressure to $T_3 = 300$ K and expanded isentropically to $p_4 = 1$ bar. Determine: **(i)** the temperature at the expander inlet, **(ii)** the refrigerating effect per kg, **(iii)** the net work per kg, and **(iv)** the COP. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation of COP:** For an isentropic compression in the Bell-Coleman cycle: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ For the isentropic expansion (same pressure ratio): $$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ Hence, $T_2 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \cdot T_1$ and $T_3 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \cdot T_4$. Refrigerating Effect: $$RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4)$$ Net work: $$W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$$ Substituting $T_2 = kT_1$ and $T_3 = kT_4$ where $k = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$\begin{aligned} W_{net} &= c_p[kT_1 - T_1 - kT_4 + T_4] \\ &= c_p(k-1)(T_1 - T_4) \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$COP = \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p(k-1)(T_1 - T_4)} = \frac{1}{k-1}$$ $$\boxed{\;COP_{BC} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}\;}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** **Given:** $T_1 = 295$ K, $p_1 = 1$ bar, $p_2 = 4$ bar, $T_3 = 300$ K, $p_4 = 1$ bar. **Step 1 — Pressure ratio and isentropic exponent:** $$r_p = 4, \quad \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ $$k = 4^{0.2857} = 1.4860$$ **Step 2 — Compute $T_2$:** $$T_2 = k \cdot T_1 = 1.4860 \times 295 = 438.37 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Find $T_4$ from $T_3 = k T_4$:** $$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{k} = \frac{300}{1.4860} = 201.88 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $$RE = 1.005 \times (295 - 201.88) = 1.005 \times 93.12 = 93.59 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Net work per kg:** $$W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$$ $$= 1.005 \times [(438.37 - 295) - (300 - 201.88)]$$ $$= 1.005 \times [143.37 - 98.12]$$ $$= 1.005 \times 45.25 = 45.48 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Coefficient of Performance:** $$COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{93.59}{45.48} = 2.058$$ **Verification with closed-form formula:** $$COP = \frac{1}{1.4860 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.4860} = 2.058 \;\;\checkmark$$ The numerical and closed-form results match perfectly, confirming the cycle is internally consistent (note $T_3/T_4 = 300/201.88 = 1.486 = T_2/T_1$). --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice 2 **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice B, June 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat **P-V** and **T-S diagram**, explain the working of the **Reversed Carnot cycle with air** as the working fluid. Why is this cycle not realized in practice? **(7 marks)** **(b)** An air refrigeration cycle operates between a cold reservoir at $-10^{\circ}$C and a hot reservoir at $40^{\circ}$C. The capacity is 10 TR. Calculate the **mass flow rate of air**, **compressor work**, and **expander work** assuming a Bell-Coleman cycle with $r_p = 5$. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Reversed Carnot Cycle Explanation:** The Reversed Carnot cycle is a fully reversible thermodynamic cycle composed of four ideal processes: 1. **Process 1 → 2 (Isothermal Compression):** Air at $T_C$ is compressed in a *isothermal* manner, rejecting heat $Q_{rej}$ to the hot reservoir at $T_H$. Because isothermal compression of a gas requires simultaneous heat removal, the compressor must be in perfect thermal contact with the hot reservoir. 2. **Process 2 → 3 (Isentropic Compression):** Air is compressed further from $T_H$ to a higher temperature $T_2 > T_H$. No heat exchange occurs. 3. **Process 3 → 4 (Isothermal Expansion):** Air expands isothermally at $T_C$ (the lower of the two reservoirs in this reversed direction), absorbing heat $Q_{abs}$ from the cold space. 4. **Process 4 → 1 (Isentropic Expansion):** Air expands further back to state 1. **P-V Diagram (ASCII):** ``` P ↑ | 2 ─────── 3 | │\ │\ | │ \ │ \ | │ \ │ \ | │ \ │ \ | │ \ │ \ | 1───────4─────── | (cycle traced anticlockwise) +─────────────────────→ V ``` **T-S Diagram (ASCII):** ``` T ↑ | 2 │ 3 | │ │ | TH ──│───────│ | │ │ | │ │ | TC ──│───────│ | 1 │ 4 | └───────┘ +─────────────────────→ S ``` **Why it is not realized in practice:** 1. **Isothermal compression of a gas is physically impossible** in a finite-time machine — you cannot simultaneously compress the gas and remove its heat of compression at exactly the same rate. 2. The cycle requires two **separate expanders/heat exchangers** operating at $T_C$ and $T_H$ with infinite surface area, which is impractical. 3. Real machines cannot be fully reversible; friction, pressure drop, and finite-time heat exchange introduce irreversibilities that lower the COP. **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** **Given:** $T_C = -10 + 273 = 263$ K, $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313$ K, Capacity $= 10$ TR, $r_p = 5$. **Step 1 — Closed-form COP and pressure-ratio term:** $$k = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 5^{0.2857}$$ Computing: $\ln 5 = 1.6094$, so $0.2857 \times 1.6094 = 0.4598$, hence $k = e^{0.4598} = 1.5838$. **Step 2 — Relate Bell-Coleman to the given temperature limits:** For the Bell-Coleman cycle operating between $T_C$ and $T_H$ with cold-chamber inlet at $T_1$ and outlet at $T_4$: In an idealized tutorial setting, we map $T_1 = T_H = 313$ K and $T_4 = T_C = 263$ K (the bell-coleman in the cooler outlet maps to the cold reservoir). $$RE = c_p (T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (313 - 263) = 50.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = \frac{\text{Capacity (kW)}}{RE} = \frac{10 \times 3.517}{50.25} = \frac{35.17}{50.25} = 0.7000 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compressor work (per kg):** $$T_2 = k T_1 = 1.5838 \times 313 = 495.73 \text{ K}$$ $$W_c = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (495.73 - 313) = 1.005 \times 182.73 = 183.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Expander work (per kg):** $$T_3 = T_2 = 495.73 \text{ K (assume cooler brings air back to T_3) — but using } T_3 = k T_4 = 1.5838 \times 263 = 416.55 \text{ K}$$ $$W_e = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (416.55 - 263) = 1.005 \times 153.55 = 154.32 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Net work (per kg) and total compressor/expander power:** $$W_{net} = W_c - W_e = 183.65 - 154.32 = 29.33 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \times W_c = 0.7000 \times 183.65 = 128.56 \text{ kW}$$ $$\dot{W}_e = \dot{m} \times W_e = 0.7000 \times 154.32 = 108.02 \text{ kW}$$ $$\dot{W}_{net} = 0.7000 \times 29.33 = 20.53 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — COP verification:** $$COP = \frac{50.25}{29.33} = 1.713 \;\; \text{or} \;\; COP = \frac{1}{1.5838 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.5838} = 1.713 \;\;\checkmark$$ **Note:** This is a *Carnot limit check* — $COP_{Carnot} = T_C/(T_H - T_C) = 263/50 = 5.26$. As expected, the Bell-Coleman COP (1.71) is much less than the Carnot COP, confirming the second-law gap. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the expander work** — many students compute $W_{net} = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ only, forgetting to subtract $W_e$. The Bell-Coleman cycle *explicitly* includes the expander. > 2. **Using $c_v$ instead of $c_p$** in the work expressions — because the processes are *open-flow* (steady-flow), the enthalpy change governs, and $h = c_p T$ for an ideal gas. > 3. **Forgetting the unit of $T$** — temperatures must be in **K (Kelvin)**, not °C. A common slip costs 1 to 2 marks. > 4. **Mixing TR with kW** — capacity in TR must be converted using $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517$ kW before computing $\dot{m}$. > 5. **Sign errors in $T_2$ and $T_4$** — the temperature at the expander outlet is *lower* than $T_C$ in some textbook variants; read the problem carefully. > 6. **P-V / T-S diagram omission** — KTU explicitly awards 2–3 marks for the *neat labelled diagram*. Never skip it. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Air refrigeration** uses a *gaseous* working fluid; **no phase change** occurs in the cycle. - [x] The **Bell-Coleman cycle** is the practical realization of the Reversed Carnot cycle for air. - [x] The four processes are: **Isentropic compression → Isobaric heat rejection → Isentropic expansion → Isobaric heat absorption**. - [x] **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** $= c_p(T_1 - T_4)$ is the heat absorbed per kg in the cold chamber. - [x] **Net work** $W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$ — the expander *recovers* a portion of the compression work. - [x] **COP** $= RE / W_{net}$; in closed form $COP = 1 / (r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1)$. - [x] For air, **$\gamma = 1.4$** and **$c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)** must be used in *every* problem. - [x] The isentropic relation $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ links all four state temperatures. - [x] **1 TR = 3.517 kW** is the universal conversion for tonnage problems. - [x] The **P-V diagram** is enclosed in an *anticlockwise* loop; the **T-S diagram** is similarly anticlockwise, with the area under 4→1 being RE and the area under 2→3 being $Q_{rej}$. - [x] Bell-Coleman is preferred for **aircraft ECS** and **marine cold storage**; its COP (0.2–0.7) is *much lower* than vapor-compression (2–4). - [x] **Reversed Carnot** with air is a *theoretical benchmark* — never realized due to the impossibility of isothermal compression of a gas in finite time. - [x] **Throttling nozzle** in cheap realizations replaces the expander, reducing COP further (this is the *Joule refrigeration cycle* variant). - [x] Always draw the **P-V and T-S diagrams** in your answer sheet — KTU awards dedicated marks for them. - [x] The COP of a Bell-Coleman cycle is **always less than the COP of the corresponding Reversed Carnot cycle** operating between the same two temperature limits. - [x] **Lower pressure ratio** → higher COP, but smaller temperature lift. **Higher pressure ratio** → lower COP, larger lift. The trade-off is the heart of the problem.

topics.title: Study of Domestic Refrigerator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study of Domestic Refrigerator ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Domestic Refrigerator** is a sealed, self-contained, electrically-operated **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** designed to maintain temperatures in the range of **0 °C to 8 °C** inside an insulated cabinet for the preservation of food, beverages, and medicines in households. It operates on a single-loop, hermetically sealed cycle in which a low-boiling refrigerant (commonly **R-134a** or **R-600a (isobutane)**) circulates continuously through four primary hardware components: **Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Device (Capillary Tube) → Evaporator → (back to Compressor)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Snapshot:** A domestic refrigerator is a single-stage, single-evaporator, hermetically-sealed Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) using a **Capillary Tube** as the expansion device and a **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV)** only in dual-temperature models. The Coefficient of Performance (COP) typically lies between **1.5 and 2.5** for modern energy-efficient units. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you are standing in a cold room holding a bucket of warm water. The water *wants* to flow downhill (from hot to cold) — this is the natural direction. A refrigerator is essentially a **"bucket-carrying servant"** who continuously carries cold energy *uphill* — from the cold food compartment (inside) and dumps it into the hot kitchen (outside). The **compressor** is the strong servant, the **condenser coils** (on the back of the fridge) are the place where the heat is dumped, and the **capillary tube** is the narrow doorway that drops the pressure so the refrigerant becomes cold again to absorb more heat. Without this servant, heat would simply flood back in and spoil your food. The refrigerator is, therefore, an active heat-pump that works against nature's natural heat-flow direction. ### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Engineering Standards | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Units | |---|---|---| | Cabinet Temperature | 2 °C to **8 °C** | °C | | Evaporator Temperature | **−20 °C to −10 °C** | °C | | Condenser Temperature | **40 °C to 55 °C** | °C | | Refrigerant (Modern) | **R-134a, R-600a (Isobutane)** | — | | Refrigerant (Older) | R-12 (CFC), R-22 (HCFC) | — | | Power Supply | **230 V, 50 Hz, Single-phase AC** | V/Hz | | Typical COP | **1.5 to 2.5** | dimensionless | | Energy Star Rating | **1-Star to 5-Star (BEE India)** | Stars | | Standard Test Condition | **Ambient = 32 °C, No-load** | °C | > [!NOTE] > **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) India** tests all domestic refrigerators at an ambient temperature of **32 °C** under no-load conditions for 24 hours to assign star ratings. Higher ISEER value = lower electricity consumption. ### 1.4 Purpose of the Laboratory Study The experiment "Study of Domestic Refrigerator" trains the student to: 1. **Identify and label** all internal and external components. 2. **Trace the refrigerant flow path** through the sealed system. 3. **Measure operating parameters** (pressures, temperatures, current, voltage). 4. **Calculate the Coefficient of Performance (COP)** and compare with theoretical values. 5. **Diagnose common faults** (gas leakage, compressor failure, thermostat malfunction, capillary choke, defrost timer issues). ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four-Stage Vapour Compression Cycle (Domestic Refrigerator) The refrigerator operates on a **closed Rankine cycle in reverse**. Each component performs a strictly defined thermodynamic process: #### Stage 1 — Compression (Compressor) - **What happens:** Low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant **vapour** is sucked in from the evaporator and compressed to a high-pressure, high-temperature **superheated vapour**. - **Process:** **Isentropic Compression (theoretically)**; practically **polytropic** with isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen} = 0.65$ to $0.85$. - **Why:** Pressure must be raised above condenser ambient pressure so the refrigerant can reject heat to the room air. #### Stage 2 — Condensation (Condenser) - **What happens:** High-pressure vapour enters the condenser coils (usually at the back of the fridge). It first **desuperheats**, then **condenses** to a saturated liquid, and finally **subcools** slightly below saturation temperature. - **Process:** **Isobaric Heat Rejection** at constant pressure $P_2$. - **Why:** The refrigerant must release the latent heat absorbed at the evaporator plus the work of compression to the surroundings. #### Stage 3 — Expansion (Capillary Tube) - **What happens:** High-pressure subcooled liquid passes through a narrow copper tube of inner diameter **0.5 mm to 2 mm** and length **1 m to 5 m**. Pressure and temperature **drop suddenly**. - **Process:** **Isenthalpic (Throttling) Process** — $h_3 = h_4$. - **Why:** A capillary tube is preferred over an expansion valve in domestic fridges because it is **cheap, has no moving parts, silent, and reliable**. A small strainer/filter-drier precedes it to prevent choking. #### Stage 4 — Evaporation (Evaporator) - **What happens:** Low-pressure, low-temperature **two-phase mixture** enters the evaporator coils (usually fitted as a plate around the freezer compartment). The liquid portion **boils off** by absorbing latent heat from the cabinet interior. - **Process:** **Isobaric Heat Absorption** at constant pressure $P_1$. - **Why:** The refrigerant, being cold, extracts heat from the food compartment — providing the refrigeration effect. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (VCRS for Domestic Refrigerator) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | $\vert$ refers to absolute enthalpy difference $\vert$ | | 2 | Compressor Work (W) | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant | | 3 | Heat Rejected (Q_cond) | $Q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | $Q_c = RE + W_c$ (Energy balance) | | 4 | Theoretical COP | $COP_{th} = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | — | **Most important for KTU** | | 5 | Actual COP | $COP_{act} = \dfrac{RE \times m_r}{W_{electrical}}$ | — | $m_r$ = mass flow rate (kg/s) | | 6 | Mass Flow Rate | $m_r = \dfrac{Q_{ref}}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | $Q_{ref}$ in kW | | 7 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $V_{disp} = \dfrac{m_r \times v_1}{\eta_{vol}}$ | m³/s | $\eta_{vol} = 0.6$ to $0.85$ | | 8 | Power Input | $P_{in} = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | W | Single-phase: $\cos\phi \approx 0.7$ | | 9 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_{cond}}{P_{evap}}$ | — | Typically 4 to 6 | | 10 | Carnot COP (Ideal Limit) | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | — | $T$ in Kelvin | | 11 | EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio) | $EER = \dfrac{Q_{ref}\ (BTU/hr)}{P_{in}\ (W)}$ | — | Used in star rating | | 12 | ISEER (Indian Seasonal EER) | $ISEER = \dfrac{\sum Q_{ref,i}}{\sum P_{in,i}}$ | — | BEE India test standard | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Always use **absolute enthalpies** (read from P–h chart or refrigerant tables) for $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you write $h_1 = 100$ (with a unit), forgetting that the actual numerical values are in kJ/kg (e.g., $h_1 = 392$ kJ/kg for R-134a). --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The domestic refrigerator is the **most widely deployed thermodynamic machine on Earth** — over **1.5 billion units** are in use globally. The same VCRS cycle scales up to: - **Cold storage warehouses** (industrial refrigeration) - **Pharmaceutical vaccine cold chains** (WHO EPI programme) - **Supermarket display freezers** - **Ice plants and packaged drinking water chillers** - **Cryogenic medical freezers** (−86 °C for COVID-19 mRNA vaccines) Engineers optimizing these systems directly combat **food wastage** (≈40 % of food in India is lost post-harvest) and reduce **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** through proper refrigerant selection (R-600a has GWP ≈ 3 vs. R-134a GWP ≈ 1430). ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Coefficient of Performance (COP) The **COP** is defined as the ratio of the *desired output* (refrigeration effect) to the *required input* (compressor work). It is a **dimensionless** performance index — higher COP means a more efficient refrigerator. **Step 1:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the cyclic, steady-flow system. Since the refrigerant returns to its initial state after one complete cycle, the net change in energy is zero. $$ \sum Q_{in,out} = \sum W_{in,out} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the heat interactions. The evaporator absorbs heat $Q_L = RE$ from the cold cabinet. The condenser rejects heat $Q_H$ to the kitchen air. The compressor consumes work $W_{net}$. $$ Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} $$ **Step 3:** Recognize that the heat absorbed at the evaporator equals the **refrigerating effect per unit mass** of refrigerant flowing. $$ Q_L = m_r \cdot (h_1 - h_4) \quad \text{[kW]} $$ **Step 4:** Recognize that the compressor work input equals the **enthalpy rise across the compressor** per unit mass. $$ W_{net} = m_r \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{[kW]} $$ **Step 5:** Take the ratio of desired output to required input — by definition, this is the COP. $$ COP = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{m_r \cdot (h_1 - h_4)}{m_r \cdot (h_2 - h_1)} $$ **Step 6:** Cancel the mass flow rate $m_r$ (it appears identically in numerator and denominator). $$ \boxed{\,COP_{th} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}\,} $$ This is the **canonical VCRS formula** that every KTU examiner expects. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant To size the compressor and predict the cooling capacity, the mass flow rate must be known. **Step 1:** The total refrigeration load $Q_{ref}$ (in kW) is the rate at which heat must be removed from the cabinet to maintain temperature. $$ Q_{ref} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{mean} + m_{food} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{door} $$ This includes heat conduction through walls, heat from warm food being loaded, and door-opening losses. **Step 2:** Equate this cooling load to the refrigerating effect per unit mass. $$ Q_{ref} = m_r \cdot (h_1 - h_4) $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for $m_r$. $$ \boxed{\,m_r = \frac{Q_{ref}}{(h_1 - h_4)} \quad \text{[kg/s]}\,} $$ **Step 4:** The compressor's theoretical swept volume (displacement) needed is then: $$ V_{disp} = \frac{m_r \cdot v_1}{\eta_{vol}} $$ where $v_1$ is the specific volume of saturated vapour at evaporator pressure $P_1$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Style) > **Problem (KTU Model):** A domestic refrigerator using **R-134a** operates between evaporator temperature $T_{evap} = -10\ °C$ and condenser temperature $T_{cond} = 40\ °C$. The refrigerant is **subcooled by 5 °C** at the condenser exit, and the vapour is **superheated by 5 °C** at the compressor inlet. The compressor has an isentropic efficiency of **80 %**. Determine: (a) the theoretical COP, (b) the actual COP, and (c) the refrigerating effect per kg. Assume no pressure drop in heat exchangers. **Reading the R-134a P–h chart at the given saturation points:** $$ h_1^{sat} @ -10\ °C \approx 392.3\ kJ/kg $$ $$ h_3^{sat} @ 40\ °C \approx 256.4\ kJ/kg $$ **(a) Enthalpy at all four state points:** State 1: Saturated vapour superheated by 5 °C → $T_1 = -5\ °C$ $$ h_1 = 392.3 + (1.7 \cdot 5) = 400.8 \approx 401\ kJ/kg $$ State 2s: Isentropic compression to $P_2 = 1017\ kPa$ (sat. pressure at 40 °C). From R-134a superheated table at $P_2 = 1.0\ MPa$ and $s_1 = s_{g} @ -10\ °C \approx 1.733\ kJ/kg\cdot K$, we get $T_{2s} \approx 49.7\ °C$. $$ h_{2s} \approx 430.0\ kJ/kg $$ State 2: Actual enthalpy with 80 % isentropic efficiency $$ h_2 = h_1 + \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen}} = 401 + \frac{430.0 - 401}{0.80} $$ $$ h_2 = 401 + 36.25 = 437.25\ kJ/kg $$ State 3: Saturated liquid subcooled by 5 °C → $T_3 = 35\ °C$ $$ h_3 = h_{f} @ 40\ °C - c_{p,liq} \cdot \Delta T = 256.4 - (1.55 \cdot 5) $$ $$ h_3 = 256.4 - 7.75 = 248.65\ kJ/kg $$ State 4: Throttling $h_4 = h_3$ $$ h_4 = 248.65\ kJ/kg $$ **(b) Refrigerating Effect:** $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = 401 - 248.65 = 152.35\ kJ/kg $$ **(c) Theoretical COP:** $$ COP_{th} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_{2s} - h_1} = \frac{152.35}{430.0 - 401} = \frac{152.35}{29.0} \approx 5.25 $$ **(d) Actual COP:** $$ COP_{act} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{152.35}{437.25 - 401} = \frac{152.35}{36.25} \approx 4.20 $$ **Analytical Result:** The actual COP ($4.20$) is lower than the theoretical COP ($5.25$) by a factor of $\eta_{isen} = 0.80$, confirming energy loss due to compressor irreversibilities. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Marking Scheme Note:** The board examiner grants **full 7 marks** for Part (a) and **full 7 marks** for Part (b) **only** if you (i) clearly state the assumptions, (ii) read the chart values correctly, and (iii) write the final boxed answer with units. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Domestic Refrigerator (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR A[Compressor
Hermetically Sealed
Reciprocating Type] --> B[Condenser
Natural or Forced
Convection Coils] B --> C[Filter Drier
Removes Moisture
and Debris] C --> D[Capillary Tube
Expansion Device
0.5 to 2 mm ID] D --> E[Evaporator
Plate or Fin Type
Located in Freezer] E --> F[Thermostat
Senses Cabinet Temp
Starts/Stops Compressor] F --> A G[Defrost Heater
and Timer] -.-> E H[Capillary Bulb
Attached to Evaporator] -.-> F ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **solid arrows** form the **refrigerant flow path** (closed loop). - The **dashed arrows** represent the **control signal** paths (thermostat sensing and defrost triggering). - The compressor is the only electrical input, drawing current via a **Relay and Overload Protector (OLP)**. --- ### 4.2 Detailed Component Interaction Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PowerPath[Power Supply and Control] PS1[230V 50Hz AC Mains] --> REL[Start Relay] REL --> OLP[Overload Protector] OLP --> CMP[Compressor Motor] PS1 --> THR[Thermostat Switch] THR -.Controls.-> REL end subgraph RefrigLoop[Refrigerant Loop] CMP --> DS[Hot Superheated Vapour] DS --> CND[Condenser Coils] CND --> SCL[Subcooled Liquid] SCL --> FD[Filter Drier] FD --> CPT[Capillary Tube] CPT --> EVP[Evaporator] EVP --> LTV[Low Temp Vapour] LTV --> CMP end subgraph CabCab[Cabinet Subsystem] EVP --> FRZ[Freezer Compartment] EVP --> REF[Refrigerator Compartment] FRZ --> CRB[Cabinet Body Insulation] REF --> CRB end subgraph Defrost[Defrost Subsystem] DFT[Defrost Timer] --> DH[Defrost Heater] DH --> EVP end ``` --- ### 4.3 P–h Diagram State Points Reference ```mermaid graph LR P1["State 1: Superheated Vapour
at Evaporator Outlet
Low P, Low T"] --> P2["State 2: Superheated Vapour
after Compressor
High P, High T"] P2 --> P3["State 3: Subcooled Liquid
after Condenser
High P, Medium T"] P3 --> P4["State 4: Two-Phase Mixture
after Capillary
Low P, Low T"] P4 --> P1 ``` **Process Labels:** - 1 → 2 : **Isentropic compression** (vertical line on P–h chart) - 2 → 3 : **Isobaric condensation + subcooling** (horizontal line moving left) - 3 → 4 : **Isenthalpic throttling** (vertical line moving down) - 4 → 1 : **Isobaric evaporation + superheating** (horizontal line moving right) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visual Memory Tip:** Always remember — on the P–h chart, **compression goes UP**, **condensation goes LEFT**, **expansion goes DOWN**, and **evaporation goes RIGHT**. This rotational pattern (counterclockwise on P–h) is the visual signature of a refrigeration cycle. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **List the four main components of a domestic refrigerator and state the function of the capillary tube.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four main components of a domestic refrigerator are: 1. **Compressor** — compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour to high pressure (1 mark) 2. **Condenser** — rejects heat from refrigerant to ambient air, condensing it to liquid (0.5 mark) 3. **Capillary Tube** — expansion device that drops pressure of liquid refrigerant (0.5 mark) 4. **Evaporator** — absorbs heat from the cabinet, evaporating the refrigerant (0.5 mark) **Function of Capillary Tube:** It is a long, narrow copper tube (typically 0.5–2 mm inner diameter, 1–5 m length) that acts as a **throttling device**. It creates a **pressure drop** between the high-pressure condenser side and the low-pressure evaporator side, causing the refrigerant to flash into a low-temperature two-phase mixture. The throttling process is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$). (1 mark for function explanation) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Why is a hermetically sealed compressor used in domestic refrigerators? Mention two refrigerants commonly used in modern units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **hermetically sealed compressor** is used because: - The entire motor-compressor unit is enclosed in a **welded steel shell**, making the system **leak-proof** (1 mark) - It is **vibration-free, noiseless, and requires no external shaft seal**, which is the most common leak point in open-type compressors (1 mark) - This is essential for domestic use where refrigerant leakage would be hazardous and would degrade performance. **Two modern refrigerants:** **R-134a (HFC)** and **R-600a (Isobutane, HC)** — both are **ozone-depletion potential (ODP) = 0**, but R-600a is preferred in modern eco-friendly refrigerators due to its very low **Global Warming Potential (GWP ≈ 3)**. (1 mark) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **Q.A.(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the working of a domestic refrigerator based on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)**. Label all four components and indicate the state of the refrigerant at each location. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** A domestic refrigerator operates on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**. The four main components and the state of refrigerant at each point are: | Location | Component | Refrigerant State | |---|---|---| | 1 (Compressor Inlet) | Evaporator Outlet → Compressor | **Low-pressure, low-temperature superheated vapour** | | 2 (Compressor Outlet) | Compressor → Condenser | **High-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour** | | 3 (Condenser Outlet) | Condenser → Capillary | **High-pressure subcooled liquid** | | 4 (Capillary Outlet) | Capillary → Evaporator | **Low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture** | **Working Cycle (4 marks):** - **Compression (1→2):** The compressor raises the pressure of the refrigerant vapour (typically from 0.2 MPa to 0.8–1.0 MPa). The temperature rises from about −10 °C to 50–70 °C. Theoretically, the process is **isentropic**. - **Condensation (2→3):** The hot high-pressure vapour enters the condenser coils mounted on the back of the refrigerator. The surrounding air (at ≈ 32 °C) cools the refrigerant, which first desuperheats, then condenses to a saturated liquid, and finally subcools by 3–5 °C. Heat rejected $Q_c = h_2 - h_3$. - **Expansion (3→4):** The subcooled liquid passes through the **capillary tube** — a long, narrow copper tube — where it undergoes a **throttling (isenthalpic) process**. Pressure and temperature both drop sharply. The refrigerant emerges as a low-pressure two-phase mixture, typically at −20 to −10 °C. - **Evaporation (4→1):** This cold mixture flows through the evaporator coils inside the freezer compartment. The liquid portion absorbs latent heat from the cabinet (food, air), evaporates completely, and the vapour slightly superheats before being sucked back by the compressor. Refrigerating Effect $RE = h_1 - h_4$. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The cycle is **closed and continuous**. Heat is continuously pumped from the cold cabinet to the warm kitchen, keeping the food preserved. --- **Q.A.(b)** A domestic refrigerator uses **R-134a** as the refrigerant. The evaporating and condensing temperatures are **−10 °C and 40 °C** respectively. The refrigerant is **subcooled by 4 °C** at the condenser exit, and the **vapour is superheated by 5 °C** at the compressor inlet. Assuming **no pressure drop** in the heat exchangers and an **isentropic compression**, calculate: **(i)** the refrigerating effect per kg, **(ii)** the compressor work per kg, and **(iii)** the theoretical COP. Use the following R-134a data: $h_1 = 401\ kJ/kg$, $h_2 = 430\ kJ/kg$, $h_3 = 256.4\ kJ/kg$, $h_4 = 248.6\ kJ/kg$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given Data:** - $T_{evap} = -10\ °C$, $T_{cond} = 40\ °C$ - Subcooling = 4 °C, Superheat = 5 °C - $h_1 = 401\ kJ/kg$, $h_2 = 430\ kJ/kg$, $h_3 = 256.4\ kJ/kg$, $h_4 = 248.6\ kJ/kg$ **Step 1: Refrigerating Effect (2 marks)** $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = 401 - 248.6 = 152.4\ kJ/kg $$ **Step 2: Compressor Work (2 marks)** $$ W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 430 - 401 = 29\ kJ/kg $$ **Step 3: Heat Rejected at Condenser (1 mark)** $$ Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3 = 430 - 256.4 = 173.6\ kJ/kg $$ **Verification using energy balance:** $$ Q_{cond} = RE + W_c = 152.4 + 29 = 181.4\ kJ/kg $$ *(Note: Small discrepancy with 173.6 is due to subcooling heat not appearing in RE — the values are chart-read approximations; the energy balance holds at 181.4 kJ/kg for a steady-state cycle).* **Step 4: Theoretical COP (2 marks)** $$ COP_{th} = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{152.4}{29} \approx 5.26 $$ **Final Answer (boxed for examiner):** $$ \boxed{\,RE = 152.4\ kJ/kg, \quad W_c = 29\ kJ/kg, \quad COP_{th} = 5.26\,} $$ --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B.(a)** Explain the construction and working of the **thermostat** and **defrost timer** in a domestic refrigerator. Why is **R-600a (isobutane)** preferred over **R-134a** in modern eco-friendly refrigerators? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Thermostat (3 marks):** The **capillary-tube thermostat** is the brain of the refrigerator. It consists of: - A **capillary bulb** filled with refrigerant, clipped to the evaporator surface - A **diaphragm** connected to a microswitch - An **adjustment knob** (temperature setting 1 to 5 or 1 to 9) **Working:** As the cabinet temperature rises, the refrigerant in the bulb evaporates, the pressure rises, the diaphragm deflects and **closes the switch**, energizing the compressor. As the cabinet cools to the set temperature, the bulb pressure drops, the diaphragm returns, the switch **opens**, and the compressor stops. This ON/OFF cycling maintains the set temperature. **Defrost Timer (2 marks):** Modern frost-free refrigerators have a **defrost heater and timer circuit** that operates every **6, 8, or 12 hours** (cumulative compressor run time). The timer energizes a heater strip (50–200 W) mounted near the evaporator, melting accumulated frost. The melt-water flows to a **drain pan** below the cabinet. After the defrost cycle (15–30 minutes), a **defrost thermostat** (or bi-metal switch) ends the cycle and restarts the compressor. **R-600a vs. R-134a (2 marks):** | Property | R-134a | R-600a (Isobutane) | |---|---|---| | ODP (Ozone Depletion) | 0 | 0 | | GWP (Global Warming) | **1430** | **≈ 3** | | Energy Efficiency | Good | **5–10 % better** | | Flammability | Non-flammable | **Mildly flammable (A3)** | | Charge Required | 150–250 g | **40–80 g** (smaller charge) | R-600a is preferred because of its **ultra-low GWP** and higher energy efficiency, making it compliant with the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol. The small charge size minimizes flammability risk. --- **Q.B.(b)** A 200-litre domestic refrigerator consumes **0.6 kWh of electricity per day**. The compressor runs for an average of **10 hours per day**, drawing **80 W** when running. The refrigerating effect is measured as **150 kJ/kg**, and the refrigerant mass flow rate is **1.5 × 10⁻³ kg/s**. Calculate: **(i)** the **actual COP** of the refrigerator, and **(ii)** the **refrigeration load** in kW. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - Daily energy consumption = 0.6 kWh/day - Compressor power input = 80 W - Compressor running time = 10 h/day - Refrigerating Effect (RE) = 150 kJ/kg - Mass flow rate $m_r = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ kg/s$ **Step 1: Verify Daily Energy (1 mark)** $$ E_{daily} = P_{in} \times t = 0.080\ kW \times 10\ h = 0.8\ kWh/day $$ *(Note: The 0.6 kWh figure likely accounts for thermostat off-time losses; we will use the measured 0.6 kWh for load calculation but 80 W for input to compressor.)* **Step 2: Refrigeration Load (3 marks)** The refrigeration load is the average rate of heat removed from the cabinet: $$ Q_{ref} = m_r \times RE = (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 150 $$ $$ Q_{ref} = 0.225\ kW = 225\ W $$ **Step 3: Actual COP (3 marks)** $$ COP_{act} = \frac{Q_{ref}}{P_{in}} = \frac{225\ W}{80\ W} $$ $$ \boxed{\,COP_{act} = 2.81\,} $$ **Conclusion:** The refrigerator has a COP of about **2.81**, which is reasonable for a domestic unit (typical range 1.5–3.0). The refrigeration load of **225 W** corresponds to a daily removal of: $$ Q_{daily} = 0.225 \times 24 = 5.4\ kWh/day $$ This is more than the electricity input, confirming the refrigerator is essentially **pumping heat** rather than creating it. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Writing $COP = 152.4 / 29$ without showing the units cancellation in the formula. Always write the final answer with **COP = 5.26 (dimensionless)**. > 2. **Mixing up states:** Confusing $h_3$ and $h_4$. Remember — **throttling means $h_3 = h_4$** by the First Law; you must read $h_3$ from the chart and reuse it as $h_4$. > 3. **Ignoring subcooling/superheat:** Students often take saturated values from the P–h chart but forget the corrections $\Delta h = c_p \cdot \Delta T$. This can lead to 1–2 mark deductions. > 4. **No block diagram in the answer:** A "Study of Domestic Refrigerator" question without a labelled block diagram is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**. > 5. **Forgetting the safety devices:** When asked about components, students often miss the **Overload Protector (OLP), Start Relay, and Filter-Drier** — these are essential hardware and should always be mentioned. > 6. **Reversing Carnot COP formula:** Some students write $COP = (T_H - T_L)/T_L$ instead of $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$. The correct numerator is the **cold reservoir temperature in Kelvin**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Cycle Type:** Domestic refrigerator = **Single-stage Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)**, hermetically sealed. - **Four Components (mnemonic: "CCEE"):** **C**ompressor → **C**ondenser → Capillary (Expansion) → **E**vaporator. - **Expansion Device:** **Capillary Tube** (cheaper, silent, no moving parts) — preferred over TEV for domestic use. - **Refrigerant (Modern):** **R-600a (Isobutane)** — ODP = 0, GWP ≈ 3, energy-efficient. - **Refrigerant (Older):** R-12 (CFC, banned) → R-22 (HCFC, phase-out) → R-134a (HFC, transition) → R-600a (current). - **Theoretical COP Formula:** $COP_{th} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \dfrac{RE}{W_c}$ - **Energy Balance:** $Q_{cond} = RE + W_c$ - **Temperature Ranges:** Evaporator = **−20 to −10 °C**, Condenser = **40 to 55 °C**, Cabinet = **2 to 8 °C**. - **Pressure Ratio:** Typically **4 to 6** for R-134a domestic units. - **Thermostat:** Capillary-bulb type; senses evaporator temperature; cycles compressor ON/OFF. - **Defrost System:** Heater + timer + bi-metal thermostat; operates every 6–12 hours in frost-free models. - **Safety Devices:** Overload Protector (OLP), Start Relay (PTC or current-type), Filter-Drier, High/Low pressure switches. - **Test Standard:** **BEE India** tests at **32 °C ambient, no-load, 24-hour cycle** for star rating. - **P–h Chart Direction:** Counter-clockwise cycle (compression UP, condensation LEFT, expansion DOWN, evaporation RIGHT). - **Insulation:** CFC-free **PU foam (Cyclopentane blown)** of thickness 40–60 mm — reduces wall heat gain. - **Expected COP Range:** Theoretical: **4 to 6**; Actual domestic: **1.5 to 3.0** (lower due to compressor inefficiency, heat leak, subcooling losses). - **Lab Measurement Points:** Suction pressure ($P_1$), discharge pressure ($P_2$), suction temperature ($T_1$), discharge temperature ($T_2$), current ($I$), voltage ($V$), and cabinet temperature. - **Safety Note:** R-600a is **mildly flammable**; refrigerator service workshops must have refrigerant recovery units and leak detectors rated for HC refrigerants.

topics.title: Studies on Heat Exchangers and Their Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Studies on Heat Exchangers and Their Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Exchanger (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *heat exchanger* is a device that transfers thermal energy between two or more fluids (separated by a solid wall) that are at different temperatures, without allowing them to mix. In refrigeration and air-conditioning systems, heat exchangers function as **condensers, evaporators, liquid-suction heat exchangers, and economizers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Study (KTU Lab Focus):** In the RAC Laboratory, the experiment on "Studies on Heat Exchangers and Their Performance" involves determining the **Heat Transfer Rate (Q)**, **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**, **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**, and **Effectiveness (ε)** of a given heat exchanger test rig under steady-state conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two trains running on parallel tracks — one carrying hot coffee mugs (Hot Fluid) and the other carrying cold milk bottles (Cold Fluid). If the trains pass through a tunnel with a thin glass wall between them, the heat will gradually pass from the coffee mugs to the milk bottles without the two ever mixing. The **glass wall is the heat transfer surface**, the **tunnel is the heat exchanger**, and the **amount of milk that warms up per second is the Heat Transfer Rate (Q)**.

A simpler everyday example is the **car radiator** — hot coolant from the engine runs through tubes while atmospheric air is blown across them by a fan. The coolant loses heat to the air; this is exactly how an evaporative-type heat exchanger behaves in a refrigeration plant.

### 1.3 Classification of Heat Exchangers

- **Based on Flow Arrangement:**
  - *Parallel Flow (Co-current)*
  - *Counter Flow (Counter-current)*
  - *Cross Flow*
  - *Shell and Tube (Combined Cross and Counter)*
- **Based on Construction:**
  - *Concentric (Double) Tube*
  - *Shell and Tube*
  - *Plate Type*
  - *Finned Tube*
- **Based on Heat Transfer Mechanism:**
  - *Direct Contact Type*
  - *Recuperative (Indirect)*
  - *Regenerative (Storage Type)*

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four standard metrics always computed in the KTU lab exam are: **(i) Heat Transfer Rate Q (kW)**, **(ii) LMTD (°C)**, **(iii) Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient U (W/m²·K)**, and **(iv) Effectiveness ε (dimensionless)**. Their **SI units must be remembered** as they fetch easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The performance analysis of a heat exchanger follows a rigorous logical sequence:

- **Step 1 — Steady-State Energy Balance:** At steady state, the energy lost by the hot fluid equals the energy gained by the cold fluid (assuming zero heat loss to surroundings).
- **Step 2 — Computation of Heat Transfer Rate (Q):** Q is calculated independently from both the hot-stream and cold-stream energy balances. The two values should match within an experimental error of ±5%.
- **Step 3 — LMTD Calculation:** The temperature profile is non-linear due to the changing temperature difference along the length. Therefore, the **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is used instead of the arithmetic mean.
- **Step 4 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):** U combines the convective resistances of the hot fluid, cold fluid, and the conductive resistance of the wall.
- **Step 5 — Effectiveness (ε):** It compares the actual heat transferred to the maximum possible heat transfer.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Is Important

- The **LMTD method** works only when the specific heats of fluids remain constant — a condition closely approximated in most lab experiments using water as the working fluid.
- The **Effectiveness-NTU (ε-NTU)** method is preferred when outlet temperatures are unknown or when the heat exchanger is part of a larger network.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Heat Transfer Rate (Hot) | $Q_h = \dot{m}_h \, c_{ph} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | kW | Energy lost by hot fluid |
| 2 | Heat Transfer Rate (Cold) | $Q_c = \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | kW | Energy gained by cold fluid |
| 3 | Heat Transfer Rate (Avg.) | $Q = \dfrac{Q_h + Q_c}{2}$ | kW | KTU valuation prefers average |
| 4 | LMTD Counter Flow | $\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | °C | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ |
| 5 | LMTD Parallel Flow | $\Delta T_{lm,PF} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | °C | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ |
| 6 | LMTD Correction Factor | $\Delta T_{lm} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | °C | $F < 1$ for shell & tube |
| 7 | Overall Heat Transfer Coeff. | $U = \dfrac{Q}{A_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | W/m²·K | $A_s$ is heat transfer area |
| 8 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | dimensionless | For $C_{min}$ side |
| 9 | NTU | $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A_s}{C_{min}}$ | dimensionless | $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ |
| 10 | Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ | kW/K | For each fluid stream |
| 11 | Capacity Ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le C_r \le 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (constant temperature difference), LMTD reduces to the arithmetic mean. This happens in **condensers** (hot fluid at constant $T_{sat}$).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

In real RAC systems, **evaporators** behave as counter-flow heat exchangers (refrigerant inside tubes, air/chilled water outside), and **condensers** operate as cross-flow or shell-and-tube units. The performance study conducted in this lab directly maps to the design verification of an actual chiller or HVAC plant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a differential element of a heat exchanger of length $dx$ and surface area $dA_s$. Let the hot fluid temperature be $T_h$ and cold fluid temperature be $T_c$.

**Step 1 — Write the differential heat transfer:**
The rate of heat transfer through the differential area is proportional to the local temperature difference:

$$dQ = U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dA_s$$

**Step 2 — Express the energy change for each stream:**

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \, c_{ph} \, dT_h = \dot{m}_c \, c_{pc} \, dT_c$$

This means the temperature change of the hot stream is negative (cooling) and the cold stream is positive (heating).

**Step 3 — Write the differential of the temperature difference:**

For a **counter-flow** exchanger:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

From Step 2:

$$dT_h = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h \, c_{ph}}, \qquad dT_c = \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c \, c_{pc}}$$

Therefore:

$$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left[ \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h \, c_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c \, c_{pc}} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $dQ$ from Step 1:**

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dA_s \left[ \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} \right]$$

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot \Delta T \cdot dA_s \cdot \left( \dfrac{C_h + C_c}{C_h \cdot C_c} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate from inlet to outlet:**

$$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \dfrac{C_h + C_c}{C_h \cdot C_c} \right) dA_s$$

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\dfrac{U \cdot A_s \cdot (C_h + C_c)}{C_h \cdot C_c}$$

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -\dfrac{U \cdot A_s \cdot (C_h + C_c)}{C_h \cdot C_c}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $Q$ using overall energy balance:**
Total heat transfer is $Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$.

For counter-flow:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}, \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

After algebraic rearrangement of the logarithmic relation:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$

**Step 7 — Final heat transfer equation:**

$$Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** When $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$, use the approximation $\Delta T_{lm} \approx (\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$ to avoid numerical division by zero. KTU examiners often give such data to test numerical stability awareness.

### 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for a Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

**Step 1 — Define effectiveness:**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}}$$

where $Q_{max}$ is the heat transfer that would occur if the fluid with minimum heat capacity rate were brought to the inlet temperature of the other fluid.

$$Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

**Step 2 — Express actual Q in terms of minimum side:**

For $C_{min} = C_h$:

$$Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}{C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})} = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$

**Step 4 — Using the $\varepsilon$-NTU relation for counter-flow:**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU (1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-NTU (1 - C_r)]}$$

**Step 5 — For parallel flow:**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU (1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sample Computation Flow

| Step | Compute | Given Inputs | Calculation Reference |
|------|---------|--------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_h$ | $\rho_h$, $V_h$, $d$ | $\dot{m}_h = \rho_h \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot V_h$ |
| 2 | $Q_h$ | $\dot{m}_h$, $c_{ph}$, $T_{h,in}$, $T_{h,out}$ | $Q_h = \dot{m}_h c_{ph}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ |
| 3 | $Q_c$ | $\dot{m}_c$, $c_{pc}$, $T_{c,in}$, $T_{c,out}$ | $Q_c = \dot{m}_c c_{pc}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ |
| 4 | $Q_{avg}$ | $Q_h$, $Q_c$ | $Q_{avg} = (Q_h + Q_c)/2$ |
| 5 | LMTD | $T_{h,in}$, $T_{h,out}$, $T_{c,in}$, $T_{c,out}$ | $\Delta T_{lm}$ as per flow type |
| 6 | $U$ | $Q_{avg}$, $A_s$, LMTD | $U = Q / (A_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$ |
| 7 | $\varepsilon$ | $T_{h,in}$, $T_{h,out}$, $T_{c,in}$ | $\varepsilon = (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})/(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ |
| 8 | NTU | $U$, $A_s$, $C_{min}$ | $NTU = U A_s / C_{min}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Heat Exchanger Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH1[Hot Fluid Tank] --> nodeH2[Hot Fluid Pump]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3[Rotameter Hot]
    nodeH3 --> nodeH4[Heater Element]
    nodeH4 --> nodeH5[Inlet T1 Hot]
    nodeH5 --> nodeHX1[HEAT EXCHANGER CORE]
    nodeHX1 --> nodeH6[Outlet T2 Hot]
    nodeH6 --> nodeH7[Hot Fluid Return Tank]

    nodeC1[Cold Fluid Tank] --> nodeC2[Cold Fluid Pump]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Rotameter Cold]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC5[Inlet T3 Cold]
    nodeC5 --> nodeHX1
    nodeHX1 --> nodeC6[Outlet T4 Cold]
    nodeC6 --> nodeC7[Cold Fluid Drain]

    nodeHX1 --> nodeD1[Data Acquisition Unit]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2[Display and Logger]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Identification:** The two rotameters, the heater at the inlet of the hot stream, four thermocouples (T1, T2, T3, T4), and the central heat exchanger block form the standard test rig layout.

### 4.2 Counter-Flow vs Parallel-Flow Temperature Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CF[COUNTER FLOW CONFIGURATION]
        direction LR
        cfi1[Hot Inlet T_h_in Right] --> cfh1[Hot Stream Decreases]
        cfh1 --> cfo1[Hot Outlet T_h_out Left]
        cfc1[Cold Stream Increases] --> cfc2
        cfo1 --- cfc1
        cfi1 --- cfc2
    end

    subgraph PF[PARALLEL FLOW CONFIGURATION]
        direction LR
        pfi1[Hot Inlet T_h_in Left] --> pfh1[Hot Stream Decreases]
        pfh1 --> pfo1[Hot Outlet T_h_out Right]
        pfc1[Cold Stream Increases] --> pfc2
        pfi1 --- pfc1
        pfo1 --- pfc2
    end
```

### 4.3 Control Logic Flow for Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Experiment] --> step1[Switch ON Pumps and Heater]
    step1 --> step2[Set Hot Fluid Flow Rate]
    step2 --> step3[Set Cold Fluid Flow Rate]
    step3 --> step4[Wait for Steady State 10 to 15 minutes]
    step4 --> step5[Record T1 T2 T3 T4 and Flow Rates]
    step5 --> step6[Calculate Q_h and Q_c]
    step6 --> step7{Check Q_h approx Q_c?}
    step7 -- Yes --> step8[Compute LMTD]
    step7 -- No --> step9[Adjust Flow and Repeat]
    step8 --> step10[Compute U and NTU]
    step10 --> step11[Compute Effectiveness epsilon]
    step11 --> endA[Tabulate Results and Plot]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term *Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)*. Why is it preferred over the arithmetic mean temperature difference?

**Model Answer:**
The Log Mean Temperature Difference is defined as the temperature difference that, if constant along the length of the heat exchanger, would give the same heat transfer rate as the actual varying temperature difference. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences at the two ends of the exchanger.

It is preferred over the arithmetic mean because, in actual heat exchangers, the temperature difference between the hot and cold streams varies non-linearly along the length (due to exponentially decaying temperature profiles), and only the logarithmic mean correctly represents this average driving potential. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is meant by the *effectiveness* of a heat exchanger? Write its mathematical expression.

**Model Answer:**
The effectiveness ($\varepsilon$) of a heat exchanger is defined as the ratio of the actual heat transfer rate to the maximum possible heat transfer rate:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity that ranges from 0 to 1. An effectiveness of 1 indicates an ideal counter-flow heat exchanger of infinite area. For practical exchangers:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$

**[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A concentric tube heat exchanger is used to cool oil (specific heat $2.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, mass flow rate $0.5 \text{ kg/s}$) from $120°C$ to $80°C$ using water (specific heat $4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, mass flow rate $0.8 \text{ kg/s}$) entering at $30°C$. Calculate:
   (i) The heat transfer rate. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The outlet temperature of water. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The LMTD for counter-flow arrangement. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** If the heat transfer area is $2.5 \text{ m}^2$, determine the overall heat transfer coefficient and the effectiveness of the heat exchanger. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a)(i) Heat transfer rate from oil side:**

$$Q = \dot{m}_o \cdot c_{po} \cdot (T_{o,in} - T_{o,out})$$

$$Q = 0.5 \times 2.0 \times (120 - 80)$$

$$\boxed{Q = 40 \text{ kW}}$$

**(a)(ii) Outlet temperature of water:**

From energy balance, $Q = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$

$$40 = 0.8 \times 4.18 \times (T_{w,out} - 30)$$

$$T_{w,out} - 30 = \dfrac{40}{3.344} = 11.96°C$$

$$\boxed{T_{w,out} \approx 41.96°C \approx 42°C}$$

**(a)(iii) LMTD for counter-flow:**

$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 42 = 78°C$
$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 30 = 50°C$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{78 - 50}{\ln(78/50)} = \dfrac{28}{\ln(1.56)} = \dfrac{28}{0.4447}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} \approx 62.96°C}$$

**(b) Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$U = \dfrac{Q}{A_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{40 \times 1000}{2.5 \times 62.96}$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 254.1 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}}$$

**Effectiveness:**

Capacity rates:
- $C_{oil} = 0.5 \times 2.0 = 1.0 \text{ kW/K}$
- $C_{water} = 0.8 \times 4.18 = 3.344 \text{ kW/K}$

Therefore $C_{min} = 1.0 \text{ kW/K}$ (oil is the minimum).

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{120 - 80}{120 - 30} = \dfrac{40}{90}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.444}$$

**[Total: 14 Marks]**

---

#### Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the difference between parallel-flow and counter-flow heat exchangers. Mention any two advantages of counter-flow over parallel-flow exchangers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger has $U = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$, area $5 \text{ m}^2$, and operates on a counter-flow basis. Hot oil ($c_p = 2.1 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, flow $0.6 \text{ kg/s}$) enters at $150°C$ and is to be cooled to $90°C$. Cooling water ($c_p = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$) enters at $25°C$ with a flow rate of $1.0 \text{ kg/s}$. Using the $\varepsilon$-NTU method, determine:
   (i) The effectiveness. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The actual heat transfer rate. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The outlet temperature of water. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a)** Sketches and explanation:

**Parallel Flow:** Both hot and cold fluids enter at the same end and flow in the same direction. The temperature difference is maximum at the inlet and minimum at the outlet. The two streams tend to approach a common outlet temperature, limiting the heat transfer.

**Counter Flow:** The fluids enter at opposite ends and flow in opposite directions. The temperature difference is relatively more uniform along the length, and the cold fluid can be heated to a temperature higher than the hot fluid outlet temperature.

**Two Advantages of Counter-Flow:**
1. Higher mean temperature difference (LMTD) for the same inlet/outlet conditions → higher heat transfer rate for the same area.
2. The cold fluid outlet temperature can exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature, which is impossible in parallel flow.
3. Higher effectiveness for the same NTU and capacity ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)(i) Effectiveness:**

Capacity rates:
- $C_h = 0.6 \times 2.1 = 1.26 \text{ kW/K}$
- $C_c = 1.0 \times 4.18 = 4.18 \text{ kW/K}$

$C_{min} = 1.26 \text{ kW/K}$ (oil side)

Capacity ratio:
$$C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{1.26}{4.18} = 0.3014$$

NTU:
$$NTU = \dfrac{U A_s}{C_{min}} = \dfrac{500 \times 5}{1260} = 1.984$$

For counter-flow:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU (1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-NTU (1 - C_r)]}$$

Compute $NTU(1 - C_r) = 1.984 \times (1 - 0.3014) = 1.984 \times 0.6986 = 1.3860$

$$\exp(-1.3860) = 0.2501$$

Numerator: $1 - 0.2501 = 0.7499$
Denominator: $1 - 0.3014 \times 0.2501 = 1 - 0.0754 = 0.9246$

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{0.7499}{0.9246} \approx 0.8111$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.811}$$

**(b)(ii) Actual heat transfer rate:**

$$Q_{max} = C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 1.26 \times (150 - 25) = 157.5 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q_{actual} = \varepsilon \cdot Q_{max} = 0.811 \times 157.5$$

$$\boxed{Q_{actual} \approx 127.7 \text{ kW}}$$

**(b)(iii) Outlet temperature of water:**

$$Q_{actual} = \dot{m}_c c_{pc} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

$$127.7 = 1.0 \times 4.18 \times (T_{c,out} - 25)$$

$$T_{c,out} - 25 = 30.55°C$$

$$\boxed{T_{c,out} \approx 55.55°C}$$

**[Total: 14 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Wrong sign convention:** Many students take $T_{c,in} - T_{c,out}$ instead of $T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}$ for the cold-stream energy balance, leading to a negative $Q_c$. Marks are deducted for not identifying the inconsistency.
> 2. **Mixing parallel and counter-flow formulas:** Using $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ for a parallel-flow problem will give an incorrect LMTD. Always identify flow configuration from the figure first.
> 3. **Forgetting $A_s$ in the $U$ formula:** A common error is computing $U = Q/\Delta T_{lm}$ without dividing by area. KTU examiners specifically allocate **1 mark** for the area unit conversion.
> 4. **Not cross-checking $Q_h$ vs $Q_c$:** The two values should match within ±5%. If they differ significantly, students are expected to comment on heat loss to surroundings.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute $C_{min}$ first** before applying the $\varepsilon$-NTU formula. Many use $C_h$ blindly and lose 1–2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LMTD** is valid for constant $c_p$ and single-phase fluids. It requires known inlet and outlet temperatures of both streams.
- **$\varepsilon$-NTU method** is used when outlet temperatures are unknown, or when the heat exchanger is part of a network.
- **Counter-flow** always gives higher $\varepsilon$ than parallel-flow for the same NTU and $C_r$.
- The **shell-and-tube** heat exchanger uses an **LMTD correction factor $F$** because it is neither purely counter-flow nor cross-flow.
- **Effectiveness $\varepsilon$** lies between **0 and 1**; never above 1 for a single heat exchanger.
- For a **condenser**, the hot fluid temperature is constant (phase change), so $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ and the LMTD collapses to the arithmetic mean.
- For an **evaporator**, the cold fluid (refrigerant) boils at constant temperature, and the same constant-LMTD simplification applies.
- **Energy balance error** in lab reports should not exceed ±5%; otherwise, the experiment is considered invalid by KTU rubric.
- Always quote **SI units** — Watts for $Q$, W/m²·K for $U$, dimensionless for $\varepsilon$ and NTU.
