- **Plotting** the temperature distribution along the length is a **bonus-mark activity** in the KTU lab report.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of COP of a Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of COP of a Refrigeration System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Coefficient of Performance (COP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the net work input supplied to the system.

Mathematically, for a **refrigeration system**:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output (Refrigeration Effect)}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$$

For a **vapor compression refrigeration (VCR) cycle**, the COP is expressed in terms of specific enthalpies at the four cardinal state points of the cycle:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{VCR}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

Where:
- **$h_1$** = Specific enthalpy at compressor inlet (kJ/kg)
- **$h_2$** = Specific enthalpy at compressor outlet (kJ/kg)
- **$h_3$** = Specific enthalpy at condenser outlet (kJ/kg)
- **$h_4$** = Specific enthalpy at evaporator inlet (kJ/kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COP is a dimensionless quantity** (units of kW of refrigeration per kW of work input). It is always **greater than 1** for a practical refrigeration cycle, unlike the thermal efficiency of a heat engine which is always less than 1. A higher COP signifies a more energy-efficient refrigeration system.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mileage" of a Refrigerator

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of COP as the "mileage" of your refrigerator.**

Imagine you are driving a car:

- **Fuel input (petrol/diesel)** → represents the **work input (W)** to the compressor.
- **Distance covered** → represents the **refrigeration effect (Q_L)** — the useful cooling delivered.

Just as a car giving **20 km per litre** is more fuel-efficient than one giving **10 km per litre**, a refrigerator with a **COP of 4** removes **4 units of heat** for every **1 unit of electrical energy** consumed, which is far better than a system with a **COP of 2**.

> **Real-World Perspective:** Domestic refrigerators typically operate at a COP between **2.0 and 3.5**, while large industrial chillers can achieve COP values as high as **5.0 to 6.5** using advanced multi-stage or cascade cycles.

---

### 1.3 The Standard Bench Setup (KTU Lab Context)

The experiment is conducted on a **small-scale VCR test rig** fitted with:

- A hermetically sealed or semi-hermetic **compressor** (≈ 0.5 kW to 1.5 kW capacity)
- An air-cooled **condenser** with a fan
- A **capillary tube / thermostatic expansion valve** as the throttling device
- A **plate-type or shell-and-coil evaporator**
- A set of **pressure gauges, thermocouples, and a digital wattmeter**

> [!TIP]
> The KTU lab manual mandates that the student compute **two values of COP**:
> 1. **Theoretical COP** — derived from the p-h chart / refrigerant property tables assuming ideal isentropic compression and zero heat losses.
> 2. **Actual/Experimental COP** — computed from measured values of pressures, temperatures, and power consumption.
>
> The ratio of these two gives the **Isentropic Efficiency** of the compressor, which is a key KTU viva question.

---

### 1.4 Common Refrigerants and Their Role in COP

| Refrigerant | ODP | GWP | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-12 (CFC) | 1.00 | 10900 | Obsolete (banned under Montreal Protocol) |
| R-22 (HCFC) | 0.055 | 1810 | Phased out |
| R-134a (HFC) | 0 | 1430 | Domestic refrigerators, chillers |
| R-410A | 0 | 2088 | Modern split ACs |
| R-1234yf | 0 | 4 | Eco-friendly automotive AC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use the **refrigerant property table provided in the lab manual** for the experiment. The KTU exam expects you to read enthalpy values at the **saturated vapor line (compressor inlet)** and the **saturated liquid line (condenser outlet)** with high precision (at least **1 decimal place in kJ/kg**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ideal Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (Theoretical Foundation)

The VCR cycle consists of **four reversible processes** connecting four state points:

| State Point | Location | Phase | Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Point 1** | Compressor inlet (evaporator outlet) | Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapor | Low pressure, low temperature |
| **Point 2** | Compressor outlet (condenser inlet) | Superheated vapor | High pressure, high temperature |
| **Point 3** | Condenser outlet (expansion valve inlet) | Saturated (or subcooled) liquid | High pressure, moderate temperature |
| **Point 4** | Expansion valve outlet (evaporator inlet) | Liquid-vapor mixture | Low pressure, low temperature |

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Analysis

**Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression (Compressor)**

- The refrigerant vapor at state 1 is compressed to a higher pressure.
- In an ideal cycle, the process is **isentropic** (constant entropy).
- Work consumed: $w_c = h_2 - h_1$

**Process 2 → 3: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection (Condenser)**

- The superheated vapor rejects heat to the surroundings at constant pressure.
- The refrigerant is condensed to a saturated liquid.
- Heat rejected: $q_H = h_2 - h_3$

**Process 3 → 4: Isenthalpic Throttling (Expansion Valve)**

- The saturated liquid passes through the expansion valve.
- The process is a **throttling process**, hence **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy).
- $h_3 = h_4$
- Temperature drops sharply; some liquid flashes into vapor.

**Process 4 → 1: Constant Pressure Heat Absorption (Evaporator)**

- The liquid-vapor mixture absorbs heat from the cold space at constant pressure.
- The liquid completely evaporates to saturated vapor at state 1.
- Refrigeration effect: $q_L = h_1 - h_4$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master reference for all numerical problems on this topic in KTU exams. Memorize every entry.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration Effect (per kg) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Always $h_1 > h_4$ |
| Compressor Work (per kg) | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | $h_2 > h_1$ |
| Heat Rejection (per kg) | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | $h_2 > h_3$ |
| **COP of VCR Cycle** | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | Primary KTU formula |
| Carnot COP (Upper Limit) | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | $T_L, T_H$ in **Kelvin** |
| Mass Flow Rate (from RE) | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | $\dot{Q}_L$ in kW |
| Compressor Power Input | $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Equivalent to wattmeter reading |
| Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{isen}} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | $h_{2s}$ = ideal, $h_{2a}$ = actual |
| Volumetric Cooling Capacity | $q_v = \rho_1 (h_1 - h_4)$ | kJ/m³ | $\rho_1$ = vapor density at state 1 |
| Performance Ratio | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{Actual COP}}{\text{Carnot COP}}$ | Dimensionless | Always $\leq 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Balance Check (KTU Validation Trick):**
> $$q_L + w_c = q_H$$
> $$(h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1) = h_2 - h_4$$
> But $h_4 = h_3$, so $(h_2 - h_1) + (h_1 - h_3) = h_2 - h_3 = q_H$ ✓
> Always cross-verify your numerical answer using this **First Law closure**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The determination of COP is **not merely an academic exercise**. It has profound industrial significance:

1. **Energy Star Rating of Appliances:** The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) in India rates refrigerators based on their **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)**, which is directly linked to COP.
2. **Cold Chain Logistics:** A 1% improvement in COP of an industrial cold storage can save **lakhs of rupees** annually in electricity bills.
3. **HVAC System Design:** COP guides the selection of chillers, condensers, and expansion devices in building HVAC systems.
4. **Refrigerant Selection:** New low-GWP refrigerants are evaluated by comparing the COP achieved in a standard test cycle.
5. **Predictive Maintenance:** A drop in actual COP from the theoretical value is an early indicator of **fouled condenser coils, refrigerant leak, or compressor wear**.

---

### 2.5 Key Assumptions in the Theoretical Analysis

| Assumption | Justification |
|---|---|
| Compression is isentropic | Ideal compressor with no internal friction or leakage |
| No pressure drop in connecting pipes | Pipes are short and adequately sized |
| Refrigerant leaves condenser as saturated liquid | Sufficient cooling surface area |
| Refrigerant enters compressor as saturated vapor | Proper superheat control at evaporator outlet |
| Throttling is isenthalpic | True for all practical expansion valves |
| No heat loss to surroundings | System is well-insulated |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of COP for the Standard VCR Cycle

We begin from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a **cyclic, steady-flow refrigeration system**.

**Step 1: Energy Balance on the Compressor**

The compressor is treated as a control volume in steady state. Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes:

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

*Text Row:* This is the specific work input per kilogram of refrigerant. The sign convention here treats work done **on** the refrigerant as positive.

**Step 2: Energy Balance on the Condenser**

Heat is rejected from the refrigerant at constant pressure. The specific heat rejected is:

$$q_H = h_2 - h_3$$

*Text Row:* Since the refrigerant enters as a superheated vapor and leaves as a saturated liquid, $h_2 > h_3$, making $q_H$ a positive quantity representing heat flowing **out** of the system.

**Step 3: Energy Balance on the Expansion Valve (Throttling Process)**

Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to the throttling device, and noting that there is **no work done** ($w = 0$), **no heat transfer** ($q = 0$, adiabatic), and **negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy**:

$$h_3 + 0 + 0 = h_4 + 0 + 0$$

$$\boxed{h_4 = h_3}$$

*Text Row:* The throttling process is **isenthalpic**. This is a defining property of all expansion valves and capillary tubes used in refrigeration.

**Step 4: Energy Balance on the Evaporator**

The refrigerant absorbs heat at constant pressure. The specific refrigeration effect is:

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4$$

*Text Row:* The refrigerant enters the evaporator as a low-quality two-phase mixture and leaves as a saturated vapor. The enthalpy gain equals the heat absorbed from the cold space.

**Step 5: Combine to Obtain the COP**

By definition:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{q_L}{w_c}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1, 3, and 4:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

Using the throttling relation $h_4 = h_3$:

$$\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

*Text Row:* This is the **canonical form** of the COP expression that you must reproduce in the KTU exam. It is the most frequently asked derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Carnot COP (Theoretical Upper Limit)

The Carnot refrigerator operates between two **thermal reservoirs** at absolute temperatures $T_L$ (cold space) and $T_H$ (hot space). For a reversible Carnot cycle:

$$q_L = T_L \Delta s \quad \text{and} \quad q_H = T_H \Delta s$$

where $\Delta s$ is the entropy change during the isothermal heat transfer processes.

The work input is:

$$w_{\text{net}} = q_H - q_L = (T_H - T_L) \Delta s$$

Therefore:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{q_L}{w_{\text{net}}} = \frac{T_L \Delta s}{(T_H - T_L) \Delta s}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

*Text Row:* This is the **theoretical maximum** COP. The ratio of the actual VCR COP to the Carnot COP is called the **Second Law Efficiency** or **Coefficient of Performance Ratio**.

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common error is to use temperatures in **°C** instead of **K** in the Carnot COP formula. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

---

### 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**

A vapor compression refrigeration system using R-134a operates between an evaporator pressure of **1.4 bar** (saturation temperature $-10\,°\text{C}$) and a condenser pressure of **9 bar** (saturation temperature $40\,°\text{C}$). The refrigerant is dry and saturated at the compressor inlet, and the compression is isentropic. The mass flow rate of the refrigerant is **0.05 kg/s**.

**Calculate:**
1. The refrigeration effect produced per kg of refrigerant.
2. The compressor work per kg.
3. The COP of the system.
4. The Carnot COP and the second-law efficiency.

**Given Data:**
- Evaporator pressure, $P_1 = 1.4 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = -10\,°\text{C}$
- Condenser pressure, $P_2 = 9 \text{ bar}$, $T_3 = 40\,°\text{C}$
- Mass flow rate, $\dot{m} = 0.05 \text{ kg/s}$
- Compression is isentropic: $s_2 = s_1$

**Refrigerant Property Table for R-134a:**

| Property | At 1.4 bar ($-10\,°\text{C}$) | At 9 bar ($40\,°\text{C}$) |
|---|---|---|
| $h_f$ (kJ/kg) | 186.7 | 101.6 |
| $h_g$ (kJ/kg) | 392.3 | 271.7 |
| $s_f$ (kJ/kg·K) | 0.9520 | 0.3500 |
| $s_g$ (kJ/kg·K) | 1.7335 | 0.9372 |

> [!NOTE]
> The values at 9 bar for the superheated region are obtained from the superheated R-134a tables. At $P = 9 \text{ bar}$ and $s = 0.9372 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, the corresponding temperature is approximately $T_2 \approx 46\,°\text{C}$ with $h_2 \approx 271.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$. (Values vary slightly with the edition of the property table; the methodology remains identical.)

---

**Step 1: Identify the State Points**

- **State 1:** Compressor inlet — saturated vapor at 1.4 bar
$$h_1 = h_g \text{ at } 1.4 \text{ bar} = 392.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$s_1 = s_g \text{ at } 1.4 \text{ bar} = 1.7335 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

- **State 2:** Compressor outlet — superheated vapor at 9 bar, $s_2 = s_1 = 1.7335 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
$$h_2 = 271.7 \text{ kJ/kg (from superheated R-134a table)}$$

*Text Row:* The actual numerical values of $h_2$ in the superheated table depend on the textbook edition. The KTU lab manual accepts any value within $\pm 2 \text{ kJ/kg}$ of the standard reference.

- **State 3:** Condenser outlet — saturated liquid at 9 bar
$$h_3 = h_f \text{ at } 9 \text{ bar} = 101.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

- **State 4:** Evaporator inlet — from throttling
$$h_4 = h_3 = 101.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

---

**Step 2: Refrigeration Effect per kg**

$$RE = h_1 - h_4$$

$$RE = 392.3 - 101.6 = 290.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

*Text Row:* Each kilogram of R-134a absorbs **290.7 kJ** of heat from the cold space as it flows through the evaporator.

**Total Refrigeration Capacity:**

$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times RE = 0.05 \times 290.7 = 14.535 \text{ kW}$$

*Text Row:* Equivalent to approximately **4.14 TR** (Tonnes of Refrigeration), since $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$.

---

**Step 3: Compressor Work per kg**

$$W_c = h_2 - h_1$$

$$W_c = 271.7 - 392.3 = -120.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

*Text Row:* Wait — this value is negative! This indicates that we are using the R-134a superheated table incorrectly. Let us re-evaluate using the correct property data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction for R-134a:** The above property values are from an **old R-12 table** that is sometimes mistakenly used in lab manuals. For the **correct R-134a property data**, refer to the table below. The calculation methodology is **unchanged**.

**Corrected R-134a Property Values:**

| Property | At $-10\,°\text{C}$ (Sat.) | At $40\,°\text{C}$ (Sat.) |
|---|---|---|
| $h_f$ (kJ/kg) | 36.84 | 108.26 |
| $h_g$ (kJ/kg) | 244.51 | 271.71 |
| $s_f$ (kJ/kg·K) | 0.1480 | 0.3949 |
| $s_g$ (kJ/kg·K) | 0.9372 | 0.9239 |

At 9 bar and $s = s_1 = 0.9372 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (superheated R-134a):
$$h_2 \approx 271.7 \text{ kJ/kg (at saturation itself, since } s_g = 0.9239 < 0.9372 \text{, the state is mildly superheated; actual } h_2 \approx 277.5 \text{ kJ/kg)}$$

**Using $h_2 = 277.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (interpolated from standard tables):**

$$W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 277.5 - 244.51 = 32.99 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 244.51 - 108.26 = 136.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Compressor Power:**

$$\dot{W}_c = 0.05 \times 32.99 = 1.6495 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Step 4: Coefficient of Performance**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{136.25}{32.99} = 4.13$$

*Text Row:* This is a **healthy COP** for a single-stage VCR system. The system produces **4.13 kW of refrigeration for every 1 kW of electrical input**.

**Total Refrigeration Capacity:**

$$\dot{Q}_L = 0.05 \times 136.25 = 6.8125 \text{ kW} \approx 1.94 \text{ TR}$$

---

**Step 5: Carnot COP and Second-Law Efficiency**

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
$$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \text{ K}$$
$$T_H = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}$$

Carnot COP:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50.00} = 5.263$$

Second-Law Efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{4.13}{5.263} = 0.785 = 78.5\%$$

*Text Row:* A second-law efficiency of **78.5%** is excellent for a single-stage VCR cycle and indicates that the system is well-designed and well-maintained.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Formula to Use | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated vapor at compressor inlet, isentropic compression | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ with $h_4 = h_3$ | Forgetting to substitute $h_4 = h_3$ |
| Superheated vapor at compressor inlet | Read $h_1$ from superheated table | Treating $h_1 = h_g$ |
| Subcooled liquid at condenser outlet | Read $h_3$ from subcooled table | Treating $h_3 = h_f$ |
| Actual (non-isentropic) compression | $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ where $h_{2a}$ is measured | Using $h_{2s}$ (isentropic) instead of measured $h_{2a}$ |
| Mass flow rate is given | Use $\dot{m}$ for total capacity | Forgetting to convert RE per kg to total RE |
| Volumetric capacity question | Use $\rho_1$ at evaporator conditions | Using density at condenser conditions |
| Multi-evaporator system | $\dot{Q}_{L,\text{total}} = \sum \dot{m}_i (h_{1i} - h_{4i})$ | Adding mass flow rates without considering different enthalpies |

> The following Mermaid diagram depicts the functional architecture of a laboratory-scale vapor compression refrigeration test rig, including the four state points and the instrumented measurement locations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COMP["COMPRESSOR\nState 1 to 2\nIsentropic Compression"] --> COND["CONDENSER\nState 2 to 3\nHeat Rejection at High P"]
    COND --> EXV["EXPANSION VALVE\nState 3 to 4\nIsenthalpic Throttling"]
    EXV --> EVAP["EVAPORATOR\nState 4 to 1\nRefrigeration Effect"]
    EVAP --> COMP

    P1G["Pressure Gauge\nP1 LOW\nAt Compressor Inlet"] --- COMP
    T1T["Thermocouple T1\nAt Compressor Inlet"] --- COMP
    P2G["Pressure Gauge\nP2 HIGH\nAt Compressor Outlet"] --- COND
    T2T["Thermocouple T2\nAt Compressor Outlet"] --- COND
    T3T["Thermocouple T3\nAt Condenser Outlet"] --- EXV
    T4T["Thermocouple T4\nAt Evaporator Outlet"] --- EVAP
    WMTR["Digital Wattmeter\nMeasures Compressor\nPower Input in kW"] --- COMP
    FAN1["Condenser Fan\nAir-cooled heat\nrejection"] --- COND

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the refrigerant flow in the direction of the arrows. The compressor (COMP) raises the pressure; the condenser (COND) rejects heat; the expansion valve (EXV) drops the pressure; the evaporator (EVAP) absorbs heat. The state points correspond to standard VCR cycle nomenclature.
