## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]: Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigeration system. Why is COP always greater than 1 for practical refrigerators?**

**Model Answer:**

The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigeration system is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect to the net work input supplied to the system:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

COP is always greater than 1 for practical refrigerators because the desired output ($Q_L$) is the heat extracted from the cold space, while the work input is only the **fraction** of the heat equivalent that has been upgraded by the compressor. The remaining heat is rejected to the surroundings. In a heat engine, the work output is **less than** the heat input (by the Second Law), making efficiency always less than 1. In a refrigerator, the useful effect is the **larger** heat extracted, while the work input is the **smaller** quantity driving the cycle, so the ratio exceeds 1. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]: What is the difference between the theoretical COP and the actual COP of a vapor compression refrigeration system?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Theoretical COP | Actual COP |
|---|---|---|
| Compression process | Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) | Polytropic with internal irreversibilities |
| Heat transfer | No losses to surroundings | Heat gains/losses through pipes and insulation |
| Pressure drop | Negligible in connecting pipes | Finite pressure drop in suction and discharge lines |
| Refrigerant state | Dry saturated at compressor inlet | Slightly superheated due to suction line heat gain |
| Condenser exit | Saturated liquid | Subcooled liquid (improves COP slightly) |
| Value | Higher (typically 4 to 6) | Lower (typically 2 to 4) |

The ratio $\dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}}$ is called the **Second-Law Efficiency** or **Coefficient of Performance Ratio** and is a measure of how closely the real cycle approaches the ideal reversible cycle. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the COP of a standard vapor compression refrigeration cycle in terms of the enthalpies at the four state points. Clearly state the assumption made for the throttling process.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vapor compression refrigeration system uses R-134a as the working fluid. The evaporator operates at $-15\,°\text{C}$ and the condenser at $40\,°\text{C}$. The refrigerant is dry saturated at the compressor inlet. Assuming isentropic compression and a refrigerant mass flow rate of $0.04 \text{ kg/s}$, calculate:

1. The refrigeration effect in kW
2. The compressor work in kW
3. The COP of the system
4. The Carnot COP and second-law efficiency

**Use the following R-134a data:**
- At $-15\,°\text{C}$: $h_f = 31.46 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_g = 241.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_g = 0.9290 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- At $40\,°\text{C}$: $h_f = 108.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_g = 0.9239 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- At 9 bar and $s = 0.9290 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (superheated): $h_2 = 277.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

Consider a steady-flow VCR cycle with four state points. Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each component with the assumptions of negligible kinetic and potential energy changes:

**Compressor (1 → 2):** Isentropic compression requires work input:
$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Condenser (2 → 3):** Heat rejected at constant pressure:
$$q_H = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Expansion Valve (3 → 4):** Throttling process — no work, no heat transfer, negligible KE/PE changes:
$$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Evaporator (4 → 1):** Heat absorbed at constant pressure:
$$q_L = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

By definition:
$$\text{COP} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

Substituting (iii):
$$\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

The **key assumption** is that the throttling process is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$), which is justified because the expansion valve performs no work, involves negligible heat transfer (process is rapid), and has negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Identify state points**
- $h_1 = h_g \text{ at } -15\,°\text{C} = 241.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_2 = 277.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (given, from superheated table)
- $h_3 = h_f \text{ at } 40\,°\text{C} = 108.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_4 = h_3 = 108.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (throttling)

**Step 2: Refrigeration effect**
$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 241.10 - 108.26 = 132.84 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times RE = 0.04 \times 132.84 = 5.314 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Compressor work**
$$W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 277.3 - 241.10 = 36.20 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$\dot{W}_c = 0.04 \times 36.20 = 1.448 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: COP of the system**
$$\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{132.84}{36.20} = 3.67$$

**Step 5: Carnot COP and Second-Law Efficiency**

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
$$T_L = -15 + 273.15 = 258.15 \text{ K}$$
$$T_H = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258.15}{55.00} = 4.694$$

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{3.67}{4.694} = 0.782 = 78.2\%$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Refrigeration Effect | $5.314 \text{ kW}$ |
| Compressor Work | $1.448 \text{ kW}$ |
| Actual COP | $3.67$ |
| Carnot COP | $4.694$ |
| Second-Law Efficiency | $78.2\%$ |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the procedure to determine the COP of a refrigeration system experimentally in the laboratory. List the apparatus required and the readings to be taken.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A refrigeration system operates between a cold reservoir at $-5\,°\text{C}$ and a hot reservoir at $35\,°\text{C}$. The actual COP of the system is 2.8. Calculate:

1. The Carnot COP
2. The second-law efficiency
3. The work input per kW of refrigeration
4. The heat rejected per kW of refrigeration

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Experimental Procedure [7 Marks]:**

**Apparatus Required:**
- Vapor compression refrigeration test rig (0.5 to 1.5 kW capacity)
- R-134a refrigerant (charged in the system)
- Two bourdon-type pressure gauges (one at compressor inlet, one at outlet)
- Four K-type thermocouples with digital temperature indicator
- Digital wattmeter (0 to 2000 W range)
- Rotameter or Coriolis mass flow meter
- Stopwatch

**Procedure:**
1. Visually inspect the test rig for any visible refrigerant leakage, loose connections, and ensure the condenser fan is unobstructed.
2. Verify the refrigerant charge by checking the sight glass on the liquid line.
3. Connect the wattmeter in series with the compressor power line.
4. Switch on the condenser fan and the evaporator water pump (if any).
5. Start the compressor and note the start time.
6. Allow the system to run for **10 to 15 minutes** to reach steady-state conditions (indicated by stable gauge readings and constant wattmeter reading).
7. Record all the following readings:
   - Compressor inlet pressure $P_1$ and temperature $T_1$
   - Compressor outlet pressure $P_2$ and temperature $T_2$
   - Condenser outlet temperature $T_3$
   - Evaporator outlet temperature $T_4$
   - Compressor power input $W_c$ (from wattmeter)
   - Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (if measured)
   - Ambient temperature $T_{\text{amb}}$
8. Repeat the readings **three times** at 5-minute intervals to ensure repeatability.
9. Refer to the R-134a saturation property tables (provided in the lab manual) to determine the enthalpies $h_1$, $h_3$, and $h_4$ at the measured pressures and temperatures.
10. For $h_2$, use the superheated R-134a table at pressure $P_2$ and entropy $s_1$ (assumed isentropic compression) — or directly use the measured $T_2$ if the actual value is to be considered.
11. Calculate:
    - Refrigeration Effect per kg: $RE = h_1 - h_4$
    - Compressor Work per kg: $W_c = h_2 - h_1$
    - $\text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{W_c}$
12. Compare the experimental COP with the Carnot COP and compute the second-law efficiency.
13. Shut down the system, purge the test data, and prepare the lab report.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- $T_L = -5\,°\text{C} = 268.15 \text{ K}$
- $T_H = 35\,°\text{C} = 308.15 \text{ K}$
- $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = 2.8$

**Step 1: Carnot COP**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268.15}{308.15 - 268.15} = \frac{268.15}{40.00} = 6.704$$

**Step 2: Second-Law Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{2.8}{6.704} = 0.418 = 41.8\%$$

*Interpretation:* The system operates at only **41.8%** of the theoretical maximum efficiency, indicating significant scope for improvement — perhaps by using multi-stage compression with intercooling or a cascade system.

**Step 3: Work Input per kW of Refrigeration**

By definition of COP:
$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$$

For $Q_L = 1 \text{ kW}$:
$$W_{\text{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}} = \frac{1.0}{2.8} = 0.357 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Heat Rejected per kW of Refrigeration**

From the First Law for a cyclic refrigerator:
$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{net}}$$
$$Q_H = 1.0 + 0.357 = 1.357 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Carnot COP | $6.704$ |
| Second-Law Efficiency | $41.8\%$ |
| Work Input (per kW of cooling) | $0.357 \text{ kW}$ |
| Heat Rejected (per kW of cooling) | $1.357 \text{ kW}$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature Units:** Forgetting to convert **°C to K** in the Carnot COP formula. This single mistake costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Throttling Equation:** Writing $h_3 \neq h_4$ or omitting the statement that throttling is isenthalpic. Always show the SFEE derivation for the expansion valve.
> 3. **Property Table Confusion:** Mixing up R-12 and R-134a property values. Always confirm the refrigerant with the lab instructor before the exam.
> 4. **Mass Flow Rate Error:** Forgetting to multiply the specific refrigeration effect (kJ/kg) by mass flow rate (kg/s) to get the total refrigeration capacity in kW. Units are **strictly checked** by KTU examiners.
> 5. **Sign Convention:** Writing $w_c = h_1 - h_2$ instead of $h_2 - h_1$. The work done **on** the refrigerant must be **positive**.
> 6. **Energy Balance Closure Check:** Failing to verify $(h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1) = h_2 - h_3$ at the end. This is a quick sanity check and earns **bonus marks** if shown.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following high-density bullet list consolidates the entire topic for last-minute revision before the KTU lab exam.**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ **COP** = Refrigeration Effect (Q_L) / Work Input (W_net) — dimensionless, always > 1.
- ☐ **Carnot COP** = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — temperatures strictly in **Kelvin**.
- ☐ **Second-Law Efficiency** = $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} / \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$ — always ≤ 1.
- ☐ **Throttling process** is **isenthalpic**: $h_3 = h_4$.
- ☐ **Isentropic compression**: $s_1 = s_2$ (ideal case only).

**The Four State Points (Memorize These):**
- ☐ **State 1** — Compressor inlet, low pressure, saturated or slightly superheated vapor.
- ☐ **State 2** — Compressor outlet, high pressure, superheated vapor.
- ☐ **State 3** — Condenser outlet, high pressure, saturated or subcooled liquid.
- ☐ **State 4** — Evaporator inlet, low pressure, two-phase liquid-vapor mixture.

**Master Equations (KTU Exam Favourites):**
- ☐ $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$
- ☐ $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$ in kW
- ☐ $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ in kW
- ☐ $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$ in kW
- ☐ Energy balance: $\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c = \dot{Q}_H$ (always verify!)

**Key Factors That Increase COP:**
- ☐ Lower **evaporator temperature** ($T_L$ decreases → numerator of Carnot COP **decreases** → wait, this actually **decreases** COP. Correction: COP is increased by **raising** $T_L$ or **lowering** $T_H$.)
- ☐ **Lower condenser temperature** (better cooling water or ambient air).
- ☐ **Higher evaporator temperature** (warmer cold space, less temperature lift).
- ☐ **Lower subcooling** of refrigerant (less work in expansion).
- ☐ **Use of multi-stage compression with intercooling** for large temperature lifts.

**Experimental Best Practices:**
- ☐ Always wait for **steady state** (10–15 minutes) before recording.
- ☐ Take **at least three readings** and report the **mean value**.
- ☐ Record **ambient temperature** — it affects condenser performance.
- ☐ Use the **correct refrigerant property table** (R-134a is most common).
- ☐ Compute **both theoretical and actual COP** — the ratio gives the **isentropic efficiency** of the compressor.

**Common Viva Questions (Be Prepared):**
- ☐ *"Why is the COP of a refrigerator always greater than 1?"*
- ☐ *"What happens to COP if the condenser cooling water temperature increases?"*
- ☐ *"Why is the throttling process assumed to be isenthalpic?"*
- ☐ *"What is the difference between COP and EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio)?"*
- ☐ *"How does a lower evaporator temperature affect the compressor discharge temperature?"*
- ☐ *"Why do we use subcooling before the expansion valve?"*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Pro Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** Always carry a **scientific calculator**, a **printed R-134a property table** (or memorize the saturation values at 0 °C, 10 °C, 20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C), and a **graph sheet** to plot COP vs. evaporator temperature if required. Most marks are lost in **unit conversion errors** and **property table misreading** — practice the table reading at least **5 times** before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Refrigeration Load for Cold Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Refrigeration Load for Cold Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Refrigeration Load Estimation for Cold Storage** is the systematic thermodynamic calculation of the total rate of heat removal (expressed in **kW** or **TR — Tons of Refrigeration, where 1 TR = 3.517 kW**) required to maintain a designated cold storage enclosure at a prescribed low temperature, against all sources of heat ingress, when storing perishable or temperature-sensitive commodities.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is also termed the **Total Cooling Load (Q_total)** or **Design Refrigeration Load** of a cold store, and forms the foundational step in selecting the appropriate compressor, condenser, evaporator, and refrigerant for the refrigeration plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to identify the individual components contributing to the total refrigeration load, apply standard heat transfer equations, and perform numerical calculations using realistic design data for typical cold storage applications (e.g., dairy, frozen foods, fruits & vegetables).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to keep a glass of ice-cold water cold on a hot summer afternoon. The water warms up because:
1. Heat leaks in through the **glass walls** (transmission),
2. If you keep opening the lid, warm air rushes in (**infiltration**),
3. The ice itself melts because it is doing internal work to stay cold (**product load**), and
4. Someone turns on a lamp nearby, radiating extra heat (**service/equipment load**).

To keep the water cold, your "refrigeration system" must remove **all** this heat continuously. **Cold storage load estimation is exactly this — but on a much larger engineering scale**, where the "glass" is an insulated room, the "ice" is tons of potatoes or meat, and the "lamp" is the lighting, motors, and workers inside the cold room.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Cold Storage Load Estimation:**
> - **1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration)** = **3.517 kW** = **3024 kcal/hr**
> - **Specific heat of water** = **4.187 kJ/kg·K**
> - **Specific heat of ice** = **2.093 kJ/kg·K**
> - **Latent heat of fusion of water** = **334 kJ/kg**
> - **Specific heat of air** = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
> - **Density of air (at standard conditions)** = **1.204 kg/m³**
> - **Respiration rate of fruits** (e.g., apples) ≈ **0.011 W/kg** at 0 °C
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Total Refrigeration Load — Structured Logic

The **Total Refrigeration Load (Q_total)** of a cold storage is the algebraic sum of the following discrete load components. Each must be computed separately and then aggregated:

**Step 1 — Wall, Ceiling, and Floor Transmission Load (Q₁)**
This is the heat conducted through the building envelope due to the temperature difference between the cold interior and the warm exterior.
- The 'why': Heat always flows spontaneously from high temperature to low temperature. The thicker and better-insulated the wall, the lower this load.
- The 'how': Use Fourier's law of heat conduction through a composite wall: $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$, where $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $A$ is the surface area (m²), and $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference (K or °C).

**Step 2 — Product Load (Q₂)**
This is the heat that must be removed to cool the incoming product (food, medicine, etc.) from its arrival temperature down to the storage temperature, and — if applicable — to freeze the water content.
- The 'why': Every commodity has thermal mass; reducing its temperature requires energy removal.
- The 'how': $Q_2 = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{store})$ for chilling; add $m \cdot h_{fg}$ for the latent component if freezing occurs.

**Step 3 — Respiratory Load of Stored Produce (Q₃)**
Applies only to **fruits, vegetables, and living plants** — biological respiration generates metabolic heat.
- The 'why': Living produce respires even after harvest, consuming O₂ and releasing CO₂ + heat.
- The 'how': $Q_3 = m \cdot r$, where $r$ is the specific respiration rate (W/kg) at the storage temperature.

**Step 4 — Air Infiltration / Air-Curtain / Door-Opening Load (Q₄)**
Warm, humid outside air enters the cold room every time the door opens, mixing with cold interior air.
- The 'how': $Q_4 = V_{in} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) + V_{in} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_{out} - W_{in})$, where $V_{in}$ is the volume of infiltrating air (m³/s), and the second term accounts for moisture condensation.

**Step 5 — Internal Heat Sources — Lighting, Motors, Workers (Q₅)**
Electric lights, evaporator fan motors, and personnel inside the cold room all dissipate heat.
- The 'how': Sum the rated wattage of all equipment plus an allowance per worker (~ 250–300 W per person for moderate activity).

**Step 6 — Miscellaneous / Safety Factor (Q₆)**
A design margin (typically **10 % to 20 %** of the subtotal) is added to account for uncertainties.

**Final Aggregation:**

$$Q_{total} = (Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5) \times (1 + S_f)$$

where $S_f$ is the safety factor (typically 0.10 to 0.20).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Load Component | Governing Formula | Key Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Transmission (wall/ceiling/floor) | $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $U$ = W/m²K, $A$ = m², $\Delta T$ = K | Composite $U$ from layered construction |
| 2 | Product cooling (sensible) | $Q_{2s} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $m$ = kg, $c_p$ = kJ/kg·K | Above freezing point |
| 3 | Product cooling (latent) | $Q_{2l} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | $h_{fg}$ = kJ/kg | Phase change water → ice |
| 4 | Respiratory load | $Q_3 = m \cdot r$ | $r$ = W/kg | Fruits/vegetables only |
| 5 | Air infiltration (sensible) | $Q_{4s} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\dot{V}$ = m³/s | Door openings |
| 6 | Air infiltration (latent) | $Q_{4l} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | $\Delta W$ = kg water/kg dry air | Humidity load |
| 7 | Lighting load | $Q_{5a} = P_{lights} \cdot t_{usage}$ | kW × hr/day | Convert to kW average |
| 8 | Occupancy load | $Q_{5b} = N_{people} \cdot q_{person}$ | ~ 0.25–0.30 kW/person | Activity-dependent |
| 9 | Motor/fan heat | $Q_{5c} = P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{invert}$ | Fraction of input | For evaporator fans |
| 10 | Total design load | $Q_{total} = \Sigma Q_i \cdot (1 + S_f)$ | $S_f$ = 0.10–0.20 | Add safety margin |
| 11 | Convert to TR | $TR = Q_{total}(kW) / 3.517$ | — | 1 TR = 3.517 kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Accurate cold storage load estimation is the cornerstone of refrigerated warehouse design, dairy cold chains, pharmaceutical cold rooms, frozen food logistics hubs, and horticulture preservation facilities. An under-estimate spoils the product; an over-estimate inflates capital and operating costs.

### 2.3 Real-World Application Examples

- **Dairy cold storage** (milk, butter, cheese) — typically maintained at **2 °C to 4 °C**.
- **Frozen food storage** (meat, seafood, ice cream) — typically maintained at **−18 °C to −25 °C**.
- **Fruit and vegetable storage** — **0 °C to 13 °C** with **85–95 % relative humidity**.
- **Pharmaceutical cold rooms** (vaccines, insulin) — **2 °C to 8 °C** with tight tolerance.
- **Banana ripening rooms** — **13 °C to 15 °C** with ethylene control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) for a Composite Wall

Most cold storage walls are **multi-layered**: an inner structural layer, an insulation layer (e.g., polyurethane foam, expanded polystyrene), and an outer cladding. To compute transmission load, we need the **overall U-value**.

For a flat composite wall with $n$ layers in series, thermal resistance is additive:

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + \dots + R_n + R_{se}$$

where $R_i = L_i / k_i$ for each layer of thickness $L_i$ and thermal conductivity $k_i$, and $R_{si}$, $R_{se}$ are the inner and outer surface (film) resistances.

Therefore, the **overall heat transfer coefficient** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i} + \frac{1}{h_o}} \\
\Delta T &= T_{outside} - T_{inside} \\
Q_{transmission} &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

A cold room wall consists of: 10 mm cement plaster (inside), 100 mm polyurethane foam, and 6 mm plywood (outside). 
- $k_{plaster} = 0.72$ W/m·K
- $k_{PUF} = 0.024$ W/m·K
- $k_{plywood} = 0.12$ W/m·K
- $h_i = 8.7$ W/m²K (interior, still air, cold side)
- $h_o = 23$ W/m²K (exterior, 6.7 m/s wind)

**Step 1 — Compute individual resistances:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{si} &= \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{8.7} = 0.115 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{plaster} &= \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.010}{0.72} = 0.0139 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{PUF} &= \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.100}{0.024} = 4.167 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{plywood} &= \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.006}{0.12} = 0.050 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{se} &= \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{23} = 0.043 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum resistances:**

$$R_{total} = 0.115 + 0.0139 + 4.167 + 0.050 + 0.043 = 4.389 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute U:**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{4.389} = 0.228 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — If wall area $A = 40$ m² and $\Delta T = 35$ K (outside 38 °C, inside 3 °C):**

$$Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.228 \times 40 \times 35 = 319.2 \text{ W}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Product Cooling Load for Partially Frozen Goods

When a product is cooled from $T_1$ to above its freezing point $T_f$, only sensible heat is removed:

$$Q_{2s} = m \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot (T_1 - T_f)$$

When the product is cooled from $T_1$ through its freezing point and below to $T_2$, the load has three stages:
1. **Sensible cooling** from $T_1$ to freezing point $T_f$.
2. **Latent heat removal** at $T_f$ to freeze the water content.
3. **Sensible cooling** of the frozen product from $T_f$ to final $T_2$.

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{2,total} &= m \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot (T_1 - T_f) \\
&\quad + m \cdot w \cdot h_{fg} \\
&\quad + m \cdot c_{p,below} \cdot (T_f - T_2)
\end{aligned}$$

where $w$ is the mass fraction of water in the product (e.g., meat ≈ 0.70, fish ≈ 0.80, butter ≈ 0.16).

**Numerical Worked Example:**

Freeze 2000 kg of fish from 25 °C to −20 °C. Fish: $c_{p,above} = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,below} = 1.8$ kJ/kg·K, $T_f = -2$ °C, $w = 0.80$, $h_{fg} = 235$ kJ/kg (for bound water in fish, lower than pure water).

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{s1} &= 2000 \times 3.5 \times (25 - (-2)) = 2000 \times 3.5 \times 27 = 189{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{lat} &= 2000 \times 0.80 \times 235 = 376{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{s2} &= 2000 \times 1.8 \times (-2 - (-20)) = 2000 \times 1.8 \times 18 = 64{,}800 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{2,total} &= 189{,}000 + 376{,}000 + 64{,}800 = 629{,}800 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

If this cooling must be completed in **6 hours** (21,600 s), the **average product load rate** is:

$$\dot{Q}_2 = \frac{629{,}800}{21{,}600} = 29.16 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Air Infiltration Load

When the door opens, outside air (typically warm and humid) enters the cold room. The sensible load is:

$$Q_{4s} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

The latent load (from moisture condensation on the evaporator coil) is:

$$Q_{4l} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot h_{fg,water} \cdot (W_{out} - W_{in})$$

where $W$ is the humidity ratio (kg water vapor per kg dry air).

**Total infiltration load:**

$$Q_4 = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot \left[ c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T + h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W \right]$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Design Load with Safety Factor

Summing all individual load components and applying a design safety margin $S_f$ (commonly 10 %):

$$Q_{total} = (Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5) \times (1 + S_f)$$

**Conversion to Tons of Refrigeration (TR):**

$$TR = \frac{Q_{total} \text{ in kW}}{3.517}$$

---

### 3.5 Consolidated Analytical Matrix — Typical Cold Storage Load Magnitudes

| Cold Store Type | Storage Temp (°C) | Typical Specific Load (W/m³) | Dominant Load |
|-----------------|-------------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| Banana ripening | 13 – 15 | 30 – 50 | Respiration + Transmission |
| Fresh fruits & vegetables | 0 – 4 | 25 – 40 | Respiration + Transmission |
| Dairy products | 2 – 4 | 40 – 60 | Transmission + Infiltration |
| Frozen meat/seafood | −18 to −25 | 60 – 100 | Transmission (high ΔT) + Product |
| Ice cream deep freeze | −25 to −30 | 80 – 120 | Transmission dominant |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Cold Storage Load Aggregation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how individual heat sources aggregate into the final design refrigeration load, and the downstream decision flow for equipment selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cold Storage Enclosure] --> B[Q1 Transmission Load]
    A --> C[Q2 Product Load]
    A --> D[Q3 Respiratory Load]
    A --> E[Q4 Infiltration Load]
    A --> F[Q5 Internal Heat Sources]
    A --> G[Q6 Miscellaneous]

    B --> H[Sum of All Heat Gains]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I[Apply Safety Factor S_f = 0.10 to 0.20]
    I --> J[Total Design Load Q_total in kW]

    J --> K[Convert to TR]
    K --> L[Compressor Sizing]
    J --> M[Evaporator Sizing]
    J --> N[Condenser Sizing]
    J --> O[Refrigerant Selection]

    L --> P[Final Plant Specification]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
- Inputs **A through G** are independent heat contributions; each must be measured or estimated with engineering data.
- Node **H** performs the algebraic summation.
- Node **I** applies the design safety margin to account for uncertainties (e.g., door left open longer, peak ambient temperature, fouled condenser).
- Node **J** yields the design load that drives the **compressor capacity selection**, the **evaporator heat duty**, the **condenser rejection capacity**, and the **refrigerant choice** (e.g., R-134a, R-404A, R-22 alternatives, NH₃).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical Cold Storage System with Load Inputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[External Environment at T_out]
        E1[Sun Radiation]
        E2[Ambient Air Infiltration]
        E3[High T outside]
    end

    subgraph ROOM[Cold Room at T_store]
        R1[Product being cooled]
        R2[Stored Fruits/Vegetables Respiring]
        R3[Lighting and Fan Motors]
        R4[Workers]
    end

    subgraph SYS[Refrigeration System]
        S1[Evaporator Coil in Room]
        S2[Compressor]
        S3[Condenser]
        S4[Expansion Valve]
    end

    E1 -->|Q1| ROOM
    E2 -->|Q4| ROOM
    R1 -->|Q2| ROOM
    R2 -->|Q3| ROOM
    R3 -->|Q5| ROOM
    R4 -->|Q5| ROOM

    ROOM -->|Q_total must be removed| S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S1
```

**Key insight:** Every arrow entering the **ROOM** block represents an independent contribution to the total refrigeration load. The **SYS** block must be sized to remove the **algebraic sum** of all these contributions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> **List any six components of the total refrigeration load for a cold storage.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **Transmission load** — heat conducted through walls, floor, and ceiling.
2. **Product load** — sensible and latent heat of the commodities being cooled/frozen.
3. **Respiratory load** — metabolic heat from living fruits and vegetables.
4. **Air infiltration / air-change load** — heat carried in by warm outside air through door openings.
5. **Internal heat sources** — lighting, evaporator fan motors, and personnel working inside.
6. **Miscellaneous / safety load** — design margin (10–20 %) to cover uncertainties.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **Define "Tons of Refrigeration (TR)" and state its equivalent in kW.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

- **Definition:** One Ton of Refrigeration is the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb = 907.185 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.
- **Numerical value:**
$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{907.185 \times 334 \text{ kJ}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{302{,}400 \text{ kJ}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}$$
- Therefore, **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern):** Part B questions offer a choice. Attempt any ONE full question. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. All sub-parts must be answered.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

> **(a) Derive an expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient (U) of a composite cold room wall and explain the significance of each term.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

For a flat composite wall comprising $n$ layers in series, the steady-state one-dimensional heat conduction follows Fourier's Law. The thermal circuit analogy yields:

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{se}$$

Expanding each term:

$$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \dots + \frac{L_n}{k_n} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

**Significance of each term:**

- $1/h_i$ — **Internal film resistance** at the cold room air-side. Accounts for convective heat transfer between cold air and inner wall surface; depends on air velocity and surface roughness.
- $L_i / k_i$ — **Conductive resistance** of each solid layer (structural, insulation, cladding). $L_i$ is thickness in metres; $k_i$ is thermal conductivity in W/m·K. The insulation layer contributes the **largest resistance** (lowest $k$).
- $1/h_o$ — **External film resistance** on the ambient air side. Depends on outdoor wind speed; for still air ~ 8.3 W/m²K; for 6.7 m/s wind ~ 23 W/m²K.

The **overall U-value** (W/m²K) is the reciprocal:

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}}$$

And the heat transmission per unit area is $q = U \cdot \Delta T$.

**Physical meaning:** $U$ represents the **ease** with which heat flows through the wall — **lower $U$ means better insulation**. The insulation layer's $L/k$ term dominates, so engineers select low-$k$ materials (PUF, XPS) and increase their thickness to minimize $U$.

---

> **(b) A cold storage room has external dimensions 10 m × 6 m × 4 m high. The walls are constructed of 100 mm brick (k = 0.70 W/m·K), 80 mm corkboard insulation (k = 0.045 W/m·K), and 12 mm plaster (k = 0.18 W/m·K). The ceiling and floor have the same U-value as the walls. Inside temperature is 2 °C; outside is 38 °C. Compute the total transmission load.** **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Cold room external dimensions: 10 m × 6 m × 4 m (L × W × H)
- Wall layers (outside → inside): 12 mm plaster | 100 mm brick | 80 mm corkboard
- Inside temperature $T_i = 2$ °C; outside temperature $T_o = 38$ °C
- Take $h_i = 9.0$ W/m²K, $h_o = 20$ W/m²K (assumed standard).

**Step 1 — Compute resistance of each layer:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{si} &= \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{9.0} = 0.1111 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{plaster} &= \frac{0.012}{0.18} = 0.0667 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{brick} &= \frac{0.100}{0.70} = 0.1429 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{cork} &= \frac{0.080}{0.045} = 1.7778 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{se} &= \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.0500 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_{total} &= 0.1111 + 0.0667 + 0.1429 + 1.7778 + 0.0500 = 2.1485 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute U:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2.1485} = 0.4654 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute surface areas:**
- Two long walls: $2 \times (10 \times 4) = 80 \text{ m}^2$
- Two short walls: $2 \times (6 \times 4) = 48 \text{ m}^2$
- Ceiling: $10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ m}^2$
- Floor: $10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ m}^2$
- **Total $A = 80 + 48 + 60 + 60 = 248 \text{ m}^2$**

**Step 4 — Compute transmission load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= 38 - 2 = 36 \text{ K} \\
Q_1 &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.4654 \times 248 \times 36 \\
Q_1 &= 4155.2 \text{ W} \approx 4.16 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Total transmission load = **4.16 kW** (≈ **1.18 TR**).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

> **(a) Explain with a neat sketch how the air infiltration load through door openings is estimated. List the standard assumptions used.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Air infiltration load is the heat carried into the cold room by warm, humid outside air that enters whenever the door is opened. It has two components:

**Sensible component (temperature load):**

$$Q_{4s} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Latent component (moisture load):**

$$Q_{4l} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_{out} - W_{in})$$

**Total infiltration load:**

$$Q_4 = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot \left[ c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T + h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W \right]$$

The infiltration air volume flow rate is estimated as:

$$\dot{V} = n \cdot A_{door} \cdot v_{air}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of door openings per hour
- $A_{door}$ = door area (m²)
- $v_{air}$ = average velocity of inflow (typically 0.5 – 1.0 m/s)

**Standard assumptions:**
1. Air entering mixes completely with cold room air.
2. Door opening time is short compared to the time between openings.
3. Outside air is at ambient temperature and humidity.
4. Use of an **air curtain** can reduce the effective infiltration volume by 60–80 %.
5. The density and specific heat of air are taken at the average of $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$.

---

> **(b) A frozen food cold storage is maintained at −20 °C. The following data are provided:**
> - Wall area = 250 m², U = 0.30 W/m²K
> - Product load = 8000 kg/day of meat cooled from 5 °C to −20 °C; meat $c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K (frozen), $h_{fg} = 235$ kJ/kg, $T_f = -2$ °C, $c_{p,above} = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K
> - Infiltration load = 2.5 kW
> - Lights: 4 × 100 W, on 12 hr/day
> - Workers: 2 persons × 0.30 kW, 8 hr/day
> - Outside temperature = 35 °C
> - **Compute the total design refrigeration load in kW and in TR, using a 10 % safety factor.** **[7 Marks]**

**Given / Assumed:** Convert all 24-hour loads to **average kW** by dividing total energy per day by 86,400 s.

**Step 1 — Transmission load $Q_1$:**

$$\Delta T = 35 - (-20) = 55 \text{ K}$$
$$Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.30 \times 250 \times 55 = 4125 \text{ W} = 4.125 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Product load $Q_2$:**

For 8000 kg of meat cooled from 5 °C to −20 °C:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{s1} &= m \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot (T_1 - T_f) = 8000 \times 3.5 \times (5 - (-2)) \\
&= 8000 \times 3.5 \times 7 = 196{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{lat} &= m \cdot h_{fg} = 8000 \times 235 = 1{,}880{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{s2} &= m \cdot c_{p,below} \cdot (T_f - T_2) = 8000 \times 2.1 \times (-2 - (-20)) \\
&= 8000 \times 2.1 \times 18 = 302{,}400 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{2,total} &= 196{,}000 + 1{,}880{,}000 + 302{,}400 = 2{,}378{,}400 \text{ kJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to average kW:

$$Q_2 = \frac{2{,}378{,}400}{86{,}400} = 27.53 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Infiltration load $Q_4$ (given):**

$$Q_4 = 2.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Internal heat sources $Q_5$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{5,lights} &= \frac{4 \times 100 \text{ W} \times 12 \text{ hr}}{24 \text{ hr}} = 200 \text{ W (avg)} = 0.20 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{5,workers} &= \frac{2 \times 0.30 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ hr}}{24 \text{ hr}} = 0.20 \text{ kW} \\
Q_5 &= 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.40 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Sum and apply 10 % safety factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{subtotal} &= Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_4 + Q_5 \\
&= 4.125 + 27.53 + 2.5 + 0.40 = 34.555 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{total} &= 34.555 \times 1.10 = 38.01 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Tons of Refrigeration:**

$$TR = \frac{38.01}{3.517} = 10.81 \text{ TR}$$

**Final Answer:** Total design refrigeration load = **38.01 kW** ≈ **10.81 TR**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert daily kJ to average kW** — daily product load must be divided by 86,400 s before being added to instantaneous loads (kW). Many students add kJ directly, losing 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the safety factor** — the 10–20 % margin is mandatory in KTU valuation; not adding it results in loss of 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up unit of $h_{fg}$** — for water use 334 kJ/kg, for food-bound water typically 200–235 kJ/kg. Using 334 for meat is over-estimated.
> 4. **Forgetting the latent component in product load** — when cooling across the freezing point, the latent heat of freezing is often the **largest** term (≈ 60–70 % of the total). Omitting it loses 3 marks.
> 5. **Surface film resistances $R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$** — many students omit these in the U-value calculation, leading to a higher (incorrect) $U$ and inflated transmission load.
> 6. **Not converting °C to K** — since $\Delta T$ in K equals $\Delta T$ in °C, this is often a free pass, but examiners check for dimensional awareness in unit marks.
> 7. **Confusing 1 TR = 3.517 kW with 1 TR = 3.6 kW** — the latter is for 1 kWh. The correct value is **3.517 kW** for continuous heat removal.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Total cold storage load** = (Transmission + Product + Respiration + Infiltration + Internal sources) × (1 + Safety factor).
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr** — memorize this conversion.
- **Transmission load:** $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$; the **$U$-value** is the reciprocal of the sum of all thermal resistances (including inner and outer film resistances).
- **Product load** has **three stages** for freezing: sensible (above $T_f$) + latent (at $T_f$) + sensible (below $T_f$).
- **Respiratory load** applies **only to living produce** (fruits, vegetables); use tabulated respiration rates (W/kg) at the storage temperature.
- **Infiltration load** has both **sensible** (temperature) and **latent** (humidity) components.
- **Lighting, motor, and occupancy loads** are **time-averaged** over 24 hours.
- **Always apply a 10–20 % safety margin** to the sum of all individual loads.
- **Convert daily energy (kJ) to average power (kW)** by dividing by 86,400 s before summing with instantaneous loads.
- **Heat flows from hot to cold**; the higher the $\Delta T$ across the wall, the larger the transmission load.
- **For cold stores**: lower $k$, higher $L$ of insulation $\Rightarrow$ lower $U$ $\Rightarrow$ lower transmission load.
- **The dominant load** varies: chilled stores (~5 °C) → transmission + product; deep freezers (~−20 °C) → transmission dominant due to large $\Delta T$.
- **R-values** are additive; **U-values** are not.
- **Heat of respiration decreases with temperature** — apples at 0 °C respire much less than at 20 °C.
- **Air curtains** reduce infiltration load by up to 80 % if properly designed.
- **Final plant capacity** is selected at the **peak (summer)** ambient condition — never at average annual conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Tutorials on Psychrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Tutorials on Psychrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Psychrometry** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the study of the physical and thermodynamic properties of **moist air** (a binary mixture of dry air and water vapor) and the changes that occur in these properties when moist air undergoes various processes such as heating, cooling, humidification, or dehumidification.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech curriculum (Course: Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Laboratory), the term "Psychrometry" encompasses the measurement, charting, and analytical evaluation of **moist air properties** using:

1. The **Ideal Gas Law** applied independently to dry air and water vapor
2. **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** for the moist air mixture
3. The **Gibbs-Dalton Law** for additive thermodynamic properties
4. The **Psychrometric Chart** (also called the *Carrier Chart* or *Mollier Diagram for Moist Air*) as a graphical solution tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Verbatim):**
> *Psychrometry is the study of the thermodynamic properties of moist air. It involves the determination of properties such as dry bulb temperature, wet bulb temperature, dew point temperature, specific humidity, relative humidity, specific enthalpy, and specific volume, and the analysis of air-conditioning processes using the psychrometric chart.*

## 1.2 The "Why" Behind Psychrometry

Air is never perfectly dry in nature. The atmosphere always contains a certain amount of moisture (water vapor). Since this moisture directly affects:

- **Human thermal comfort** (sweating and evaporative cooling depend on air humidity)
- **Industrial processes** (textile, pharmaceutical, food processing, electronics)
- **Building design** (condensation prevention, mold control)
- **Refrigeration equipment performance** (coil frosting, latent heat loads)

…it becomes essential for an engineer to **quantify** the moisture content and its energy implications. Psychrometry provides the mathematical and graphical framework to do exactly that.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Sponge in a Glass of Water" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sponge Analogy**
> Imagine dry air as a *clear glass of water* and the sponge as the *water vapor* (moisture). The glass can only hold so much sponge before it gets saturated (just like air can only hold a certain maximum amount of moisture at a given temperature). When you "wring out" the sponge, water drips out — this is **condensation**, equivalent to the **dew point temperature** being reached. The amount of water in the sponge corresponds to the **specific humidity (ω)**, and how "stretched" the sponge is corresponds to the **relative humidity (φ)**.

## 1.4 Scope of This Module (KTU Lab Context)

In the **Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Laboratory** (PCAGL507), Module Experiment 2 on "Study and Tutorials on Psychrometry" typically covers:

1. Identification of psychrometric properties on the chart
2. Plotting of various **air-conditioning processes**:
   - Sensible Heating
   - Sensible Cooling
   - Humidification (with and without heating)
   - Dehumidification (with and without cooling)
   - Evaporative Cooling (Adiabatic Saturation)
   - Mixing of Two Air Streams
   - Cooling and Dehumidification (Bypass Factor)
3. Numerical calculations of heat added/removed and moisture added/removed
4. Use of the **sling psychrometer** (dry bulb and wet bulb thermometer) for measuring air properties

## 1.5 Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Atmospheric Pressure** | P_atm | **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** — at sea level |
| **Gas Constant for Dry Air** | R_a | **0.287 kJ/kg·K** |
| **Gas Constant for Water Vapor** | R_v | **0.4615 kJ/kg·K** |
| **Specific Heat of Dry Air** | c_pa | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** |
| **Specific Heat of Water Vapor** | c_pv | **1.88 kJ/kg·K** |
| **Latent Heat at 0°C** | h_fg | **2501 kJ/kg** |
| **Reference State** | h_a, h_v | **0 kJ/kg at 0°C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** All specific enthalpy values are referenced to **0°C** for both dry air (h_a = 0 at 0°C) and water (h_w = 0 at 0°C saturated liquid). The specific heat c_pv is taken as **1.88 kJ/kg·K** in the standard KTU formula sheet — students should **not** use 1.86 in the main calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Composition of Moist Air

Atmospheric (moist) air is a mechanical mixture of:

- **Dry air** — a fixed mixture of Nitrogen (~78%), Oxygen (~21%), Argon (~0.93%), CO₂ (~0.03%), and trace gases. Treated as a single ideal gas.
- **Water vapor** — the variable component that changes with weather, location, and process.

The total pressure of moist air equals the sum of partial pressures (**Dalton's Law**):

$$P_{atm} = P_a + P_v$$

Where:
- $P_{atm}$ = Total atmospheric pressure (Pa or kPa)
- $P_a$ = Partial pressure of dry air
- $P_v$ = Partial pressure of water vapor

Since the sum of mole fractions equals 1:

$$P_a = P_{atm} - P_v$$

## 2.2 Key Psychrometric Properties — Definitions

### 2.2.1 Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)
The temperature of moist air measured by a **standard mercury-in-glass thermometer** with the bulb exposed to the air, shielded from radiation. Denoted by **T** or **t** (in °C) or **T_K** (in K). It is the true thermodynamic temperature of the moist air mixture.

### 2.2.2 Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)
The temperature indicated by a thermometer whose bulb is covered with a wet wick and exposed to a stream of air moving at **≥ 4 m/s**. As water evaporates from the wick, it absorbs latent heat, cooling the bulb. The temperature stabilizes at the **adiabatic saturation temperature** (approximately, for air-water systems). Denoted by **T* or t*** (in °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For the air-water vapor system, WBT ≈ Adiabatic Saturation Temperature (AST). This is a *fortunate coincidence* of properties and is rigorously true for air-water systems but NOT for other moist air systems (e.g., air-alcohol).

### 2.2.3 Dew Point Temperature (DPT)
The temperature at which the water vapor in the air becomes **saturated** (φ = 100%) when cooled at constant pressure and constant specific humidity. Beyond this temperature, condensation begins. Denoted by **T_dp** or **t_dp**.

### 2.2.4 Specific Humidity (ω) — Absolute Humidity — Humidity Ratio
The mass of water vapor per unit mass of **dry air** (NOT per unit mass of moist air).

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Specific humidity is defined **per kg of dry air**, not per kg of moist air. The factor **0.622** comes from the ratio of gas constants: $R_a / R_v = 0.287/0.4615 = 0.622$. The maximum value of ω at sea level at 35°C is approximately **0.0433 kg water/kg dry air**.

### 2.2.5 Relative Humidity (φ or RH)
The ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor to the **saturation pressure** of water vapor at the same DBT:

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T)} \times 100\%$$

Where $P_{vs}(T)$ is the saturation pressure of water at the dry bulb temperature T.

### 2.2.6 Degree of Saturation (μ)
The ratio of specific humidity to the saturation specific humidity at the DBT:

$$\mu = \frac{\omega}{\omega_s}$$

### 2.2.7 Specific Enthalpy of Moist Air (h)
The total enthalpy per kg of **dry air**, referenced to 0°C:

$$h = c_{pa} \cdot T + \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T)$$

Substituting standard KTU values:

$$h = 1.005 \cdot T + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of dry air]}$$

### 2.2.8 Specific Volume of Moist Air (v)
The volume per kg of **dry air**:

$$v = \frac{R_a \cdot T_K}{P_{atm} - P_v} = \frac{R_a \cdot T_K}{P_a}$$

Substituting $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K:

$$v = \frac{0.287 \cdot T_K}{P_{atm} - P_v} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/kg of dry air]}$$

### 2.2.9 Saturation Pressure of Water Vapor — $P_{vs}$
Obtained from **steam tables** at the given temperature. For KTU problems, students are expected to use steam table values directly. An approximate Antoine equation:

$$\ln(P_{vs}) = A - \frac{B}{T_K + C}$$

With constants: A = 16.3872, B = 3885.87, C = -42.98 (giving P in kPa, T in °C). For KTU exams, use steam table data.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dalton's Law | $P_{atm} = P_a + P_v$ | kPa | Total pressure decomposition |
| 2 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | kg/kg da | **Per kg dry air** |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T)}$ | fraction or % | $P_{vs}$ from steam tables |
| 4 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s}$ | fraction | ω_s evaluated at DBT |
| 5 | Specific Enthalpy | $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$ | kJ/kg da | Reference: 0°C |
| 6 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{0.287 \, T_K}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | m³/kg da | T in Kelvin |
| 7 | Dew Point Eq. | $P_v = P_{vs}(T_{dp})$ | kPa | DBT → ω → P_v → T_dp |
| 8 | Density of Moist Air | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{0.287 \, T_K}$ | kg/m³ | Mass basis: dry air |
| 9 | Wet-Bulb Relation (Energy Balance) | $\omega_1 = \dfrac{c_{pa}(T^* - T_{wb}) + \omega_2 \cdot h_{fg2}}{h_{g2} - c_{pw} \cdot T^*}$ | kg/kg da | Used to find ω from WBT |
| 10 | Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) | $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | fraction | Used in coil processes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas above use the "per kg of dry air" convention** — this is the **KTU standard**. Do NOT use "per kg of moist air" basis unless explicitly stated.

## 2.4 The Psychrometric Chart — Visual Solution Tool

The **psychrometric chart** is a graphical plot with:

- **X-axis:** Dry Bulb Temperature (°C) — vertical lines are constant DBT
- **Y-axis:** Specific Humidity ω (kg/kg da) — horizontal lines are constant ω
- **Curved lines:** Constant Relative Humidity (φ) — bottom curve is φ = 100% (saturation)
- **Diagonal lines (upper left to lower right):** Constant Wet Bulb Temperature
- **Diagonal lines (lower left to upper right):** Constant Specific Enthalpy (h)
- **Vertical lines on saturation curve:** Constant Specific Volume (v)

Any **point** on the chart uniquely defines the state of the moist air. Any **line/process** between two points represents an air-conditioning process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Exams:** In numerical problems, the psychrometric chart provides approximate values. For precise answers, students should use the formulas and steam table data. KTU examiners typically allow ±5% tolerance when chart values are used.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **HVAC** | Designing A/C systems, calculating cooling/heating loads |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Clean room humidity control (typically 45 ± 5% RH) |
| **Textile** | Yarn spinning (60–70% RH), weaving (70–80% RH) |
| **Food Storage** | Cold storage humidity to prevent shrinkage/drying |
| **Data Centers** | Strict humidity control (40–55% RH) to prevent static |
| **Agriculture** | Greenhouse climate control for optimal plant growth |
| **Aviation** | Cabin pressurization and humidity control |
| **Hospital OTs** | Sterile environment humidity control |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Humidity Formula

Starting from the ideal gas law applied separately to dry air and water vapor:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

Dividing the second equation by the first (volumes and temperatures cancel):

$$\frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{m_v \cdot R_v}{m_a \cdot R_a}$$

Rearranging to get the mass ratio:

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

Since $\omega = m_v / m_a$ and $R_a / R_v = 0.287/0.4615 = 0.622$:

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a}$$

Substituting $P_a = P_{atm} - P_v$ from Dalton's Law:

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.622 = M_a / M_v = 28.97 / 18.015** (ratio of molar masses of dry air to water vapor). This is the **molecular weight basis**, which is numerically equal to the gas constant ratio.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Specific Enthalpy Formula

Consider 1 kg of **dry air** carrying ω kg of water vapor. The total enthalpy H (relative to 0°C):

$$H = m_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot T + m_v \cdot h_{fg,0} + m_v \cdot c_{pv} \cdot T$$

With $m_a = 1$ kg and $m_v = \omega$ kg:

$$H = c_{pa} \cdot T + \omega \cdot h_{fg,0} + \omega \cdot c_{pv} \cdot T$$

Factoring:

$$H = c_{pa} \cdot T + \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T)$$

Dividing by $m_a = 1$ kg to get enthalpy per kg of dry air:

$$\boxed{h = 1.005 \cdot T + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of dry air]}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Specific Volume Formula

For 1 kg of dry air, applying the ideal gas law:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

With $m_a = 1$ kg, $V = v$, $P_a = P_{atm} - P_v$:

$$v \cdot (P_{atm} - P_v) = R_a \cdot T$$

Solving for v:

$$\boxed{v = \frac{R_a \cdot T}{P_{atm} - P_v} = \frac{0.287 \cdot T_K}{P_{atm} - P_v} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/kg of dry air]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The temperature MUST be in **Kelvin** (T_K = T_°C + 273.15) for the gas constant R_a = 0.287 kJ/kg·K to be dimensionally consistent. A common KTU student error is using Celsius in this formula — **guaranteed 1-mark deduction**.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1: Calculating All Properties Given DBT and WBT

**Problem:** Moist air at **DBT = 35°C** and **WBT = 25°C** at atmospheric pressure **101.325 kPa**. Find: (a) Specific humidity, (b) Relative humidity, (c) Dew point temperature, (d) Specific enthalpy, (e) Specific volume, (f) Density of moist air.

**Given:**
- T = 35°C, T_wb = 25°C
- P_atm = 101.325 kPa
- From steam tables:
  - $P_{vs}(35°C) = 5.628$ kPa
  - $P_{vs}(25°C) = 3.169$ kPa
  - $P_{vs}(22.7°C) ≈ 2.790$ kPa (this is the dew point; we will verify)
  - $h_{g}(25°C) = 2547.2$ kJ/kg
  - $c_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K (water specific heat)

### Step 1: Apply the Wet-Bulb Energy Equation

The general wet-bulb relation (energy balance on adiabatic saturation process):

$$\frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{T_2 - T_1} = -\frac{c_{pa}}{h_{g2} - c_{pw} \cdot T_2}$$

Rearranging to solve for $\omega_1$:

$$\omega_1 = \omega_2 - \frac{(c_{pa} \cdot (T_2 - T_1))}{h_{g2} - c_{pw} \cdot T_2}$$

With point 2 as the saturated state at WBT:
- $T_2 = 25°C$, $\omega_2 = \omega_s(25°C)$
- $T_1 = 35°C$, $\omega_1 = ?$ (this is what we want)

First, calculate $\omega_2$:

$$\omega_2 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{vs}(25°C)}{P_{atm} - P_{vs}(25°C)}$$

$$\omega_2 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{101.325 - 3.169}$$

$$\omega_2 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{98.156}$$

$$\omega_2 = 0.622 \cdot 0.03229 = 0.02008 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

Now apply the wet-bulb equation:

$$\omega_1 = \omega_2 - \frac{c_{pa}(T_2 - T_1)}{h_{g2} - c_{pw} \cdot T_2}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02008 - \frac{1.005 \cdot (25 - 35)}{2547.2 - 4.18 \cdot 25}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02008 - \frac{1.005 \cdot (-10)}{2547.2 - 104.5}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02008 - \frac{-10.05}{2442.7}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02008 + 0.004115$$

$$\boxed{\omega_1 = 0.02420 \text{ kg/kg da}}$$

### Step 2: Find Partial Pressure of Water Vapor

$$P_v = \frac{\omega \cdot P_{atm}}{0.622 + \omega}$$

$$P_v = \frac{0.02420 \cdot 101.325}{0.622 + 0.02420}$$

$$P_v = \frac{2.4521}{0.6462} = 3.794 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Relative Humidity (Part b)

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(35°C)} = \frac{3.794}{5.628} = 0.6741$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 67.41\%}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Dew Point Temperature (Part c)

The dew point is the temperature at which $P_{vs}(T_{dp}) = P_v = 3.794$ kPa.

From steam tables, $P_{vs}(27°C) = 3.567$ kPa and $P_{vs}(28°C) = 3.778$ kPa.

Interpolating: $P_{vs}(27.5°C) ≈ 3.672$ kPa, $P_{vs}(28°C) = 3.778$ kPa.

For $P_v = 3.794$ kPa, this is just above 28°C. Interpolating between 28°C and 29°C:

At 28°C: 3.778 kPa; at 29°C: 4.005 kPa.

$$\Delta T = \frac{3.794 - 3.778}{4.005 - 3.778} = \frac{0.016}{0.227} = 0.07°C$$

$$\boxed{T_{dp} \approx 28.07°C}$$

### Step 5: Calculate Specific Enthalpy (Part d)

$$h = 1.005 \cdot T + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T)$$

$$h = 1.005 \cdot 35 + 0.02420 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 35)$$

$$h = 35.175 + 0.02420 \cdot (2501 + 65.8)$$

$$h = 35.175 + 0.02420 \cdot 2566.8$$

$$h = 35.175 + 62.116$$

$$\boxed{h = 97.29 \text{ kJ/kg da}}$$

### Step 6: Calculate Specific Volume and Density (Parts e, f)

$$v = \frac{0.287 \cdot T_K}{P_{atm} - P_v} = \frac{0.287 \cdot (35 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 3.794}$$

$$v = \frac{0.287 \cdot 308.15}{97.531}$$

$$v = \frac{88.439}{97.531} = 0.9068 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.9068 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}}$$

$$\rho = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{0.9068} = 1.103 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> This density includes **only the dry air mass** per unit volume. The total moist air density would be slightly different if you include the water vapor mass.

## 3.5 Worked Example 2: Adiabatic Mixing of Two Air Streams

**Problem:** Air stream 1: m_a1 = 5 kg/s dry air, T1 = 35°C, ω1 = 0.020 kg/kg da. Air stream 2: m_a2 = 3 kg/s dry air, T2 = 20°C, ω2 = 0.010 kg/kg da. Find the mixed air state (T3, ω3, h3) and total mass flow rate of dry air.

### Step 1: Mass Balance on Dry Air

$$m_{a3} = m_{a1} + m_{a2} = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 2: Mass Balance on Water Vapor

$$m_{a1} \cdot \omega_1 + m_{a2} \cdot \omega_2 = m_{a3} \cdot \omega_3$$

$$5 \cdot 0.020 + 3 \cdot 0.010 = 8 \cdot \omega_3$$

$$0.100 + 0.030 = 8 \cdot \omega_3$$

$$0.130 = 8 \cdot \omega_3$$

$$\boxed{\omega_3 = 0.01625 \text{ kg/kg da}}$$

### Step 3: Energy Balance (Enthalpy Balance)

$$m_{a1} \cdot h_1 + m_{a2} \cdot h_2 = m_{a3} \cdot h_3$$

First, calculate h1 and h2:

$$h_1 = 1.005 \cdot 35 + 0.020 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 35)$$

$$h_1 = 35.175 + 0.020 \cdot 2566.8 = 35.175 + 51.336 = 86.51 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \cdot 20 + 0.010 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 20)$$

$$h_2 = 20.10 + 0.010 \cdot 2538.6 = 20.10 + 25.386 = 45.49 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

Then:

$$5 \cdot 86.51 + 3 \cdot 45.49 = 8 \cdot h_3$$

$$432.55 + 136.47 = 8 \cdot h_3$$

$$569.02 = 8 \cdot h_3$$

$$h_3 = 71.13 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

### Step 4: Find Mixed DBT from h3 and ω3

$$h_3 = 1.005 \cdot T_3 + \omega_3 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_3)$$

$$71.13 = 1.005 \cdot T_3 + 0.01625 \cdot 2501 + 0.01625 \cdot 1.88 \cdot T_3$$

$$71.13 = 1.005 \cdot T_3 + 40.641 + 0.03055 \cdot T_3$$

$$71.13 - 40.641 = (1.005 + 0.03055) \cdot T_3$$

$$30.489 = 1.03555 \cdot T_3$$

$$\boxed{T_3 = 29.44°C}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Psychrometric Processes

| Process | DBT | WBT | DPT | ω | φ | h | Heat Transfer | Mass Transfer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensible Heating** | ↑ | ↑ | — | — | ↓ | ↑ | + (Q_s) | 0 |
| **Sensible Cooling** | ↓ | ↓ | — | — | ↑ | ↓ | − (Q_s) | 0 |
| **Cooling + Dehumidification** | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ → 100% | ↓ | − (Q_s + Q_l) | Condensate out |
| **Heating + Humidification** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ or ↑ | ↑ | + (Q_s) | Steam added |
| **Pure Humidification (evap.)** | ↓ | = | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | = (adiabatic) | 0 | + (Q_l as moisture) |
| **Adiabatic Saturation** | ↓ | = WBT | = | = WBT sat | = 100% | = | 0 | + (Q_l as moisture) |
| **Chemical Dehumidification** | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | + (Q_s + chem.) | Adsorbed out |

Where:
- **Q_s** = Sensible heat
- **Q_l** = Latent heat
- "↑" = increases, "↓" = decreases, "—" = constant, "=" = equal to WBT
