# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 KTU Past Pattern Analysis

In the **PCAGL507 Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Laboratory** (2024 Scheme), Module Experiment 2 — Psychrometry — appears in:

- **Continuous Evaluation (CE) Lab Record:** One full experiment write-up is mandatory
- **Lab Internal Assessment:** Viva voce on psychrometric processes and properties
- **End Semester Lab Exam:** Possibly a numerical problem based on psychrometric chart

The mark distribution typically follows KTU's lab exam pattern: **Total 50 marks = Experiment 25 + Record 5 + Viva 10 + Internal 10**.

## 5.2 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer conceptual questions typical of KTU 3-mark questions. They test definition, identification, and basic understanding.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]** Define the following terms: (i) Specific Humidity, (ii) Relative Humidity, (iii) Dew Point Temperature. State one significance of each in air-conditioning practice.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Specific Humidity (ω):** It is defined as the mass of water vapor in kg contained in 1 kg of **dry air**. Mathematically: $\omega = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$. *Significance:* It quantifies the actual moisture content of air per unit of dry air, which is essential for calculating latent heat loads in cooling coils.

(ii) **Relative Humidity (φ):** It is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor in the air to the saturation pressure of water vapor at the same dry bulb temperature, expressed as a percentage. *Significance:* It is a direct indicator of the **sweating capacity** of air and human thermal comfort (40–60% is the comfort range).

(iii) **Dew Point Temperature (T_dp):** It is the temperature at which the water vapor in the air becomes saturated and condensation begins when the air is cooled at constant pressure. *Significance:* It determines the **surface temperature** below which condensation will form on walls/windows — critical in preventing mold growth and moisture damage.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** Differentiate between Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) and Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT). Why is the difference between DBT and WBT called the "wet bulb depression"?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) | Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Measurement** | Standard thermometer with dry bulb | Thermometer with bulb covered by wet wick |
| **Air motion effect** | Independent of air motion | Requires ≥ 4 m/s air velocity for accurate reading |
| **Physical meaning** | True thermodynamic temperature of moist air | Temperature reached when air becomes saturated by evaporation |
| **Value range** | Always ≥ WBT (for unsaturated air) | Always ≤ DBT (for unsaturated air) |
| **Energy involved** | No phase change | Latent heat of vaporization absorbed from air |

**Wet Bulb Depression:** The difference (DBT − WBT) is called the wet bulb depression. It is **directly proportional to the dryness** of the air:
- A **large depression** indicates **dry air** (low relative humidity) — more evaporation possible
- A **small depression** indicates **moist air** (high relative humidity) — little evaporation possible
- **Zero depression** means the air is **saturated** (φ = 100%) and WBT = DBT

---

## 5.3 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 14-mark lab questions typically have two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The model answers below show full step-by-step working, exactly as expected in KTU valuation scripts.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q.A (a)** A sling psychrometer reads DBT = 40°C and WBT = 28°C in an air-conditioned auditorium. Atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. Using steam table data, determine: (i) Specific humidity, (ii) Relative humidity, (iii) Dew point temperature, (iv) Specific enthalpy, (v) Specific volume. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** T = 40°C, T_wb = 28°C, P_atm = 101.325 kPa.

**Steam Table Data:**
- $P_{vs}(40°C) = 7.384$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(28°C) = 3.778$ kPa
- $h_{g}(28°C) = 2550.6$ kJ/kg

**Step 1: Specific Humidity at WBT (Saturated)**

$$\omega^* = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{vs}(28°C)}{P_{atm} - P_{vs}(28°C)} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.778}{101.325 - 3.778}$$

$$\omega^* = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.778}{97.547} = 0.622 \cdot 0.03873 = 0.02409 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 2: Apply Wet Bulb Equation to Find ω**

$$\omega_1 = \omega^* - \frac{c_{pa}(T^* - T_1)}{h_{g2} - c_{pw} \cdot T^*}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02409 - \frac{1.005 \cdot (28 - 40)}{2550.6 - 4.18 \cdot 28}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02409 - \frac{1.005 \cdot (-12)}{2550.6 - 117.04}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02409 - \frac{-12.06}{2433.56}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.02409 + 0.004956 = 0.02905 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3: Partial Pressure**

$$P_v = \frac{\omega \cdot P_{atm}}{0.622 + \omega} = \frac{0.02905 \cdot 101.325}{0.622 + 0.02905} = \frac{2.944}{0.6511} = 4.521 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4: Relative Humidity**

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(40°C)} = \frac{4.521}{7.384} = 0.6123 = 61.23\%$$

**Step 5: Dew Point Temperature** — Find T where $P_{vs} = 4.521$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(31°C) = 4.491$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(32°C) = 4.759$ kPa
- Interpolating: $T_{dp} = 31 + (4.521 - 4.491)/(4.759 - 4.491) = 31 + 0.030/0.268 = 31.11°C$

**Step 6: Specific Enthalpy**

$$h = 1.005 \cdot 40 + 0.02905 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 40) = 40.20 + 0.02905 \cdot 2576.2 = 40.20 + 74.84 = 115.04 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Step 7: Specific Volume**

$$v = \frac{0.287 \cdot (40 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 4.521} = \frac{0.287 \cdot 313.15}{96.804} = \frac{89.864}{96.804} = 0.9283 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

**Final Answer Summary (Box All 5 Values):**
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| ω | **0.02905 kg/kg da** |
| φ | **61.23%** |
| T_dp | **31.11°C** |
| h | **115.04 kJ/kg da** |
| v | **0.9283 m³/kg da** |

---

**Q.A (b)** A 30 m³ room contains air at 30°C DBT and 60% RH. It is desired to condition this air to 22°C DBT and 50% RH by cooling and dehumidification. Calculate: (i) Mass of dry air in the room, (ii) Heat removed by the cooling coil, (iii) Moisture condensed out. Use P_atm = 101.325 kPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Steam Table Data:**
- $P_{vs}(30°C) = 4.246$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(22°C) = 2.645$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(11.5°C) = 1.354$ kPa (for state 2 final)

**State 1 (Before): T1 = 30°C, φ1 = 60%**

$$P_{v1} = 0.60 \cdot 4.246 = 2.548 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.548}{101.325 - 2.548} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.548}{98.777} = 0.01605 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$h_1 = 1.005 \cdot 30 + 0.01605 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 30) = 30.15 + 0.01605 \cdot 2557.4 = 30.15 + 41.05 = 71.20 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{0.287 \cdot 303.15}{98.777} = \frac{87.004}{98.777} = 0.8808 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

**State 2 (After): T2 = 22°C, φ2 = 50%**

$$P_{v2} = 0.50 \cdot 2.645 = 1.3225 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\omega_2 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.3225}{101.325 - 1.3225} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.3225}{100.003} = 0.622 \cdot 0.01322 = 0.008226 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \cdot 22 + 0.008226 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 22) = 22.11 + 0.008226 \cdot 2542.36 = 22.11 + 20.91 = 43.02 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Part (i): Mass of Dry Air**

$$m_a = \frac{V}{v_1} = \frac{30}{0.8808} = 34.06 \text{ kg of dry air}$$

**Part (ii): Heat Removed by Coil**

The total heat removed per kg of dry air:

$$\Delta h = h_1 - h_2 = 71.20 - 43.02 = 28.18 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

Total heat removed:

$$Q = m_a \cdot \Delta h = 34.06 \cdot 28.18 = 959.8 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 960 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Part (iii): Moisture Condensed**

$$\Delta \omega = \omega_1 - \omega_2 = 0.01605 - 0.008226 = 0.007824 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$m_{condensed} = m_a \cdot \Delta \omega = 34.06 \cdot 0.007824 = 0.2665 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{condensed} \approx 0.2665 \text{ kg} = 266.5 \text{ grams}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q.B (a)** Two air streams are mixed adiabatically to supply air to a manufacturing clean room. Stream A: m_a1 = 4 kg/s dry air, T1 = 38°C, φ1 = 40%. Stream B: m_a2 = 6 kg/s dry air, T2 = 18°C, φ2 = 80%. Calculate: (i) Specific humidity and enthalpy of each stream, (ii) Mass flow rate of dry air in the mixture, (iii) Specific humidity, enthalpy, and DBT of the mixed air. Use P_atm = 101.325 kPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Steam Table Data:**
- $P_{vs}(38°C) = 6.629$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(18°C) = 2.065$ kPa

**Part (i): Properties of Each Stream**

**Stream A: T1 = 38°C, φ1 = 40%**

$$P_{v1} = 0.40 \cdot 6.629 = 2.652 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.652}{101.325 - 2.652} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.652}{98.673} = 0.622 \cdot 0.02687 = 0.01672 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$h_1 = 1.005 \cdot 38 + 0.01672 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 38) = 38.19 + 0.01672 \cdot 2572.44 = 38.19 + 43.01 = 81.20 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Stream B: T2 = 18°C, φ2 = 80%**

$$P_{v2} = 0.80 \cdot 2.065 = 1.652 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\omega_2 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.652}{101.325 - 1.652} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.652}{99.673} = 0.622 \cdot 0.01658 = 0.01031 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \cdot 18 + 0.01031 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 18) = 18.09 + 0.01031 \cdot 2534.84 = 18.09 + 26.13 = 44.22 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Part (ii): Mass Flow Rate of Mixed Air**

$$m_{a3} = m_{a1} + m_{a2} = 4 + 6 = 10 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Part (iii): Mixed Air Properties**

Mass balance on water vapor:

$$m_{a1} \omega_1 + m_{a2} \omega_2 = m_{a3} \omega_3$$

$$4 \cdot 0.01672 + 6 \cdot 0.01031 = 10 \cdot \omega_3$$

$$0.06688 + 0.06186 = 10 \cdot \omega_3$$

$$0.12874 = 10 \cdot \omega_3 \Rightarrow \omega_3 = 0.01287 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

Energy balance:

$$m_{a1} h_1 + m_{a2} h_2 = m_{a3} h_3$$

$$4 \cdot 81.20 + 6 \cdot 44.22 = 10 \cdot h_3$$

$$324.80 + 265.32 = 10 \cdot h_3$$

$$590.12 = 10 \cdot h_3 \Rightarrow h_3 = 59.01 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

Now find T3 from h3 and ω3:

$$59.01 = 1.005 \cdot T_3 + 0.01287 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_3)$$

$$59.01 = 1.005 \cdot T_3 + 32.188 + 0.02420 \cdot T_3$$

$$59.01 - 32.188 = (1.005 + 0.02420) \cdot T_3$$

$$26.822 = 1.0292 \cdot T_3 \Rightarrow T_3 = 26.06°C$$

**Final Summary Box:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Stream A: ω1 | **0.01672 kg/kg da** |
| Stream A: h1 | **81.20 kJ/kg da** |
| Stream B: ω2 | **0.01031 kg/kg da** |
| Stream B: h2 | **44.22 kJ/kg da** |
| Mixed m_a3 | **10 kg/s** |
| Mixed ω3 | **0.01287 kg/kg da** |
| Mixed h3 | **59.01 kJ/kg da** |
| Mixed T3 | **26.06°C** |

---

**Q.B (b)** Air enters a cooling coil at 32°C DBT, 24°C WBT, and leaves at 18°C DBT, 17°C WBT. The mass flow rate of dry air is 2 kg/s. Atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. Calculate: (i) Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) of the coil, (ii) Bypass Factor (BF), (iii) Total heat removed, (iv) Sensible Heat Factor (SHF), (v) Latent heat removed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Steam Table Data:**
- $P_{vs}(32°C) = 4.759$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(18°C) = 2.065$ kPa
- $P_{vs}(24°C) = 2.985$ kPa (for WBT state 1)
- $P_{vs}(17°C) = 1.938$ kPa (for WBT state 2)
- $h_g(24°C) = 2545.4$ kJ/kg
- $h_g(17°C) = 2533.4$ kJ/kg

**State 1: T1 = 32°C, WBT1 = 24°C**

$$\omega_1^* = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.985}{98.34} = 0.01889 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.01889 - \frac{1.005 \cdot (24 - 32)}{2545.4 - 4.18 \cdot 24} = 0.01889 - \frac{-8.04}{2445.08} = 0.01889 + 0.003288 = 0.02218 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$h_1 = 1.005 \cdot 32 + 0.02218 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 32) = 32.16 + 0.02218 \cdot 2561.16 = 32.16 + 56.81 = 88.97 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**State 2: T2 = 18°C, WBT2 = 17°C**

$$\omega_2^* = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.938}{99.387} = 0.01213 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$\omega_2 = 0.01213 - \frac{1.005 \cdot (17 - 18)}{2533.4 - 4.18 \cdot 17} = 0.01213 - \frac{-1.005}{2462.34} = 0.01213 + 0.000408 = 0.01254 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \cdot 18 + 0.01254 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 18) = 18.09 + 0.01254 \cdot 2534.84 = 18.09 + 31.79 = 49.88 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Part (i): Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)**

ADP is the state point on the **saturation curve** through which the process line (extended) passes. The ADP is determined by extending the line between state 1 and state 2 to the saturation curve.

The Bypass Factor line equation: The process 1→2 extended to saturation.

Since the cooling process is a straight line on the psychrometric chart, we can find ADP by extrapolation. The line passes through (T1, ω1) and (T2, ω2). The slope:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{0.01254 - 0.02218}{18 - 32} = \frac{-0.00964}{-14} = 0.000689 \text{ kg/kg per °C}$$

To find ADP: extrapolate to ω_ADP such that state is saturated. Trial:

Try T_ADP = 13°C: $P_{vs}(13°C) = 1.510$ kPa
$\omega_{sat}(13°C) = 0.622 \cdot 1.510 / 99.815 = 0.009410$ kg/kg da

Line equation: $\omega = \omega_1 + \text{slope} \cdot (T - T_1) = 0.02218 + 0.000689 \cdot (T - 32)$

At T = 13°C: $\omega_{line} = 0.02218 + 0.000689 \cdot (-19) = 0.02218 - 0.01309 = 0.00909$ kg/kg da

This is close to but less than 0.00941. Try T_ADP = 13.5°C:
$P_{vs}(13.5°C) ≈ 1.563$ kPa
$\omega_{sat}(13.5°C) = 0.622 \cdot 1.563 / 99.762 = 0.009747$ kg/kg da
$\omega_{line}(13.5°C) = 0.02218 + 0.000689 \cdot (-18.5) = 0.02218 - 0.01275 = 0.00943$ kg/kg da

Close to 0.00975. By further refinement: **ADP ≈ 13.3°C**

**Part (ii): Bypass Factor (BF)**

$$BF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \frac{18 - 13.3}{32 - 13.3} = \frac{4.7}{18.7} = 0.2513$$

**Part (iii): Total Heat Removed**

$$Q_{total} = m_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2) = 2 \cdot (88.97 - 49.88) = 2 \cdot 39.09 = 78.18 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (iv): Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)**

Sensible heat removed per kg dry air:
$$q_s = c_{pa} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 1.005 \cdot (32 - 18) = 1.005 \cdot 14 = 14.07 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

Total heat removed per kg:
$$q_t = h_1 - h_2 = 39.09 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$SHF = \frac{q_s}{q_t} = \frac{14.07}{39.09} = 0.3600$$

**Part (v): Latent Heat Removed**

$$q_l = q_t - q_s = 39.09 - 14.07 = 25.02 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$Q_l = m_a \cdot q_l = 2 \cdot 25.02 = 50.04 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert T to Kelvin** in the specific volume formula — guaranteed **−1 mark**
> 2. **Using c_pv = 1.86** instead of KTU standard **1.88** in enthalpy formula — **−0.5 mark**
> 3. **Confusing the basis** — specific humidity is per kg of **dry air**, not moist air — conceptual mark loss
> 4. **Wrong factor in wet-bulb equation** — students often forget the c_pw × T* term in denominator
> 5. **Not stating units clearly** in final answer — KTU examiners specifically deduct for missing units
> 6. **ADP determination** — students often give BF without proper ADP calculation
> 7. **Mixing process** — using mass fractions incorrectly; the lever rule is **mass ratio of dry air**, not moist air

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

### ✅ Core Definition Triad
- [ ] **Psychrometry** = Study of moist air (dry air + water vapor) thermodynamics
- [ ] **Moist Air** = Ideal gas mixture obeying Dalton's Law
- [ ] **Reference state** for enthalpy = 0°C for both dry air and water

### ✅ The 8 Master Properties
- [ ] **DBT** — True temperature (T_°C or T_K)
- [ ] **WBT** — Adiabatic saturation temperature
- [ ] **DPT** — Saturation temperature at constant P and ω
- [ ] **ω** — Specific humidity = 0.622 × P_v / (P_atm − P_v)
- [ ] **φ** — Relative humidity = P_v / P_vs(T) × 100%
- [ ] **h** — Enthalpy = 1.005T + ω(2501 + 1.88T) kJ/kg da
- [ ] **v** — Specific volume = 0.287 T_K / (P_atm − P_v) m³/kg da
- [ ] **μ** — Degree of saturation = ω / ω_s

### ✅ The 3 Golden Numerical Rules
1. **Basis:** ALL quantities are per **kg of dry air** unless stated otherwise
2. **Temperature in K:** Always convert to Kelvin in v = 0.287 T / P formula
3. **Steam tables are king:** P_vs values must come from steam tables, not from memory

### ✅ The 7 Psychrometric Processes (Memorize Direction of Change)
1. Sensible Heating — DBT↑, ω→, h↑
2. Sensible Cooling — DBT↓, ω→, h↓
3. Cooling + Dehumidification — DBT↓, ω↓, h↓
4. Heating + Humidification — DBT↑, ω↑, h↑
5. Evaporative Cooling — DBT↓, ω↑, h→ (adiabatic)
6. Chemical Dehumidification — DBT↑, ω↓, h↑
7. Adiabatic Mixing — lies on straight line between two states, weighted by m_a

### ✅ The 5 Formula Patterns
1. **Find ω from DBT, WBT:** Use wet-bulb energy equation
2. **Find ω from DBT, RH:** Use $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \phi P_{vs}/(P_{atm} - \phi P_{vs})$
3. **Find T_dp from any state:** $P_v = \omega P_{atm}/(0.622 + \omega)$ then look up steam table
4. **Find h:** Direct formula with T and ω
5. **Find mixing state:** Mass and energy balance (linear interpolation on chart)

### ✅ Key Numerical Sensitivities
- ω range: **0 to ~0.045** kg/kg da (sea level)
- h range: **0 to ~200** kJ/kg da (typical A/C conditions)
- v range: **0.85 to 0.95** m³/kg da (typical)
- φ range: **0 to 100%**

### ✅ Standard Reference Values (Memorize These)
- R_a = 0.287 kJ/kg·K
- R_v = 0.4615 kJ/kg·K
- c_pa = 1.005 kJ/kg·K
- c_pv = 1.88 kJ/kg·K
- h_fg at 0°C = 2501 kJ/kg
- P_atm = 101.325 kPa
- 0.622 = M_a / M_v = R_a / R_v

### ✅ Common Exam Traps
- DBT = WBT → saturated air (φ = 100%)
- φ doesn't change during heating/cooling without moisture addition
- WBT ≈ AST (only for air-water, not for other systems)
- BF + Contact Factor = 1
- SHF + LHF = 1 (where LHF = Latent Heat Factor)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Humidity of Air Using Sling Psychrometer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Humidity of Air Using Sling Psychrometer

# Determination of Humidity of Air Using Sling Psychrometer

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Sling Psychrometer** (also called a **Whirling Hygrometer** or **Whirling Psychrometer**) is a hand-operated, manually rotated instrument consisting of two identical mercury-in-glass thermometers mounted side-by-side on a pivoted frame with a handle, used to determine the **psychrometric properties of moist air** — specifically the **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)**, **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)**, **Relative Humidity (φ)**, and **Specific Humidity (ω)** of a given air sample by exploiting the **psychrometric principle of evaporative cooling**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme RAC Lab syllabus, this experiment is a **foundational psychrometric measurement experiment** categorized under **Module 2 (Psychrometric Studies and Air Measurement)**, and serves as the gateway experiment before moving to **Refrigeration Cycle Testing** and **Air-Conditioning Apparatus Testing**.

The instrument's working relies on the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and the principle of **Mass and Heat Transfer** across the gas-liquid interface of the wet muslin wick wrapped over the bulb of one thermometer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Sweaty Bottle Trick":**
>
> Imagine a hot summer day. You take two identical cold-drink bottles out of the fridge. You wrap one in a wet cloth (just like the wet-bulb thermometer) and leave the other dry. Now you swing both bottles through the air rapidly (just like slinging the psychrometer). After a minute, which bottle feels colder? The **wet one** — because the water on its surface **evaporates into the air**, and evaporation **absorbs latent heat** from the bottle, cooling it down.
>
> The **dry bottle** simply shows the **actual air temperature** = **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)**.
>
> The **wet bottle** shows a lower temperature because evaporative cooling is pulling heat away = **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)**.
>
> The **difference** between the two readings (called **Wet Bulb Depression** = DBT − WBT) tells you **how dry the air is**:
> - If the air is already saturated (very humid, like during rain), water cannot evaporate much, so both thermometers read **almost the same** → small depression.
> - If the air is dry (like a desert afternoon), water evaporates rapidly, pulling lots of heat away, so the wet bulb reads **much lower** → large depression.

This simple everyday observation is **precisely the engineering principle** behind the Sling Psychrometer.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard References Used in this Experiment:**
> - **Atmospheric Pressure (P)** = **1.01325 bar** = **101.325 kPa** = **760 mm of Hg** (assumed standard laboratory condition)
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K**
> - **Molecular weight of dry air (Ma)** = **28.97 kg/kmol**
> - **Molecular weight of water vapor (Mv)** = **18.02 kg/kmol**
> - **Ratio Mv/Ma** = **0.622** (this number appears in every psychrometric formula)
> - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 0 °C** = **2501 kJ/kg**
> - **Latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C** = **2257 kJ/kg**
> - **Standard lab temperature range for this experiment** = **20 °C to 45 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Reading

The sling psychrometer works on **THREE simultaneous physical phenomena**:

1. **Sensible Heat Transfer (Convection):**
   The surrounding air (at DBT) gives up sensible heat to the water on the wet wick, raising the water temperature toward air temperature.

2. **Latent Heat Transfer (Evaporation):**
   The water on the wick evaporates into the air stream, absorbing its latent heat of vaporization from the wick itself, thereby lowering the wick's temperature.

3. **Steady-State Thermal Equilibrium:**
   After ~1–2 minutes of continuous whirling, an equilibrium is reached where the rate of sensible heat gained by the wick **equals** the rate of latent heat lost by evaporation. At this point, the wet-bulb thermometer reading stabilizes at the **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Whirl the Psychrometer?**
> Whirling at ~5 revolutions/second forces a **high-velocity air stream** over the wick. This:
> - Maximizes the evaporation rate (faster equilibrium)
> - Prevents the boundary layer of saturated air from stagnating around the wick
> - Brings fresh, unsaturated air continuously in contact with the wet surface
> - Ensures that the WBT reading is **true and stable**, not contaminated by room air

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for University Exam)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for the Sling Psychrometer experiment. Every formula listed is directly used in the KTU 2024 Scheme lab viva, calculations, and the ESE question paper for RAC Lab.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Wet Bulb Depression (WBD) | $WBD = DBT - WBT$ | °C or K | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | Saturation Pressure of Water Vapor (Carrier's Eqn.) | $\ln(P_s) = A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}$ | mm Hg or kPa | T in °C; A, B, C are constants |
| 3 | Carrier's Constants | $A = 18.6686$, $B = 4030.183$, $C = 235.0$ | dimensionless | Valid 0 °C to 200 °C |
| 4 | Partial Pressure of Water Vapor (at WBT) | $P_v = P_{s,WBT} - (P \cdot WBD) \cdot \left[\dfrac{A_{psy}}{280}\right]$ | kPa | P = total pressure |
| 5 | Psychrometric Constant (Approx.) | $A_{psy} = 0.000367$ | 1/°C | For standard conditions |
| 6 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{s,DBT}}$ | decimal (×100 for %) | $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$ |
| 7 | Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio) | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg water/kg dry air | Mass of water per mass of dry air |
| 8 | Specific Volume of Moist Air | $v = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T}{P - P_v}$ | m³/kg dry air | $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | Enthalpy of Moist Air | $h = 1.005 \cdot T + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T)$ | kJ/kg dry air | T in °C |
| 10 | Dew Point Temperature | $P_v = P_{s,DP}$ (use chart) | °C | Iterative from psychrometric chart |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipes in Formulas:** In the Carrier's equation and the psychrometric constant formula above, the notation $(P \cdot WBD)$ means **multiplication of total pressure with wet-bulb depression**, NOT the absolute value bars that would be represented using \vert or \mid in LaTeX. Be careful during exam writing.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is the Sling Psychrometer actually used in industry?**
>
> 1. **HVAC System Commissioning** — to verify RH inside AC ducts, clean rooms, and operating theaters.
> 2. **Textile & Paper Mills** — where humidity directly affects yarn tensile strength and paper dimensional stability.
> 3. **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** — strict humidity control is mandated by **USFDA** and **WHO GMP**.
> 4. **Food Storage Warehouses** — grains, spices, and dried foods spoil at high humidity.
> 5. **Museum & Archival Conservation** — paintings and ancient manuscripts require 50 ± 5 % RH.
> 6. **Meteorological Stations** — the official Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) uses a Stevenson-screen-mounted sling psychrometer at 1.2 m height above ground.
> 7. **Calibration Standard** — the sling psychrometer is the **calibration reference** for all electronic humidity sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Carrier's Equation for Saturation Pressure

The **Antoine-type saturation pressure equation** used universally in psychrometric calculations is the **Carrier's Equation**. It relates the saturation pressure of water vapor $P_s$ (in mm Hg) to the temperature $T$ (in °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier's Equation (Empirical, Valid from 0 °C to 200 °C):**
>
> $$\ln(P_s) = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$$
>
> where $A = 18.6686$, $B = 4030.183$, $C = 235.0$

**Step-by-step derivation logic (since this is empirical, we show the form and the constants derivation):**

**Step 1:** Carrier's original empirical observation (1911) was that plotting $\ln(P_s)$ vs $1/T$ (Clausius-Clapeyron plot) gives a near-straight line for water, with a slight curvature over wide temperature ranges.

**Step 2:** To account for this curvature, Carrier introduced a **third constant C** in the denominator, transforming the simple Clausius-Clapeyron line into a **3-parameter Antoine equation**.

**Step 3:** The constants A, B, C were obtained by **non-linear regression** against the IAPWS (International Association for the Properties of Water and Steam) steam tables.

**Step 4:** Solving the equation for $P_s$:

$$
P_s = \exp\left(A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Unit conversion — if $P_s$ in mm Hg, multiply by $133.322$ to convert to Pa, or divide by $7.5006$ to convert from mm Hg to kPa.

$$
P_s \text{ (kPa)} = \dfrac{P_s \text{ (mm Hg)}}{7.5006}
$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Partial Pressure of Water Vapor ($P_v$)

**Starting from the steady-state energy balance** on the wet-bulb wick:

**At equilibrium:**

$$
\text{Heat gained by wick from air (convection)} = \text{Heat lost by wick (evaporation)}
$$

**Let:**
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization at WBT (kJ/kg)
- $m_w$ = mass of water evaporated per unit time
- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (kW/m²·K)
- $A_w$ = wick surface area (m²)
- $(T_{DBT} - T_{WBT})$ = wet-bulb depression

**Energy balance:**

$$
h_c \cdot A_w \cdot (T_{DBT} - T_{WBT}) = m_w \cdot h_{fg}
$$

**Mass transfer analogy** (using the Lewis relation, $\dfrac{h_c}{k_y \cdot c_s} = 1$, where $k_y$ is the mass transfer coefficient and $c_s$ is humid heat):

$$
m_w = k_y \cdot A_w \cdot (P_{s,WBT} - P_v)
$$

**Substituting and rearranging:**

$$
P_v = P_{s,WBT} - \dfrac{c_s \cdot (P - P_v)}{h_{fg} \cdot L_e^{2/3}} \cdot (T_{DBT} - T_{WBT})
$$

**For the common engineering approximation** (assuming $P_v \ll P$, and Lewis number $L_e = 1$):

$$
P_v = P_{s,WBT} - A_{psy} \cdot P \cdot (T_{DBT} - T_{WBT})
$$

where the **psychrometric constant** is:

$$
A_{psy} = \dfrac{c_s \cdot M_a}{L_e^{2/3} \cdot M_v \cdot h_{fg}} \cdot \text{unit conversion factor}
$$

For air-water vapor at standard conditions:

$$
A_{psy} \approx 0.000367 \; \text{per °C (or 1/K)}
$$

**Final form used in KTU exams:**

$$
P_v = P_{s,WBT} - \left( P \times WBD \right) \times \dfrac{0.000367 \cdot 280}{280} = P_{s,WBT} - P \cdot WBD \cdot 0.000367
$$

(Simplified for $P = 1$ atm)

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Specific Humidity ($\omega$)

**Definition:** Specific humidity = mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air.

**Ideal gas assumption** for both dry air and water vapor:

$$
P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T \quad \text{and} \quad P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T
$$

**Dividing the two equations:**

$$
\dfrac{m_v}{m_a} = \dfrac{P_v \cdot R_a}{P_a \cdot R_v}
$$

**Using** $R = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{M}$, where $\bar{R}$ = universal gas constant:

$$
\dfrac{R_a}{R_v} = \dfrac{M_v}{M_a} = \dfrac{18.02}{28.97} = 0.622
$$

**Substituting $P_a = P - P_v$:**

$$
\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{kg water vapor}}{\text{kg dry air}} \right]
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Question Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:**
> - DBT = 35 °C
> - WBT = 25 °C
> - Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa
>
> **Find:** Relative Humidity (φ), Specific Humidity (ω), Dew Point (DP), Enthalpy (h)

**Step 1:** Compute Wet Bulb Depression.

$$
WBD = DBT - WBT = 35 - 25 = 10 \; °C
$$

**Step 2:** Saturation pressure at WBT = 25 °C (from steam tables or Carrier's equation).

$$
\ln(P_{s,25}) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{25 + 235}
$$

$$
\ln(P_{s,25}) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{260} = 18.6686 - 15.5007 = 3.1679
$$

$$
P_{s,25} = e^{3.1679} = 23.76 \; \text{mm Hg} = \dfrac{23.76}{7.5006} = 3.167 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Saturation pressure at DBT = 35 °C.

$$
\ln(P_{s,35}) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{35 + 235} = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{270}
$$

$$
\ln(P_{s,35}) = 18.6686 - 14.926 = 3.7426
$$

$$
P_{s,35} = e^{3.7426} = 42.18 \; \text{mm Hg} = 5.624 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Partial pressure of water vapor.

$$
P_v = P_{s,WBT} - P \cdot WBD \cdot 0.000367
$$

$$
P_v = 3.167 - 101.325 \times 10 \times 0.000367
$$

$$
P_v = 3.167 - 0.372 = 2.795 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Relative Humidity.

$$
\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{s,DBT}} = \dfrac{2.795}{5.624} = 0.4970 = 49.70 \; \%
$$

**Step 6:** Specific Humidity.

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.795}{101.325 - 2.795} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.795}{98.53} = 0.01764 \; \text{kg/kg dry air}
$$

**Step 7:** Enthalpy.

$$
h = 1.005 \times 35 + 0.01764 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 35)
$$

$$
h = 35.175 + 0.01764 \times 2566.8 = 35.175 + 45.28 = 80.45 \; \text{kJ/kg dry air}
$$

**Step 8:** Dew Point — find T such that $P_{s,T} = P_v = 2.795$ kPa.

From steam tables: $P_{s} = 2.795$ kPa corresponds to $T_{DP} \approx 22.6 \; °C$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Observation Table Template (For Lab Record)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU External Lab Examiner will check that your observation table is properly formatted. Use this template:**

| Sl. No. | Trial | DBT (°C) | WBT (°C) | WBD (°C) | Barometric Pressure (mm Hg) |
|---------|-------|----------|----------|----------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | After 1 min whirling | 35.0 | 26.5 | 8.5 | 760 |
| 2 | After 2 min whirling | 35.0 | 25.5 | 9.5 | 760 |
| 3 | After 3 min whirling (stable) | 35.0 | 25.0 | 10.0 | 760 |
| 4 | After 4 min whirling (verify) | 35.0 | 25.0 | 10.0 | 760 |

**Stop whirling when two consecutive readings match within ±0.5 °C.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete measurement chain** of the Sling Psychrometer — from the physical apparatus through the heat-mass transfer process to the final calculated psychrometric properties. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric naming rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Atmospheric Air Sample] --> A2[Entering Inlet of Sling Frame]
    A2 --> A3[Impact on Dry Bulb Thermometer]
    A3 --> A4[Reads DBT in degrees C]
    A2 --> A5[Impact on Wet Bulb Thermometer Wick]
    A5 --> A6[Water Evaporates from Muslin Cloth]
    A6 --> A7[Absorbs Latent Heat of Vaporization]
    A7 --> A8[Wick Temperature Falls]
    A8 --> A9[Steady State WBT Reached]
    A4 --> A10[Log DBT Value]
    A9 --> A11[Log WBT Value]
    A10 --> A12[Compute WBD equals DBT minus WBT]
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Lookup Ps at WBT from Steam Table]
    A12 --> A14[Lookup Ps at DBT from Steam Table]
    A13 --> A15[Compute Partial Pressure Pv]
    A14 --> A16[Compute Relative Humidity Phi]
    A15 --> A16
    A15 --> A17[Compute Specific Humidity Omega]
    A16 --> A18[Determine Dew Point from Psychrometric Chart]
    A17 --> A19[Compute Enthalpy h of Moist Air]
    A18 --> A20[Final Psychrometric State Reported]
    A19 --> A20
```

### 4.2 Apparatus Component Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Handle with Swivel Pivot] --> B2[Rotating Frame]
    B2 --> B3[Mounting Bracket Left]
    B2 --> B4[Mounting Bracket Right]
    B3 --> B5[Mercury Thermometer Dry Bulb]
    B3 --> B6[Bulb Exposed Directly to Air]
    B4 --> B7[Mercury Thermometer Wet Bulb]
    B4 --> B8[Muslin Cloth Wick Wrapped Around Bulb]
    B8 --> B9[Water Reservoir Wick Dipping Tube]
    B9 --> B10[Distilled Water Supply]
    B5 --> B11[DBT Scale 0 to 50 C]
    B7 --> B12[WBT Scale 0 to 50 C]
    B11 --> B13[Output Reading 1]
    B12 --> B14[Output Reading 2]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of the Wet Bulb (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Glass Thermometer Stem] --> C2[Mercury Reservoir Bulb]
    C2 --> C3[Thin Layer of Water on Glass]
    C3 --> C4[Muslin Cloth Wicking Fabric]
    C4 --> C5[Saturated Boundary Air Layer]
    C5 --> C6[Whirling Air Stream at 5 m per s]
    C6 --> C7[DBT Stream of Unaffected Air]
    C6 --> C8[Evaporation Mass Transfer Path]
    C8 --> C9[Latent Heat Absorbed from C2]
```

### 4.4 Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Start of Experiment] --> D2[Inspect Apparatus and Check Cleanliness]
    D2 --> D3[Soak Muslin Wick in Distilled Water]
    D3 --> D4[Mount Wick Securely on Wet Bulb]
    D4 --> D5[Hold Sling by Handle]
    D5 --> D6[Whirl at 4 to 5 rev per s for 60 s]
    D6 --> D7[Stop and Record DBT and WBT]
    D7 --> D8{Difference Between Two Successive WBT readings less than 0.5 C}
    D8 -- No --> D6
    D8 -- Yes --> D9[Record Final Stable Values]
    D9 --> D10[Note Barometric Pressure from Lab Barometer]
    D10 --> D11[Calculate WBD]
    D11 --> D12[Calculate Ps at WBT using Carriers Equation]
    D12 --> D13[Calculate Pv]
    D13 --> D14[Calculate Relative Humidity]
    D14 --> D15[Calculate Specific Humidity]
    D15 --> D16[Determine Dew Point from Chart]
    D16 --> D17[Calculate Enthalpy]
    D17 --> D18[End of Experiment and Reporting]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) and Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT). Why is a Sling Psychrometer whirled before taking a reading?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **DBT (1 mark):** The temperature of air measured by a standard mercury-in-glass thermometer whose bulb is **dry** and exposed directly to the air sample. It represents the **true sensible temperature** of the air.
- **WBT (1 mark):** The temperature measured by an identical thermometer whose bulb is covered with a **muslin cloth wick kept moist with distilled water**. The water evaporates into the surrounding unsaturated air, drawing latent heat from the bulb and lowering its temperature.
- **Why whirled? (1 mark):** Whirling at ~5 rev/s forces a **high-velocity air stream** over the wick, ensuring continuous removal of the saturated air boundary layer. This speeds up the attainment of **thermal equilibrium** and gives a **stable, accurate WBT reading**. Without whirling, the boundary layer stagnates and the reading becomes erroneous (too high).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is Wet Bulb Depression? How does its magnitude indicate the relative humidity of air?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Wet Bulb Depression is the arithmetic difference between the DBT and the WBT of a given air sample, expressed in °C or K.
  $$WBD = DBT - WBT$$
- **WBD indicates humidity (2 marks):** The WBD is **inversely related** to the relative humidity of air. **If WBD is large** (large difference between DBT and WBT), it means the air is **dry** (low RH), because more water can evaporate and pull more heat away from the wick. **If WBD is small or zero**, it means the air is **saturated or near-saturated** (high RH, near 100%), because little water can evaporate.
  - WBD = 0 → RH = 100% (saturated air, fog/rain)
  - WBD = 1–3 °C → RH = 70–90% (humid)
  - WBD = 4–6 °C → RH = 50–70% (comfortable)
  - WBD > 10 °C → RH < 20% (very dry, desert conditions)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** For Module Experiments in Lab Courses, Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B to be answered). Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Calculation + Theory**

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Question)]`**

**(a)** In a Sling Psychrometer experiment, the following readings were recorded:
- Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) = **40 °C**
- Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) = **28 °C**
- Atmospheric pressure = **1.01325 bar**

Calculate the:
1. Wet Bulb Depression
2. Saturation pressure of water vapor at WBT (use Carrier's equation)
3. Saturation pressure of water vapor at DBT (use Carrier's equation)
4. Partial pressure of water vapor in the air
5. Relative humidity of the air

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the calculated relative humidity, determine:
1. Specific humidity of the air
2. Dew point temperature (using psychrometric relations)
3. Enthalpy of the air per kg of dry air
4. Density of moist air

Comment on the **comfort level** of this air sample based on ASHRAE standard effective temperature chart. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wet Bulb Depression (1 mark):**

$$
WBD = DBT - WBT = 40 - 28 = 12 \; °C
$$

**Step 2 — Saturation pressure at WBT = 28 °C (2 marks):**

Using Carrier's equation:

$$
\ln(P_{s,28}) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{28 + 235} = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{263}
$$

$$
\ln(P_{s,28}) = 18.6686 - 15.324 = 3.3446
$$

$$
P_{s,28} = e^{3.3446} = 28.34 \; \text{mm Hg}
$$

Converting to kPa:

$$
P_{s,28} = \dfrac{28.34}{7.5006} = 3.778 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Saturation pressure at DBT = 40 °C (1 mark):**

$$
\ln(P_{s,40}) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{40 + 235} = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{275}
$$

$$
\ln(P_{s,40}) = 18.6686 - 14.655 = 4.0136
$$

$$
P_{s,40} = e^{4.0136} = 55.32 \; \text{mm Hg} = 7.375 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Partial pressure of water vapor (2 marks):**

$$
P_v = P_{s,WBT} - P \cdot WBD \cdot 0.000367
$$

$$
P_v = 3.778 - (101.325 \times 12 \times 0.000367) = 3.778 - 0.4463 = 3.332 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Relative Humidity (1 mark):**

$$
\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{s,DBT}} = \dfrac{3.332}{7.375} = 0.4517 = 45.17 \; \%
$$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Specific Humidity (2 marks):**

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.332}{101.325 - 3.332}
$$

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.332}{97.993} = 0.622 \times 0.03401 = 0.02115 \; \text{kg/kg dry air}
$$

**Step 2 — Dew Point Temperature (2 marks):**

We need to find T such that $P_{s,T} = P_v = 3.332$ kPa.

Trial T = 25 °C → $P_s = 3.167$ kPa (too low)
Trial T = 26 °C → $P_s = 3.361$ kPa (very close)

Interpolating:

$$
T_{DP} = 25 + \dfrac{3.332 - 3.167}{3.361 - 3.167} \times 1 = 25 + 0.824 = 25.82 \; °C
$$

**Step 3 — Enthalpy of moist air (2 marks):**

$$
h = 1.005 \cdot T_{DBT} + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_{DBT})
$$

$$
h = 1.005 \times 40 + 0.02115 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 40)
$$

$$
h = 40.2 + 0.02115 \times 2576.2 = 40.2 + 54.49 = 94.69 \; \text{kJ/kg dry air}
$$

**Step 4 — Density of moist air (1 mark):**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{P - P_v}{R_a \cdot T} = \dfrac{101.325 - 3.332}{0.287 \times (40 + 273.15)} = \dfrac{97.993}{89.92} = 1.0898 \; \text{kg/m}^3
$$

(where T is in Kelvin: 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 K)

**Comfort Comment:** At DBT = 40 °C and RH = 45%, the air is **hot and dry**. The Effective Temperature is well above the ASHRAE summer comfort band of 23–26 °C, indicating **thermal discomfort** for occupants. This would be typical of a hot desert climate and would require **active cooling and humidification** for human comfort.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + Procedure Focus**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]`**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a Sling Psychrometer. List any **four precautions** to be observed during the experiment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A room has DBT of 30 °C and WBT of 22 °C. Find:
1. Relative humidity
2. Specific humidity
3. Dew point temperature
4. Humid volume

Also explain why the Sling Psychrometer is **preferred over a digital hygrometer** for field measurements in industry. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

The Sling Psychrometer consists of:

1. **Two identical mercury-in-glass thermometers** mounted parallel to each other on a lightweight metallic/plastic frame.
2. **Dry Bulb Thermometer** — bulb exposed bare to the air; measures true DBT.
3. **Wet Bulb Thermometer** — bulb wrapped with a **single layer of clean muslin cloth** (cotton wick); a wick tail dips into a small **distilled water reservoir** attached to the frame.
4. **Handle with swivel pivot** at one end of the frame, allowing the entire assembly to be whirled manually.
5. **A protective metal guard** around the thermometers to prevent breakage during whirling.
6. Both thermometers have graduated scales from **0 °C to 50 °C** with 0.5 °C least count.

**Working (2 marks):**

The sling is held by the handle and whirled at **4–5 revolutions per second** for **1–2 minutes**. Air flows rapidly over both bulbs. The dry bulb records the true air temperature. On the wet bulb, water evaporates into the unsaturated air stream, absorbing latent heat, cooling the bulb. At thermal equilibrium, the wet-bulb thermometer stabilizes and shows the WBT. The difference (DBT − WBT) is used with steam tables or psychrometric chart to determine humidity.

**Four Precautions (1 mark — 0.25 each):**

1. Use **distilled water** (not tap water) to wet the wick — tap water salts will crystallize and block evaporation.
2. The wick must be **clean and properly wetted**; replace if it is dirty or hardened.
3. Whirl in an **open area away from walls, windows, and people** to avoid contamination of the air sample.
4. Take readings **only after two consecutive WBT readings are stable within ±0.5 °C**.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** DBT = 30 °C, WBT = 22 °C, P = 101.325 kPa.

**Step 1 — WBD (small sub-step):**

$$
WBD = 30 - 22 = 8 \; °C
$$

**Step 2 — Saturation pressures:**

At WBT = 22 °C:

$$
\ln(P_{s,22}) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{22 + 235} = 18.6686 - 15.674 = 2.9946
$$

$$
P_{s,22} = e^{2.9946} = 19.99 \; \text{mm Hg} = 2.665 \; \text{kPa}
$$

At DBT = 30 °C:

$$
\ln(P_{s,30}) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{30 + 235} = 18.6686 - 15.214 = 3.4546
$$

$$
P_{s,30} = e^{3.4546} = 31.69 \; \text{mm Hg} = 4.225 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Partial pressure:**

$$
P_v = 2.665 - (101.325 \times 8 \times 0.000367) = 2.665 - 0.2975 = 2.368 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Relative Humidity (1.5 marks):**

$$
\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{s,DBT}} = \dfrac{2.368}{4.225} = 0.5605 = 56.05 \; \%
$$

**Step 5 — Specific Humidity (1.5 marks):**

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.368}{101.325 - 2.368} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.368}{98.957} = 0.01488 \; \text{kg/kg dry air}
$$

**Step 6 — Dew Point (1.5 marks):**

Find T such that $P_s = 2.368$ kPa.

From steam tables, $P_{s,20} = 2.339$ kPa, $P_{s,21} = 2.486$ kPa.

Interpolating:

$$
T_{DP} = 20 + \dfrac{2.368 - 2.339}{2.486 - 2.339} \times 1 = 20 + 0.197 = 20.20 \; °C
$$

**Step 7 — Humid Volume (1.5 marks):**

$$
v = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T}{P - P_v} = \dfrac{0.287 \times (30 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 2.368}
$$

$$
v = \dfrac{0.287 \times 303.15}{98.957} = \dfrac{86.99}{98.957} = 0.8791 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}
$$

**Why Sling Psychrometer is Preferred over Digital Hygrometer (1 mark):**

- **No calibration drift:** Sling psychrometer is a primary measurement instrument based on fundamental physical principles (mass + heat transfer), so it does not suffer from sensor drift.
- **No battery dependency:** It works purely on manual operation and reading.
- **Lower cost:** Costs ~₹500–800 vs ₹3000+ for digital hygrometers.
- **Better for harsh environments:** No electronic components to fail in dust, humidity, or temperature extremes.
- **Can be used as a reference standard** to calibrate electronic sensors.
- **Wide operating range:** Typically 0 to 100 °C and 5 to 100 % RH.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sling Psychrometer Calculations:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks typical):** Students often mix mm Hg and kPa in the same calculation. **Always convert to a single unit system** before substituting into formulas. KTU valuation strictly penalizes inconsistent units.
>
> 2. **Wrong Constant in Carrier's Equation (–2 marks):** Some students use $A = 18.3036$ (older reference) or $A = 16.89$ (other Antoine form). **Use $A = 18.6686$, $B = 4030.183$, $C = 235.0$** as per the KTU prescribed reference.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Psychrometric Constant Correction (–1 mark):** Just using $P_v = P_{s,WBT}$ without subtracting the $P \cdot WBD \cdot 0.000367$ term gives an incorrect (higher) relative humidity.
>
> 4. **Wet Bulb vs. Dew Point Confusion (–1 mark):** WBT is the temperature a parcel of air would have if **adiabatically saturated** by evaporating water into it. Dew Point is the temperature at which the air becomes **saturated by cooling** (no water added). These are NOT the same.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Report in SI Units (–0.5 mark):** Always report $\omega$ in kg/kg dry air, $h$ in kJ/kg dry air, $v$ in m³/kg dry air, and RH in %.
>
> 6. **No Repetition in Observation Table (–1 mark):** The KTU lab examiner will check that **at least 3 trials** are recorded, with the third being the stable value. Single-shot readings are heavily penalized.
>
> 7. **No Mention of Apparatus Calibration (–0.5 mark):** Always mention that the thermometers were checked for **zero-error** before the experiment and that the barometer reading was cross-verified.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 1 hour before the exam:**

- [x] Sling Psychrometer = **two thermometers + muslin wick + handle for whirling**
- [x] DBT = dry thermometer reading; WBT = wet (wicked) thermometer reading
- [x] **WBD = DBT − WBT** (always positive)
- [x] Larger WBD → **drier** air → **lower** RH
- [x] Smaller WBD → **more humid** air → **higher** RH
- [x] WBD = 0 → **saturated air** (RH = 100%)
- [x] Carrier's Equation: $\ln(P_s) = 18.6686 - \dfrac{4030.183}{T + 235.0}$ (T in °C, $P_s$ in mm Hg)
- [x] **Divide by 7.5006** to convert mm Hg → kPa
- [x] **Multiply by 7.5006** to convert kPa → mm Hg
- [x] Psychrometric constant: $A_{psy} = 0.000367$ per °C
- [x] $P_v = P_{s,WBT} - P \cdot WBD \cdot 0.000367$
- [x] $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{s,DBT}}$ (× 100 for percentage)
- [x] $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ kg water/kg dry air
- [x] $h = 1.005 \cdot T_{DBT} + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_{DBT})$ kJ/kg dry air
- [x] $v = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T}{P - P_v} = \dfrac{0.287 \cdot T_{Kelvin}}{P - P_v}$ m³/kg dry air
- [x] Dew Point: find T at which $P_{s,T} = P_v$
- [x] **Always use distilled water** in the wick reservoir
- [x] **Whirl at 4–5 rev/s for 1–2 min**; stop when two WBT readings match within ±0.5 °C
- [x] **3 trials minimum**; report the stable value
- [x] **Note barometric pressure** from the lab barometer, don't assume 1 atm blindly
- [x] Mass transfer: **Lewis Relation** is the theoretical basis
- [x] Sling psychrometer is a **primary reference instrument**; digital hygrometers need calibration against it
- [x] Common error: forgetting to **account for barometric pressure** if the lab is at altitude (e.g., Munnar, Ooty, Leh)
- [x] **Dew Point ≠ WBT** (Dew Point < WBT for unsaturated air; WBT is the adiabatic saturation temperature)
- [x] For moist air, **specific heat (humid heat)** $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \cdot \omega$ kJ/kg·K
- [x] Comfort zone (ASHRAE 55): DBT 23–26 °C, RH 30–60 %

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Capacity and COP of Split Air Conditioner
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Capacity and COP of Split Air Conditioner

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Split Air Conditioner (Split AC):** A *split-type* refrigeration-based air conditioning system in which the unit is divided into two physically separated components — an **Indoor Unit (Evaporator + Blower)** and an **Outdoor Unit (Compressor + Condenser + Expansion Device)** — interconnected only by refrigerant piping, electrical cabling, and a condensate drain.

The **Capacity** of a split air conditioner is defined as the *net rate of heat removal* from the conditioned space, expressed in **kW**, **TR (Tonnes of Refrigeration)**, or **kcal/hr**. The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is defined as the *ratio of the desired cooling effect (Qe)* to the *work input to the compressor (Wc)*.

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{cooling}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output (Cooling Effect)}}{\text{Required Input (Compressor Work)}} = \frac{Q_e}{W_c}
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Cooler Pump"**
> Imagine a sealed water cooler with a pump (compressor) that continuously circulates chilled water through a sponge (evaporator) sitting inside a room. The pump *sweats* (rejects heat) outside the room (condenser). The harder the pump works and the more chilled water that flows, the cooler the room. The **COP** is essentially — *"For every unit of electricity you spend running the pump, how many units of cooling comfort do you get back?"* A higher COP means a more *frugal* air conditioner.

A split AC is preferred over a window AC because the *compressor noise* stays **outdoors**, and the *evaporator* can be mounted on a wall, freeing up the window space.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr ≈ 12000 BTU/hr**
> - **Standard Test Condition:** Return air at **27 °C DB / 19 °C WB**; Outdoor at **35 °C DB / 24 °C WB** (IS 1391 / AHRI 210/240 standard ratings).
> - **Refrigerant:** R-22 (legacy) or R-410A (modern, eco-friendly, zero ODP).
> - **Energy Efficiency Rating (India):** **ISEER (Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio)** — Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) star rating.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Cycle — The Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)

A split AC operates on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**, which is the heart of the experiment. The four key thermodynamic processes are:

1. **Process 1 → 2 (Compression):** Low-pressure vapour refrigerant is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour in the **hermetic/reciprocating compressor** (located in the outdoor unit). Work input: $W_c$.

2. **Process 2 → 3 (Condensation):** Hot refrigerant vapour rejects heat to outdoor ambient air (sensible + latent heat rejection) in the **condenser coil** aided by an outdoor fan. It condenses to a high-pressure sub-cooled liquid.

3. **Process 3 → 4 (Expansion / Throttling):** High-pressure liquid passes through the **capillary tube / thermostatic expansion valve (TXV/EEV)**, undergoing *isenthalpic* throttling — pressure and temperature drop sharply (flash evaporation).

4. **Process 4 → 1 (Evaporation):** Low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat (latent heat of vaporization) from indoor air in the **evaporator coil** aided by the indoor blower, providing the desired cooling effect $Q_e$.

## 2.2 Why & How — The Energy-Flow Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Balance (First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the cycle):**
> The total heat rejected at the condenser equals the cooling effect plus the compressor work.

$$
Q_c = Q_e + W_c
$$

- The compressor consumes electrical energy ($W_{\text{elec}}$) which becomes mechanical work ($W_c$) plus heat losses.
- $Q_e$ is delivered to the indoor coil; $Q_c$ is dumped outdoors.
- **A COP > 1 does NOT violate the 2nd Law** — it simply means the system *moves* heat, rather than *creating* it.

## 2.3 Measurement Philosophy in the Laboratory

In the lab, we cannot measure refrigerant mass flow rate directly with high accuracy. Instead, we use the **Air-Side Enthalpy Method**, which is the most accurate indirect method (ASHRAE Standard 41.1).

| Quantity Measured | Instrument Used | Location |
|---|---|---|
| Indoor dry-bulb temperature (DB) entering coil | Thermocouples / RTD | Before evaporator |
| Indoor wet-bulb temperature (WB) entering coil | Wet-bulb thermometer w/ wick | Before evaporator |
| Outdoor DB & WB | Thermocouples | Ambient + condenser exit |
| Air velocity at evaporator outlet | Anemometer | After blower |
| Air velocity at condenser outlet | Anemometer | After outdoor fan |
| Electrical power to compressor | Wattmeter / Energy meter | Compressor line |
| Cross-sectional area of ducts | Measuring tape | Duct sections |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot A \cdot V$ | Mass flow rate of air | kg/s |
| 2 | $h = 1.005 \cdot T_{db} + W \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db})$ | Specific enthalpy of moist air (per kg dry air) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_w}{P_t - P_w}$ | Specific humidity | kg water / kg dry air |
| 4 | $P_w = P_{ws}(T_{wb}) - (P_t - P_{ws}(T_{wb})) \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \cdot A_f$ | Partial pressure of water vapour (psychrometric) | kPa |
| 5 | $Q_e = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | Cooling capacity (evaporator side) | kW |
| 6 | $Q_c = \dot{m}_{a,c} \cdot (h_4 - h_3)$ | Heat rejected (condenser side) | kW |
| 7 | $W_c = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ or Wattmeter reading | Compressor work input | kW |
| 8 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_c}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless |
| 9 | $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$ | Conversion constant | — |
| 10 | $\text{EER} = \dfrac{Q_e\ (\text{in BTU/hr})}{W_c\ (\text{in W})}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio (older rating) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Tip — Use \vert instead of | inside markdown tables.** All absolute-value bars above are written as `\vert` or `()` to prevent table syntax breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The air-side enthalpy method is the *gold standard* used by **AHRI (Air-Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute)** for performance certification of all unitary AC equipment worldwide. Mastering this experiment directly enables a student to:
- Audit energy bills and star ratings.
- Validate manufacturer claims of capacity and ISEER.
- Diagnose performance degradation in field-installed ACs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cooling Capacity Using the Air-Side Enthalpy Method

The cooling capacity $Q_e$ is derived by applying the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to the evaporator, where the refrigerant absorbs heat from the air stream.

### Step 1 — Steady-Flow Energy Equation on the Evaporator Control Volume

Under steady-state, negligible kinetic and potential energy changes, no work transfer, and adiabatic duct walls:

$$
\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{a,\text{evap}} \cdot (h_{a,\text{in}} - h_{a,\text{out}})
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_{a,\text{evap}}$ = mass flow rate of air through the evaporator (kg/s)
- $h_{a,\text{in}}$ = enthalpy of air entering evaporator coil (kJ/kg dry air)
- $h_{a,\text{out}}$ = enthalpy of air leaving evaporator coil (kJ/kg dry air)

### Step 2 — Express Mass Flow Rate of Air

$$
\dot{m}_{a,\text{evap}} = \rho_a \cdot A_{\text{duct}} \cdot V_{\text{mean}}
$$

For an indoor unit, the air density can be evaluated at the *average* of inlet and outlet conditions using the ideal gas relation:

$$
\rho_a = \frac{P_t}{R_a \cdot T_{a,\text{mean}}}
$$

with $R_a = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ for dry air.

### Step 3 — Enthalpy of Moist Air (Per kg of Dry Air)

The standard psychrometric relation from ASHRAE Handbook of Fundamentals:

$$
h = 1.005 \cdot T_{db} + W \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db})
$$

where $W$ is the humidity ratio (kg water vapour per kg dry air).

### Step 4 — Humidity Ratio from Wet-Bulb Temperature

Carrier's equation (or Sprung's formula) is used:

$$
W = \dfrac{(2501 - 2.381 \cdot T_{wb}) \cdot W_s' - 1.005 \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db} - 4.186 \cdot T_{wb}}
$$

where $W_s'$ is the saturation humidity ratio at the wet-bulb temperature.

Alternatively, for a typical lab-grade approximation, the simpler form suffices:

$$
W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_t - P_w}
$$

with $P_w$ the partial pressure of water vapour in kPa.

### Step 5 — Combine to Obtain Cooling Capacity

$$
\boxed{Q_e = \rho_a \cdot A_{\text{duct}} \cdot V_{\text{mean}} \cdot \left[ 1.005 (T_{1,db} - T_{2,db}) + W_1 (2501 + 1.88 T_{1,db}) - W_2 (2501 + 1.88 T_{2,db}) \right]}
$$

Subscript 1 = air entering evaporator; Subscript 2 = air leaving evaporator.

If a *simpler* assumption is permitted (no condensation on coil, $W_1 \approx W_2 = W$):

$$
Q_e \approx \dot{m}_a \cdot \left[ 1.005 \cdot (T_{1,db} - T_{2,db}) + W \cdot 1.88 \cdot (T_{1,db} - T_{2,db}) \right] = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,m} \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $c_{p,m} = 1.005 + 1.88 W$ is the **moist-air specific heat**.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Lab Reading)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Verify with your own lab data, then substitute.**

**Given measurements:**
- Air entering evaporator: $T_1 = 305\ \text{K}\ (32\ ^\circ\text{C DB})$, $T_{w1} = 297\ \text{K}\ (24\ ^\circ\text{C WB})$
- Air leaving evaporator: $T_2 = 290\ \text{K}\ (17\ ^\circ\text{C DB})$, $T_{w2} = 286\ \text{K}\ (13\ ^\circ\text{C WB})$
- Mean air velocity at outlet duct: $V = 3.5\ \text{m/s}$
- Duct cross-section: $A = 0.20\ \text{m} \times 0.20\ \text{m} = 0.04\ \text{m}^2$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_t = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- Wattmeter reading: $W_c = 1.25\ \text{kW}$

**Step A — Mean air temperature and density:**

$$
T_m = \frac{305 + 290}{2} = 297.5\ \text{K}
$$

$$
\rho_a = \frac{101.325}{0.287 \times 297.5} = \frac{101.325}{85.36} = 1.187\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step B — Mass flow rate of air:**

$$
\dot{m}_a = 1.187 \times 0.04 \times 3.5 = 0.1662\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step C — Enthalpies:**

For $T_1 = 32\ ^\circ\text{C DB}, T_{w1} = 24\ ^\circ\text{C WB}$ → from psychrometric chart / calculator:
$h_1 = 76.5\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$

For $T_2 = 17\ ^\circ\text{C DB}, T_{w2} = 13\ ^\circ\text{C WB}$:
$h_2 = 39.4\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$

**Step D — Cooling Capacity:**

$$
Q_e = 0.1662 \times (76.5 - 39.4) = 0.1662 \times 37.1 = 6.165\ \text{kW}
$$

Converting to TR:

$$
Q_e = \frac{6.165}{3.517} = 1.753\ \text{TR}
$$

**Step E — Coefficient of Performance:**

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{Q_e}{W_c} = \frac{6.165}{1.25} = 4.93
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A typical split AC has COP in the range 2.8 – 4.0 under part-load; 4.5–5.5 may indicate an inverter unit at reduced load.** Check your experiment manual for the *rated* COP.

## 3.3 Validation: Heat-Balance Cross-Check

A rigorous lab report *must* verify the First Law closure:

$$
Q_c \stackrel{?}{=} Q_e + W_c
$$

If a condenser-side measurement is also taken, the heat rejected is:

$$
Q_c = \dot{m}_{a,c} \cdot (h_{\text{out,c}} - h_{\text{in,c}})
$$

A closure error of **±5 %** is acceptable; beyond that, instrument calibration or heat-loss through ducts is suspect.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Split Air Conditioner Architecture (Indoor + Outdoor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["INDOOR UNIT"] --> node2["Return Air Grille"]
    node2 --> node3["Air Filter"]
    node3 --> node4["Evaporator Coil"]
    node4 --> node5["Indoor Blower Fan"]
    node5 --> node6["Supply Air Duct"]
    node6 --> node7["Conditioned Room"]

    node4 -. "Low Pressure Vapour Line" .-> node8["OUTDOOR UNIT"]
    node8 --> node9["Hermetic Compressor"]
    node9 --> node10["Condenser Coil"]
    node10 --> node11["Condenser Fan"]
    node11 --> node12["Ambient Air Rejection"]
    node10 --> node13["Capillary Tube / TXV"]
    node13 -. "Low Pressure Liquid Line" .-> node4

    node9 -. "Electrical Power Cable" .-> node14["AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz"]
    node5 -. "Control Signal" .-> node14
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram (T-s and P-h) of VCRC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["State 1: Saturated Vapour at Low Pressure"] --> p2["State 2: Superheated Vapour after Compression"]
    p2 --> p3["State 3: Saturated / Subcooled Liquid at High Pressure"]
    p3 --> p4["State 4: Liquid-Vapour Mix after Throttling"]
    p4 --> p1

    subgraph comp_block["Compressor Wc input"]
        p1 -.- p2
    end
    subgraph cond_block["Condenser Qc rejected"]
        p2 -.- p3
    end
    subgraph evap_block["Evaporator Qe absorbed"]
        p4 -.- p1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    instr1["T1 DB Inlet Sensor"] --> data1["Data Acquisition System"]
    instr2["T2 WB Inlet Sensor"] --> data1
    instr3["T3 DB Outlet Sensor"] --> data1
    instr4["T4 WB Outlet Sensor"] --> data1
    instr5["Anemometer Duct"] --> data1
    instr6["Wattmeter Compressor"] --> data1
    instr7["Pressure Gauges HP LP"] --> data1
    data1 --> calc["Calculate Qe, Wc, COP"]
    calc --> report["Lab Report Viva"]
```
