The air absorbs moisture (adiabatic saturation / cooling and humidification). 2. **Dehumidification** → Achieved by a **Cooling Coil** (with surface temperature below the dew point) where water vapour condenses out as the air is cooled below its dew point. ## 2.2 Detailed Process Steps ### Step 1: Identify the Inlet State (State 1) - Measure DBT$_1$, WBT$_1$ using thermometers placed at the inlet duct. - Use the psychrometric chart to find: $W_1$, $h_1$, $\phi_1$, $v_1$ (specific volume), DPT$_1$. ### Step 2: Identify the Outlet State (State 2) - Measure DBT$_2$, WBT$_2$ at the outlet duct. - From the chart, read: $W_2$, $h_2$, $\phi_2$, $v_2$, DPT$_2$. ### Step 3: Determine the Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air - Measure the **velocity of air** using an anemometer at the duct: $V$ (m/s). - Measure the **cross-sectional area** of the duct: $A$ (m²). - Volumetric flow rate: $\dot{V} = V \times A$ (m³/s). - Mass flow rate of moist air: $\dot{m}_{moist} = \dot{V} / v_1$ (kg/s). - Mass flow rate of **dry air**: $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_{moist}}{1 + W_1} \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** All psychrometric calculations are referenced to **dry air**, never moist air. This is a fundamental KTU convention. ### Step 4: Calculate the Humidification or Dehumidification Load The **moisture load** is the rate at which water must be added (humidification) or removed (dehumidification): $$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \times \vert W_2 - W_1 \vert \quad \text{(kg/s of water)}$$ - If $W_2 > W_1$ → **Humidification** (water must be added) - If $W_2 < W_1$ → **Dehumidification** (water must be removed) ### Step 5: Calculate the Associated Heat Loads - **Sensible Heat Load** (temperature change only): $$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \times c_{pa} \times (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{(kW)}$$ - **Latent Heat Load** (moisture change only): $$Q_L = \dot{m}_a \times (W_2 - W_1) \times h_{fg} \quad \text{(kW)}$$ - **Total Heat (Enthalpy) Load**: $$Q_T = Q_s + Q_L = \dot{m}_a \times (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{(kW)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental KTU Relationship:** The total heat load can be computed either by summing sensible and latent loads OR directly from the enthalpy difference. Both must give the same answer — this is a great self-check during exams. ### Step 6: Calculate the Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_T} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}$$ This determines the **nature of the cooling process**: - SHF = 1 → Pure sensible cooling (no moisture change) - SHF = 0 → Pure latent cooling (no temperature change) - 0 < SHF < 1 → Combined sensible + latent cooling (most AC applications) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dot{V} = V \times A$ | Volumetric flow rate of air | m³/s | | 2 | $\dot{m}_{moist} = \dot{V} / v_1$ | Mass flow rate of moist air | kg/s | | 3 | $\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_{moist} / (1 + W_1)$ | Mass flow rate of **dry air** | kg/s | | 4 | $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \times \vert W_2 - W_1 \vert$ | Humidification or Dehumidification load | kg/s | | 5 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \times c_{pa} \times \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load | kW | | 6 | $Q_L = \dot{m}_a \times \Delta W \times h_{fg}$ | Latent heat load | kW | | 7 | $Q_T = Q_s + Q_L$ | Total heat load | kW | | 8 | $Q_T = \dot{m}_a \times (h_2 - h_1)$ | Total heat (enthalpy method) | kW | | 9 | $\text{SHF} = Q_s / Q_T$ | Sensible Heat Factor | dimensionless | | 10 | $W = 0.622 \times \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | Specific humidity from partial pressure | kg/kg dry air | | 11 | $\phi = P_v / P_{g}$ | Relative humidity | % | | 12 | $\text{BPF} = (T_2 - T_3) / (T_1 - T_3)$ | Bypass factor (cooling coil) | dimensionless | | 13 | ADP from psychrometric chart | Surface temp of cooling coil | °C | | 14 | $m_{water, hourly} = \dot{m}_w \times 3600$ | Moisture load per hour | kg/hr | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In HVAC industry practice, the **humidification/dehumidification load** estimation is critical for: - **Sizing the air washer pump** (flow rate of recirculated water). - **Selecting the cooling coil capacity** in TR (Tonnes of Refrigeration) — a typical 1.5 TR window AC dehumidifies about 1–2 L/hr of water. - **Designing chilled water flow rates** in central plants. - **Duct sizing** to ensure the air mass flow rate is sufficient to carry the load. - **Energy auditing** of existing buildings. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Relevance:** Pharmaceutical cleanrooms, textile mills (humidification to 70-80% RH prevents fibre breakage), hospitals, data centres, and paper storage warehouses all rely on precise humidification/dehumidification load calculations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Humidification/Dehumidification Load Formula ### Starting from First Principles Consider a control volume of air entering a device (air washer or cooling coil). - **Inlet mass flow rate of dry air** = $\dot{m}_a$ (kg/s, constant throughout the device since dry air is neither added nor removed). - **Inlet specific humidity** = $W_1$ (kg water / kg dry air). - **Outlet specific humidity** = $W_2$ (kg water / kg dry air). **Mass of water vapour entering per second** = $\dot{m}_a \times W_1$ (kg/s) **Mass of water vapour leaving per second** = $\dot{m}_a \times W_2$ (kg/s) **Net water mass that must be added (or removed)** = Difference of the two: $$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \times (W_2 - W_1) \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$ - If positive → humidification load - If negative → dehumidification load (magnitude is the load) ### Derivation of the Total Heat Load The total enthalpy of moist air per kg of dry air is: $$h = c_{pa} \times T + W \times (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \times T) \quad \text{(kJ/kg dry air)}$$ For inlet state 1 and outlet state 2: $$ \begin{aligned} h_1 &= c_{pa} \times T_1 + W_1 \times h_{fg,0} \quad \text{(simplified, T in °C, } h_{fg,0} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg)} \\ h_2 &= c_{pa} \times T_2 + W_2 \times h_{fg,0} \end{aligned} $$ Total heat transfer rate (per kg of dry air): $$ \begin{aligned} q_T &= h_2 - h_1 \\ &= c_{pa} \times (T_2 - T_1) + h_{fg,0} \times (W_2 - W_1) \\ &= \underbrace{c_{pa} \times (T_2 - T_1)}_{\text{Sensible term}} + \underbrace{h_{fg,0} \times (W_2 - W_1)}_{\text{Latent term}} \end{aligned} $$ For $\dot{m}_a$ kg/s of dry air: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_T &= \dot{m}_a \times q_T \\ Q_T &= \dot{m}_a \times c_{pa} \times (T_2 - T_1) + \dot{m}_a \times h_{fg,0} \times (W_2 - W_1) \\ Q_T &= Q_s + Q_L \quad \text{(derived)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Worked-Out Numerical Demonstration (Worked Example) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Air enters an air washer at DBT = 40 °C and WBT = 26 °C. It leaves at DBT = 30 °C and WBT = 25 °C. The air velocity in the duct is 8 m/s and duct cross-section is 0.5 m². Calculate (a) the humidification load, (b) the sensible heat load, (c) the latent heat load, (d) the total heat load, and (e) the SHF. **Step 1: Read the psychrometric chart at State 1 (40 °C DBT, 26 °C WBT):** $$ \begin{aligned} W_1 &= 0.0151 \text{ kg/kg dry air} \\ h_1 &= 79.5 \text{ kJ/kg dry air} \\ v_1 &= 0.902 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Read the psychrometric chart at State 2 (30 °C DBT, 25 °C WBT):** $$ \begin{aligned} W_2 &= 0.0198 \text{ kg/kg dry air} \\ h_2 &= 81.3 \text{ kJ/kg dry air} \\ v_2 &= 0.876 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Calculate the volumetric and mass flow rates:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{V} &= V \times A = 8 \times 0.5 = 4.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\ \dot{m}_a &= \frac{\dot{V}}{v_1} = \frac{4.0}{0.902} = 4.434 \text{ kg/s of dry air} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Calculate the humidification load (since $W_2 > W_1$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_w &= \dot{m}_a \times (W_2 - W_1) \\ &= 4.434 \times (0.0198 - 0.0151) \\ &= 4.434 \times 0.0047 \\ &= 0.02084 \text{ kg/s} \\ &= 0.02084 \times 3600 = 75.0 \text{ kg/hr of water} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Calculate the sensible heat load:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_s &= \dot{m}_a \times c_{pa} \times (T_2 - T_1) \\ &= 4.434 \times 1.005 \times (30 - 40) \\ &= 4.434 \times 1.005 \times (-10) \\ &= -44.56 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ The negative sign indicates **heat is being removed** (cooling) from the air. Magnitude = **44.56 kW**. **Step 6: Calculate the latent heat load:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_L &= \dot{m}_a \times h_{fg} \times (W_2 - W_1) \\ &= 4.434 \times 2500 \times 0.0047 \\ &= 52.10 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ The positive sign here indicates that **latent energy is added to the air** (water evaporated into it, absorbing its latent heat from the air — that is why the air cools down). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention Wisdom:** During humidification with evaporative cooling, the air loses sensible heat (gets cooler) but gains latent heat (absorbs water vapour). Energy is conserved — the latent heat gained by the water comes from the sensible heat of the air. **Step 7: Calculate the total heat load:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_T &= Q_s + Q_L = -44.56 + 52.10 = 7.54 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification via enthalpy method:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_T &= \dot{m}_a \times (h_2 - h_1) = 4.434 \times (81.3 - 79.5) = 4.434 \times 1.8 = 7.98 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ (The small difference is due to chart reading approximations — in a KTU exam, a difference of < 5% is acceptable.) **Step 8: Calculate the SHF:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SHF} &= \frac{\vert Q_s \vert}{\vert Q_T \vert} = \frac{44.56}{52.10} = 0.855 \end{aligned} $$ (Since $Q_T$ in an air washer is usually net positive, the convention used is magnitude-based.) > [!NOTE] > **Final Summary Table for the Worked Example:** | Quantity | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}$ | 4.0 | m³/s | | Mass flow rate of dry air $\dot{m}_a$ | 4.434 | kg/s | | Humidification load | 75.0 | kg water/hr | | Sensible heat load (cooling) | 44.56 | kW | | Latent heat load (humidification) | 52.10 | kW | | Net total heat load | 7.98 | kW | | Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) | 0.855 | — | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Air Conditioning System with Humidification/Dehumidification Stages ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Return Air
from Room
DBT1 WBT1] --> B[Mixing Chamber
Fresh Air + Return Air] F[Fresh Air
from Outside] --> B B --> C[Pre-Filter
Removes Dust] C --> D[Cooling Coil
Dehumidification
Removes Moisture] D --> E[Heating Coil
Reheat if Needed] E --> G[Air Washer /
Spray Chamber
Humidification] G --> H[Supply Fan
Blower] H --> I[Supply Air Duct
To Conditioned Room
DBT2 WBT2] I --> J[Conditioned
Room Space] J --> A D -.-> K1[Condensate
Drain Water
Dehumidification Load] G -.-> K2[Recirculation
Pump + Spray Nozzles
Humidification Load] ``` ## 4.2 Psychrometric Process Path Diagram (On Chart) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["State 1
DBT1 = 40 C
WBT1 = 26 C
W1 = 0.0151 kg/kg
h1 = 79.5 kJ/kg"] P2["State 2
DBT2 = 30 C
WBT2 = 25 C
W2 = 0.0198 kg/kg
h2 = 81.3 kJ/kg"] P3["ADP
Apparatus Dew Point
~ 22 C
Surface Temp of Coil"] P1 -->|"Cooling + Dehumidification
on Coil (Path 1-3)"| P3 P3 -->|"Sensible Heating
Reheat (Path 3-2)"| P2 P1 -.->|"Actual Process with
Bypass Factor BPF"| P2 S["Process Summary: W2 > W1 so HUMIDIFICATION
Net Qs negative, QL positive, SHF ~ 0.86"] P2 --> S ``` ## 4.3 Detailed Mermaid Block Diagram of Laboratory Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INLET ["INLET SECTION"] A1[Inlet Duct] --> A2[Orifice Meter
for Air Flow] A2 --> A3[Inlet T1, Tw1
Thermometers] A3 --> A4[Inlet U-Tube
Manometer] end subgraph PROCESS ["PROCESS SECTION"] B1[Air Washer Chamber
with Spray Nozzles] --> B2[Recirculation
Water Pump] B2 --> B3[Water Tank
with Heater] B3 --> B1 end subgraph OUTLET ["OUTLET SECTION"] C1[Outlet T2, Tw2
Thermometers] --> C2[Outlet Orifice
Meter] C2 --> C3[Blower /
Centrifugal Fan] C3 --> C4[Discharge Duct
to Atmosphere] end A4 --> B1 B1 --> C1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** The key measurement points in the laboratory experiment are the **inlet and outlet dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures**, the **air velocity** (or volume flow rate via orifice), and the **water spray rate** in the air washer. Always start your answer by listing these measurements. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Q: Define (i) Humidification Load, and (ii) Dehumidification Load of an air conditioning system. State the SI unit for both.** **Model Answer:** > (i) **Humidification Load** is the rate of mass of water vapour that must be added to the air stream per unit time to raise the specific humidity from the inlet value $W_1$ to the desired outlet value $W_2$ (where $W_2 > W_1$). Mathematically, > $$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \times (W_2 - W_1)$$ > > (ii) **Dehumidification Load** is the rate of mass of water vapour that must be removed (condensed) from the air stream per unit time to lower the specific humidity from the inlet value $W_1$ to the desired outlet value $W_2$ (where $W_2 < W_1$). Mathematically, > $$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \times (W_1 - W_2)$$ > > **SI unit for both: kg/s (or kg/hr in practice).** ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q: What is the Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)? What does SHF = 1 and SHF = 0 signify?** **Model Answer:** > The **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** is the ratio of the sensible heat load to the total heat load of the cooling coil: > $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}$$ > > - **SHF = 1** signifies **pure sensible cooling** — only temperature changes; no moisture removal (e.g., sensible cooling coil in dry climates). > - **SHF = 0** signifies **pure latent cooling** — only moisture is removed; no temperature change (e.g., chemical dehumidifier). --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Bank]** **Q: An air conditioning system handles 1.5 kg/s of dry air. The inlet air conditions are DBT = 38 °C and WBT = 28 °C. After passing through a cooling coil, the air leaves at DBT = 18 °C and WBT = 17 °C. The air is then passed through an air washer where it is humidified. After the air washer, the air is at DBT = 22 °C and WBT = 21 °C. Calculate:** **(a) [7 Marks] The dehumidification load in the cooling coil, the sensible and latent heat loads in the coil, and the SHF of the coil.** **(b) [7 Marks] The humidification load in the air washer, the net heat load after the washer, and the total moisture added per hour in the entire system (if any).** Use: $c_{pa}$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg}$ = 2500 kJ/kg. #### Model Solution **Step 1: State identification using psychrometric chart.** For **State 1 (Inlet to cooling coil):** DBT = 38 °C, WBT = 28 °C - $W_1$ = 0.0210 kg/kg dry air - $h_1$ = 87.5 kJ/kg dry air For **State 2 (Outlet of cooling coil / Inlet to air washer):** DBT = 18 °C, WBT = 17 °C - $W_2$ = 0.0132 kg/kg dry air - $h_2$ = 51.6 kJ/kg dry air For **State 3 (Outlet of air washer):** DBT = 22 °C, WBT = 21 °C - $W_3$ = 0.0160 kg/kg dry air - $h_3$ = 62.6 kJ/kg dry air ### Part (a) Solution **Dehumidification load in cooling coil (State 1 → State 2):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{w,\text{dehum}} &= \dot{m}_a \times (W_1 - W_2) \\ &= 1.5 \times (0.0210 - 0.0132) \\ &= 1.5 \times 0.0078 \\ &= 0.0117 \text{ kg/s} \\ &= 0.0117 \times 3600 = 42.12 \text{ kg/hr} \end{aligned} $$ **Sensible heat load in cooling coil:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_s &= \dot{m}_a \times c_{pa} \times (T_2 - T_1) \\ &= 1.5 \times 1.005 \times (18 - 38) \\ &= 1.5 \times 1.005 \times (-20) \\ &= -30.15 \text{ kW (cooling, magnitude = 30.15 kW)} \end{aligned} $$ **Latent heat load in cooling coil:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_L &= \dot{m}_a \times h_{fg} \times (W_2 - W_1) \\ &= 1.5 \times 2500 \times (0.0132 - 0.0210) \\ &= 1.5 \times 2500 \times (-0.0078) \\ &= -29.25 \text{ kW (latent cooling, magnitude = 29.25 kW)} \end{aligned} $$ **Total heat load of the coil:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_T &= Q_s + Q_L = -30.15 + (-29.25) = -59.40 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Sensible Heat Factor:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SHF} &= \frac{\vert Q_s \vert}{\vert Q_T \vert} = \frac{30.15}{59.40} = 0.508 \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (b) Solution **Humidification load in air washer (State 2 → State 3):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{w,\text{hum}} &= \dot{m}_a \times (W_3 - W_2) \\ &= 1.5 \times (0.0160 - 0.0132) \\ &= 1.5 \times 0.0028 \\ &= 0.0042 \text{ kg/s} \\ &= 0.0042 \times 3600 = 15.12 \text{ kg/hr} \end{aligned} $$ **Net heat load in the air washer (State 2 → State 3):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{washer}} &= \dot{m}_a \times (h_3 - h_2) \\ &= 1.5 \times (62.6 - 51.6) \\ &= 1.5 \times 11.0 = 16.5 \text{ kW (added to air)} \end{aligned} $$ **Total moisture added in the entire system:** Since the cooling coil removed 42.12 kg/hr and the air washer added 15.12 kg/hr, the **net moisture removed** by the system = 42.12 − 15.12 = **27.00 kg/hr** (overall dehumidification). #### Results Summary | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Dehumidification load (coil) | 42.12 kg/hr | | Sensible heat (coil) | 30.15 kW (cooling) | | Latent heat (coil) | 29.25 kW (cooling) | | SHF (coil) | 0.508 | | Humidification load (washer) | 15.12 kg/hr | | Net heat in washer | +16.5 kW (added) | | Net moisture removed by system | 27.00 kg/hr | --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]** **Q: An air washer is used to humidify air in a textile mill. The inlet air has DBT = 42 °C and WBT = 24 °C, and leaves the washer at DBT = 32 °C and WBT = 28 °C. The duct dimensions are 0.8 m × 0.6 m, and the air velocity measured by an anemometer is 5 m/s. Calculate:** **(a) [7 Marks] The mass flow rate of dry air in kg/s and the mass flow rate of water being sprayed (humidification load) in kg/hr.** **(b) [7 Marks] The sensible heat, latent heat, and total heat transfer rates. Comment on whether the air washer is acting as a cooler or heater in this process.** Use: $c_{pa}$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg}$ = 2500 kJ/kg. #### Model Solution **Step 1: State identification from psychrometric chart.** - **State 1 (Inlet):** DBT = 42 °C, WBT = 24 °C → $W_1$ = 0.0100 kg/kg dry air, $h_1$ = 67.5 kJ/kg dry air, $v_1$ = 0.928 m³/kg dry air - **State 2 (Outlet):** DBT = 32 °C, WBT = 28 °C → $W_2$ = 0.0210 kg/kg dry air, $h_2$ = 86.5 kJ/kg dry air ### Part (a) Solution **Cross-sectional area of the duct:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 0.8 \times 0.6 = 0.48 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Volumetric flow rate of air:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{V} &= V \times A = 5 \times 0.48 = 2.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Mass flow rate of dry air:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_a &= \frac{\dot{V}}{v_1} = \frac{2.4}{0.928} = 2.586 \text{ kg/s of dry air} \end{aligned} $$ **Humidification load (water spray rate):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_w &= \dot{m}_a \times (W_2 - W_1) \\ &= 2.586 \times (0.0210 - 0.0100) \\ &= 2.586 \times 0.0110 \\ &= 0.02845 \text{ kg/s} \\ &= 0.02845 \times 3600 = 102.4 \text{ kg/hr of water} \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (b) Solution **Sensible heat transfer rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_s &= \dot{m}_a \times c_{pa} \times (T_2 - T_1) \\ &= 2.586 \times 1.005 \times (32 - 42) \\ &= 2.586 \times 1.005 \times (-10) \\ &= -25.99 \text{ kW (heat removed from air)} \end{aligned} $$ **Latent heat transfer rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_L &= \dot{m}_a \times h_{fg} \times (W_2 - W_1) \\ &= 2.586 \times 2500 \times 0.0110 \\ &= 71.12 \text{ kW (latent energy added to air)} \end{aligned} $$ **Total heat transfer rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_T &= Q_s + Q_L = -25.99 + 71.12 = +45.13 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification by enthalpy method:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_T &= \dot{m}_a \times (h_2 - h_1) = 2.586 \times (86.5 - 67.5) = 2.586 \times 19.0 = 49.13 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ (Small difference due to chart reading — within acceptable KTU tolerance of 5–8%.) #### Results Summary & Comment | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Mass flow rate of dry air | 2.586 kg/s | | Humidification load | 102.4 kg/hr | | Sensible heat | −25.99 kW (air cooled) | | Latent heat | +71.12 kW (water vapour added) | | Total heat | +45.13 kW (net heat added to air) | **Comment:** The air washer is acting primarily as a **HUMIDIFIER with simultaneous air cooling** (evaporative cooling effect). Although the air DBT drops from 42 °C to 32 °C (sensible cooling of 10 °C), the massive addition of water vapour (latent energy gain of 71.12 kW) more than compensates, resulting in a **net heating of the air's total enthalpy**. This is a classic **adiabatic saturation process** and is highly desirable in textile mills where high humidity is needed without raising the air temperature. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign convention trap:** Many students forget to mark $Q_s$ as negative when air is being cooled. Always show the sign explicitly. > 2. **Mass flow basis:** A very common mistake is to use the **moist air** mass flow rate instead of the **dry air** mass flow rate in the formulas. All psychrometric equations use **dry air** as the basis. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation. > 3. **Specific humidity vs. Relative humidity:** Do not confuse $W$ (kg/kg) with $\phi$ (%). If a problem gives $\phi$ directly, you must first use the saturation pressure tables to find $W$. > 4. **Chart reading error:** Always read the psychrometric chart properties at the correct state. Inlet state uses inlet WBT, not outlet. > 5. **Unit conversion:** A common error is forgetting to convert kg/s to kg/hr (multiply by 3600). This is checked explicitly in KTU papers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Read before entering the exam hall!** - ✅ **Humidification Load** = $\dot{m}_a \times (W_2 - W_1)$ when $W_2 > W_1$ (units: kg/s or kg/hr of water). - ✅ **Dehumidification Load** = $\dot{m}_a \times (W_1 - W_2)$ when $W_2 < W_1$ (units: kg/s or kg/hr of water condensed). - ✅ **Sensible heat** is associated with **temperature change only** — formula: $Q_s = \dot{m}_a c_{pa} \Delta T$. - ✅ **Latent heat** is associated with **moisture change only** — formula: $Q_L = \dot{m}_a h_{fg} \Delta W$. - ✅ **Total heat** can be calculated as $Q_s + Q_L$ OR $\dot{m}_a (h_2 - h_1)$ — both must agree (energy conservation). - ✅ **Always use dry air mass flow rate** — never use moist air mass flow rate in psychrometric equations. - ✅ **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** = $Q_s / Q_T$ — determines process type. - ✅ **Bypass Factor (BPF)** = $(T_2 - T_{ADP}) / (T_1 - T_{ADP})$ — used in cooling coil design. - ✅ **Air washer** primarily causes **humidification + sensible cooling** (adiabatic saturation), with slight net heating of total enthalpy. - ✅ **Cooling coil** primarily causes **dehumidification + sensible cooling** (cooling and dehumidification process), with strong net cooling. - ✅ **Standard values to memorize:** $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2500$ kJ/kg, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa. - ✅ **Conversion factor:** kg/s × 3600 = kg/hr. - ✅ **Specific humidity $W$** is always in **kg water per kg dry air** (NOT per kg moist air). - ✅ **DPT, WBT, DBT order:** DPT ≤ WBT ≤ DBT (always, at the same state). - ✅ **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** is the surface temperature of the cooling coil — found by extending the process line on the psychrometric chart to the saturation curve. - ✅ **Always draw a neat psychrometric sketch** in your answer showing both states, the process line, and SHF/Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF) lines — this fetches 1–2 grace marks in KTU valuation. - ✅ **Laboratory procedure summary:** Measure inlet/outlet DBT & WBT, duct velocity, duct area → find $W_1, W_2$ from chart → find $\dot{m}_a$ → apply formulas → tabulate results.

topics.title: Study and Performance Experiment on Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Performance Experiment on Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)**: A heat-operated refrigeration cycle in which the mechanical compressor of a conventional vapour compression system is replaced by a thermally-driven **absorber–generator–pump assembly** that transports the refrigerant from low-pressure evaporator to high-pressure condenser. The most common working pairs are **Ammonia–Water (NH₃–H₂O)** for sub-zero applications and **Lithium Bromide–Water (LiBr–H₂O)** for air-conditioning applications above **0 °C**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory manual defines this experiment as one in which the student must identify the components, sketch the cycle on a P–h diagram, tabulate generator, condenser, absorber, evaporator temperatures, and evaluate the **Actual Coefficient of Performance (COP)** against the **Theoretical COP**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sponge and Bucket" Analogy**
> Think of the **absorber** as a thirsty sponge and the **generator** as the place where that sponge is squeezed dry.
> - The sponge (absorber) soaks up low-pressure refrigerant vapour — *just like a sponge soaking up water*.
> - The soaked sponge is then squeezed (heated in the generator) to release the refrigerant at high pressure.
> - The released vapour goes to the condenser, while the squeezed sponge (weak solution) is recycled back to absorb more vapour.

No mechanical shaft work is needed; only **heat input** at the generator and **heat rejection** at the condenser and absorber. This is why VARS is preferred where waste heat or solar energy is available (e.g., gas-fired domestic refrigerators, railway catering coaches, large commercial chillers).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Generator temperature | $T_g$ | **80 °C – 180 °C** |
| Condenser temperature | $T_c$ | **35 °C – 50 °C** |
| Absorber temperature | $T_a$ | **25 °C – 40 °C** |
| Evaporator temperature | $T_e$ | **−30 °C – 5 °C** |
| Generator pressure | $P_g$ | **10 – 20 bar** (NH₃) |
| Evaporator pressure | $P_e$ | **1 – 3 bar** (NH₃) |
| Actual COP | $\text{COP}_{\text{act}}$ | **0.3 – 0.6** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Components and Their Thermodynamic Role

The Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System consists of **eight major components**. Each plays a precise role in the closed thermodynamic loop:

1. **Evaporator** — Refrigerant (NH₃) evaporates by absorbing heat $Q_e$ from the cold space at low pressure $P_e$.
2. **Absorber** — Low-pressure NH₃ vapour is absorbed by weak aqua-ammonia solution, forming strong aqua-ammonia. Heat $Q_a$ is rejected to cooling water.
3. **Solution Pump** — Mechanically lifts strong solution from absorber (low $P$) to generator (high $P$). Pump work $W_p$ is small but non-zero.
4. **Generator (Boiler)** — Heat $Q_g$ supplied externally drives NH₃ out of the strong solution as vapour.
5. **Rectifier** — Removes water droplets from the NH₃ vapour leaving the generator before it enters the condenser (improves purity).
6. **Condenser** — High-pressure NH₃ vapour rejects heat $Q_c$ to cooling water and condenses to liquid.
7. **Expansion Valve (Refrigerant)** — Throttles liquid NH₃ from $P_c$ to $P_e$, causing partial flashing and cooling.
8. **Expansion Valve (Solution)** — Throttles weak solution from generator pressure back to absorber pressure.

### 2.2 Why "Heat Instead of Work"?

In a Vapour Compression System (VCRS), compressing vapour from low pressure to high pressure requires significant **shaft work**, typically **100 %** of the compressor's electrical input. In VARS, this compression is achieved **thermally** because the solution pump handles only the liquid (whose specific volume is roughly **1/1000** that of vapour). The pump work is therefore **negligible** compared to the generator heat input.

### 2.3 Working Pair Comparison

| Property | NH₃ – H₂O | LiBr – H₂O |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant | Ammonia | Water |
| Absorbent | Water | Lithium Bromide |
| Refrigeration range | **−30 °C to +5 °C** | **+5 °C to +20 °C** |
| Generator temp | 80 – 180 °C | 70 – 95 °C |
| Crystallization risk | Low | **High** (if cooled below 40 %) |
| Crystallization risk avoided by | — | Use of hot gas by-pass / dilution |
| Common application | Industrial cold storage, ice plants | Commercial A/C, hotel chillers |
| Toxicity of refrigerant | High (NH₃) | None (water) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every formula** that a KTU 2024 examiner expects to see in the laboratory record and ESE viva for this experiment.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Energy Input}} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}$ | General definition of COP for any refrigeration system |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}$ | Actual COP of VARS (pump work $W_p$ usually neglected in basic experiments) |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{(T_g - T_e)\,T_a}$ | Maximum (reversible / Carnot-based) COP of VARS |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \left(\dfrac{T_e}{T_a}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{T_g - T_a}{T_g - T_e}\right)$ | Alternative form of theoretical COP |
| 5 | $Q_e = m_r \,(h_g - h_f)$ at evaporator | Refrigeration effect (mass flow $\times$ enthalpy difference) |
| 6 | $Q_g = m_s \,(h_{gs} - h_{as})$ | Heat input at generator (mass of strong solution $\times$ enthalpy rise) |
| 7 | $Q_c = m_r \,(h_{cv} - h_{cl})$ | Heat rejected at condenser |
| 8 | $Q_a = Q_g + Q_e - Q_c$ | Energy balance: heat rejected at absorber |
| 9 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_c - T_e}$ | Carnot COP between $T_e$ and $T_c$ (upper limit reference) |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{act}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{theo}}} \times 100$ | Relative efficiency of the actual system (often asked in KTU viva) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Rule**: All temperatures in thermodynamic formulas **must be in Kelvin (K)**. Convert °C by adding **273.15**. Marks are deducted in KTU valuation for using Celsius directly inside an absolute-temperature formula.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic refrigerators** (gas-operated, Electrolux model) use NH₃–H₂O VARS and run continuously on kerosene or LPG without electricity — vital for rural electrification.
- **Solar-powered chillers** employ LiBr–H₂O absorption chillers coupled to parabolic trough collectors.
- **Cogeneration plants** use waste heat from turbines to drive absorption chillers, improving overall plant efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Theoretical COP of VARS

The theoretical (maximum) COP is derived by treating the absorber-generator combination as an **inverse Rankine heat engine** working between the temperatures $T_a$, $T_g$, and $T_e$.

**Step 1 — Identify the three temperature reservoirs of the heat-pump analogue**

The generator absorbs heat $Q_g$ at $T_g$.
The absorber rejects heat $Q_a$ at $T_a$.
The evaporator absorbs heat $Q_e$ at $T_e$.
The condenser rejects heat $Q_c$ at $T_c$.

**Step 2 — Apply the First Law to the combined absorber–generator assembly**

Treating absorber + generator + pump as a black box that takes in $Q_g$ and $Q_e$ and rejects $Q_a$:

$$
Q_a = Q_g + Q_e
$$

(Here the pump work $W_p$ and $Q_c$ are kept out of this sub-system for the COP derivation.)

**Step 3 — Apply the Second Law (entropy balance) for the reversible case**

$$
\frac{Q_g}{T_g} = \frac{Q_a}{T_a} + \frac{Q_e}{T_e}
$$

Rearranging for $Q_a$:

$$
Q_a = Q_g \, \frac{T_a}{T_g} - Q_e \, \frac{T_a}{T_e}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3 to eliminate $Q_a$**

$$
Q_g + Q_e = Q_g \, \frac{T_a}{T_g} - Q_e \, \frac{T_a}{T_e}
$$

Group the $Q_g$ terms on the left:

$$
Q_g - Q_g \, \frac{T_a}{T_g} = -Q_e - Q_e \, \frac{T_a}{T_e}
$$

Factor:

$$
Q_g \left(1 - \frac{T_a}{T_g}\right) = -Q_e \left(1 + \frac{T_a}{T_e}\right)
$$

$$
Q_g \left(\frac{T_g - T_a}{T_g}\right) = -Q_e \left(\frac{T_e + T_a}{T_e}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Take the magnitude (drop the negative sign because $Q_e$ is the heat absorbed, taken as positive for COP)**

$$
\frac{Q_g}{Q_e} = \frac{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)}{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the reciprocal to obtain $\text{COP}_{\text{theoretical}} = Q_e / Q_g$**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{theo}} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g} = \frac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classical KTU expression** for the theoretical COP of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System. It is sometimes written with $(T_e + T_a)$ in the denominator, equivalent to the form used in the cheat sheet above.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **canonical KTU 2024-Style numerical** that examiners set on this experiment. Solve it fully before reading the solution.

**Question**: In an ammonia–water vapour absorption refrigeration system, the temperatures observed are:

- Generator: $T_g = 160\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Absorber: $T_a = 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Evaporator: $T_e = -10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$

Compute the **theoretical COP** and the **Carnot COP** (using $T_e$ and $T_c = 40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).

---

**Solution — Step A: Convert temperatures to Kelvin**

$$
T_g = 160 + 273 = 433 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_a = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_e = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_c = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}
$$

**Solution — Step B: Substitute into the theoretical COP formula**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{theo}} = \frac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)}
$$

Numerator:

$$
T_e \,(T_g - T_a) = 263 \times (433 - 303) = 263 \times 130 = 34\,190
$$

Denominator:

$$
T_g \,(T_e + T_a) = 433 \times (263 + 303) = 433 \times 566 = 245\,078
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{theo}} = \frac{34\,190}{245\,078} = 0.1395
$$

**Solution — Step C: Carnot COP between evaporator and condenser**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_e}{T_c - T_e} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26
$$

**Solution — Step D: Comparison comment**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{theo}} \approx 0.14 \quad \ll \quad \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} \approx 5.26
$$

The huge gap shows that the theoretical COP of VARS is intrinsically much smaller than a Carnot limit because VARS uses **two heat sources and two heat sinks** (a heat transformer), not just two reservoirs. This is the most-discussed viva point.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Common KTU Student Errors

| Step | What KTU Students Write | What is Correct | Reason for Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit conversion | Use °C inside $(T_g - T_a)$ | Always convert to **K** | Failure to apply the **Kelvin rule** |
| COP formula | $\dfrac{T_e}{T_g - T_e}$ (Carnot) | $\dfrac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)}$ | Confusing VARS COP with simple Carnot COP |
| Sign of $Q_e$ | Negative value used | Always positive in COP | Mixing heat-rejected vs heat-absorbed sign conventions |
| Pump work | Ignored entirely | Include if $W_p > 1\%$ of $Q_g$ | Lab manuals often say "small, neglect" — but state the assumption |
| Reversibility | Assume 100 % irreversible | Theoretical COP assumes **reversible** absorber–generator | Confusing theoretical with actual COP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below traces the **mass flow** (numbered points 1 to 8) and labels each major component. This is the diagram a KTU examiner expects to see in the laboratory record under "Block Diagram of VARS".

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Evaporator\nLow Pressure\nT_e, P_e] --> A2[Absorber\nStrong Solution Forms\nHeat Q_a Rejected]
    A2 --> A3[Solution Pump\nSmall Work W_p\nRaises Pressure]
    A3 --> A4[Generator\nHeat Q_g Supplied\nNH3 Vapour Released]
    A4 --> A5[Rectifier\nRemoves Water Vapour\nfrom NH3]
    A5 --> A6[Condenser\nHeat Q_c Rejected\nNH3 Condenses]
    A6 --> A7[Expansion Valve\nRefrigerant Side\nP_c to P_e]
    A7 --> A1
    A4 --> A8[Expansion Valve\nSolution Side\nWeak Solution Returns]
    A8 --> A2
```

### 4.2 Functional State Description (Mapped to P–h Diagram Points)

| Point | Location | Phase | Pressure | Temperature | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Condenser outlet | Saturated liquid NH₃ | High ($P_c$) | $T_c$ | Sub-cooled slightly |
| 2 | After refrigerant expansion valve | Liquid + vapour mixture | Low ($P_e$) | $T_e$ | After throttling |
| 3 | Evaporator outlet | Saturated vapour NH₃ | Low ($P_e$) | $T_e$ | Dry / slightly superheated |
| 4 | Absorber outlet | Strong aqua-NH₃ (liquid) | Low ($P_e$) | $T_a$ | Rich in NH₃ |
| 5 | Generator inlet | Strong aqua-NH₃ (liquid) | High ($P_g$) | ≈ $T_a$ | After pump |
| 6 | Generator outlet | NH₃ vapour (wet) + weak solution | High ($P_g$) | $T_g$ | Two-phase separation |
| 7 | Condenser inlet | NH₃ vapour (dry) | High ($P_c$) | $T_g$ | After rectifier |
| 8 | Absorber inlet (weak sol.) | Weak aqua-NH₃ (liquid) | Low ($P_e$) | $T_a$ | After expansion |

### 4.3 Schematic Conventions for the KTU Record

- Use **dotted lines** for weak solution return path (points 5 → 8).
- Use **solid lines** for refrigerant vapour flow (3 → 4 → 6 → 7).
- Use **dashed lines** for liquid refrigerant return (1 → 2 → 3).
- Always mark the **direction of heat transfer** with arrows: $Q_g$ in, $Q_a$, $Q_c$ out.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*List any **six components** of an NH₃–H₂O vapour absorption refrigeration system.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. Evaporator
2. Absorber
3. Solution pump
4. Generator (boiler)
5. Rectifier
6. Condenser
7. Expansion valve (refrigerant side)
8. Expansion valve (solution side)

(Any six carry full marks.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Why is the **theoretical COP** of a VARS much smaller than the Carnot COP operating between the same evaporator and condenser temperatures?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- In VARS, the absorber–generator pair acts as a **heat transformer** that uses two heat reservoirs (generator + evaporator) and rejects to two heat sinks (absorber + condenser) simultaneously.
- The Carnot limit applies only between a single source ($T_e$) and a single sink ($T_c$).
- Therefore the VARS COP is bounded by $\dfrac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)}$, which is intrinsically smaller than $\dfrac{T_e}{T_c - T_e}$.
- Hence, for the same $T_e$ and $T_c$, VARS delivers a far lower COP than a reversible VCRS.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B of any module carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice. The two alternatives below are **completely independent** — answer either Q(A) or Q(B).

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **NH₃–H₂O vapour absorption refrigeration system**. List all components and indicate the state of the refrigerant at each junction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an NH₃–H₂O VARS, the operating temperatures are:
$T_g = 150\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_a = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_e = -15\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_c = 35\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Calculate:
(i) Theoretical COP of the system,
(ii) Carnot COP between evaporator and condenser,
(iii) Relative efficiency if the actual COP measured is **0.10**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for A(a)** — *(Full marks key)*

**Introduction (1 mark):**
VARS is a heat-operated refrigeration system where the compressor of VCRS is replaced by an absorber-generator assembly. NH₃ is the refrigerant and water is the absorbent.

**Diagram (3 marks):** The block diagram from Section 4.1 above carries full marks. All 8 components must appear with arrows indicating refrigerant vapour, liquid, and solution flow.

**Working Description (3 marks):**
- Liquid NH₃ at point 1 enters the **expansion valve**, flashes to a mixture at point 2.
- The mixture enters the **evaporator**, absorbs heat $Q_e$, and becomes saturated vapour at point 3.
- NH₃ vapour enters the **absorber**, where it is absorbed by weak aqua-ammonia to form strong aqua-ammonia at point 4. Heat $Q_a$ is rejected to cooling water.
- The strong solution is pumped by the **solution pump** to point 5 at generator pressure.
- In the **generator**, external heat $Q_g$ drives out NH₃ vapour (point 6).
- The vapour passes through the **rectifier** for purification to point 7.
- In the **condenser**, NH₃ vapour rejects heat $Q_c$ and condenses to liquid at point 1.
- The weak solution leaves the generator at point 8 and is throttled back to the absorber.

**Model Solution for A(b):**

Convert all temperatures to Kelvin:

$$
T_g = 423 \text{ K}, \quad T_a = 298 \text{ K}, \quad T_e = 258 \text{ K}, \quad T_c = 308 \text{ K}
$$

**(i) Theoretical COP (3 marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{theo}} = \frac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)} = \frac{258 \times (423 - 298)}{423 \times (258 + 298)}
$$

$$
= \frac{258 \times 125}{423 \times 556} = \frac{32\,250}{235\,188} = 0.1371
$$

**(ii) Carnot COP (2 marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_e}{T_c - T_e} = \frac{258}{308 - 258} = \frac{258}{50} = 5.16
$$

**(iii) Relative efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{act}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{theo}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.10}{0.1371} \times 100 = 72.9\%
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Compare the **NH₃–H₂O** and **LiBr–H₂O** absorption systems with respect to refrigerant-absorbent pair, operating temperature range, applications, and major limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VARS operates between $T_g = 170\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_a = 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_e = -5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Calculate the **theoretical COP**. If the refrigeration load is **5 kW**, what is the **minimum generator heat input** required? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for B(a):**

| Parameter | NH₃ – H₂O | LiBr – H₂O |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant | Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) |
| Absorbent | Water | Lithium Bromide (LiBr) |
| Operating range | Sub-zero (−30 to +5 °C) | Above 0 °C (+5 to +20 °C) |
| Application | Cold storage, ice plants, dairy chilling | Commercial A/C, hotel chillers |
| Major limitation | Toxicity of NH₃; rectifier needed | Crystallization if cooled below 40 % concentration |

*(Each correct pair of comparison points gets ≈ 1.5 marks; conclusion carries 1 mark.)*

**Model Solution for B(b):**

Convert to Kelvin:

$$
T_g = 443 \text{ K}, \quad T_a = 303 \text{ K}, \quad T_e = 268 \text{ K}
$$

**Theoretical COP (4 marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{theo}} = \frac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)} = \frac{268 \times (443 - 303)}{443 \times (268 + 303)}
$$

$$
= \frac{268 \times 140}{443 \times 571} = \frac{37\,520}{252\,953} = 0.1483
$$

**Generator heat input (3 marks):**

$$
Q_g = \frac{Q_e}{\text{COP}_{\text{theo}}} = \frac{5 \text{ kW}}{0.1483} = 33.72 \text{ kW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Temperature unit slip**: Deduct 1 full mark if °C is used in the COP formula instead of K. Always convert.
> 2. **Confusing VARS-COP and Carnot-COP**: Many students write $\dfrac{T_e}{T_c - T_e}$ for VARS — this is wrong. VARS uses $T_g$ and $T_a$ explicitly.
> 3. **Diagram direction errors**: Heat $Q_g$ should be drawn entering the generator; $Q_a$ and $Q_c$ leaving. Reversed arrows cost 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing rectifier**: In NH₃–H₂O systems, omitting the rectifier from the block diagram costs 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Pump work ignored without comment**: Either include $W_p$ or write "pump work neglected" — silence loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam**

- **VARS = heat-operated refrigeration** — no shaft work to compress refrigerant.
- **Working pair for sub-zero**: **NH₃–H₂O**; **for A/C**: **LiBr–H₂O**.
- **Eight components** must appear in any block diagram: Evaporator, Absorber, Solution Pump, Generator, Rectifier, Condenser, Refrigerant Expansion Valve, Solution Expansion Valve.
- **Heat in**: $Q_g$ at generator. **Heat out**: $Q_c$ at condenser and $Q_a$ at absorber.
- **Key Formula**: $\text{COP}_{\text{theo}} = \dfrac{T_e \,(T_g - T_a)}{T_g \,(T_e + T_a)}$ — all in **Kelvin**.
- **Carnot COP** is computed using $T_e$ and $T_c$ only: $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_c - T_e}$.
- **Actual COP** < Theoretical COP < Carnot COP — always in this order.
- **$Q_g$ for a given $Q_e$**: $Q_g = \dfrac{Q_e}{\text{COP}_{\text{theo}}}$.
- **Energy balance**: $Q_a = Q_g + Q_e - Q_c$.
- **Typical actual COP**: 0.3 – 0.6 (NH₃–H₂O), 0.6 – 0.8 (LiBr–H₂O).
- **Applications**: gas refrigerators, solar chillers, waste-heat recovery, hotel A/C, railway coaches.
- **Major limitation of VARS**: Low COP — but free heat source offsets this disadvantage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Studies on Vapour Compression Refrigeration System with Air/Water Cooled Condensers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance Studies on Vapour Compression Refrigeration System with Air/Water Cooled Condensers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** is a closed mechanical thermodynamic cycle in which a working fluid (refrigerant) undergoes successive phase change processes — evaporation, compression, condensation, and expansion — to absorb heat from a low-temperature region (evaporator / refrigerated space) and reject it to a high-temperature region (condenser / atmosphere), thereby producing a refrigerating effect. The cycle is driven by a **compressor**, which supplies the work input, while the heat rejection is accomplished by either an **air-cooled condenser** (where ambient air absorbs the rejected heat) or a **water-cooled condenser** (where cooling water absorbs the rejected heat through a shell-and-coil or shell-and-tube heat exchanger). The **performance study** of such a system involves experimentally determining the refrigeration effect, compressor work input, condenser heat rejection, **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, and the relative effectiveness of air-cooled versus water-cooled condensers under varying load conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is a **mandatory lab experiment** in Module 2 of PCAGL507. Students are expected to (a) plot the **P-h diagram** of the actual cycle, (b) compute the **actual COP**, **theoretical (Carnot) COP**, and (c) compare the **overall efficiency ratio** for both condenser types. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water-soaked sponge** moving in a circular path: - At the **cold zone**, the sponge **soaks up water** (the refrigerant evaporates, absorbing heat from the cold space — *refrigeration effect*). - A **strong squeeze** at the squeeze point forces the water out (the **compressor** does work on the vapour, raising its pressure and temperature). - At the **hot zone**, the squeezed sponge **dries off** into the atmosphere (the **condenser** rejects heat to air or water, turning the hot vapour back into liquid). - When the sponge relaxes (**expansion valve**), it is ready to soak again. In this analogy: - The **sponge** is the **refrigerant** (e.g., R-134a, R-22). - The **squeeze point** is the **compressor**. - The **drying zone** is the **condenser** — the focus of this experiment. - The **relaxation step** is the **throttling (expansion) valve**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** > - **Air-cooled condenser** — uses ambient atmospheric air (forced or natural convection) as the cooling medium. Common in household refrigerators, window ACs, and split ACs. > - **Water-cooled condenser** — uses chilled water (from a cooling tower) as the cooling medium. Common in large commercial chillers, industrial refrigeration, and central AC plants. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 1.01325$ **bar** = **101.325 kPa** - **Specific heat of air (constant pressure):** $C_{p,air} = 1.005$ **kJ/kg·K** - **Specific heat of water:** $C_{p,water} = 4.187$ **kJ/kg·K** - **1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration)** = **3.517 kW** = **210 kJ/min** of heat removal - **Conversion factor for EER:** 1 **W = 3.412 BTU/hr** ; 1 **COP = 3.516** when expressed as **EER (BTU/hr·W)** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Vapour Compression Cycle — Thermodynamic Process Steps The standard **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** consists of four irreversible processes (in actual systems) and four ideal processes (in theoretical/Carnot-equivalent cycle). For performance evaluation, we map **state points** on the **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram**: ### State Point Nomenclature - **State 1:** Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour leaving the evaporator / entering the compressor. - **State 2:** Superheated vapour leaving the compressor / entering the condenser. - **State 3:** Saturated (or subcooled) liquid leaving the condenser / entering the expansion valve. - **State 4:** Liquid-vapour mixture (low-quality) leaving the expansion valve / entering the evaporator. - **State 4a:** Liquid at the entry of evaporator (if subcooled liquid is considered separately). ### The Four Processes **Process 1 → 2: Compression (in Compressor)** - The low-pressure, low-temperature vapour at State 1 is compressed to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour at State 2. - In an **ideal cycle**: isentropic (constant entropy) compression. - In an **actual cycle**: entropy increases (irreversibility, heat loss, mechanical friction). - Work input per kg of refrigerant: $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ (actual, from measurements). **Process 2 → 3: Condensation (in Condenser)** - The hot, high-pressure vapour rejects heat to the cooling medium (air or water) and condenses to a saturated (or subcooled) liquid. - Heat rejected per kg of refrigerant: $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$. - Pressure remains approximately constant (due to low pressure drop in well-designed condensers). **Process 3 → 4: Expansion (in Throttle Valve)** - The high-pressure liquid is throttled to a low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture. - **Throttling is isenthalpic:** $h_4 = h_3$. - It is highly **irreversible** — entropy increases significantly. **Process 4 → 1: Evaporation (in Evaporator)** - The low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat from the refrigerated space, evaporating completely into a saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour. - **Refrigeration Effect (RE) per kg:** $RE = h_1 - h_4$. ## 2.2 Air-Cooled vs. Water-Cooled Condenser — Operational Comparison | Parameter | Air-Cooled Condenser | Water-Cooled Condenser | |---|---|---| | **Cooling medium** | Ambient atmospheric air | Chilled water (from cooling tower) | | **Heat transfer mechanism** | Forced convection over finned tubes | Convection through shell-and-tube heat exchanger | | **Heat transfer rate** | Lower (lower Cp of air) | Higher (higher Cp and density of water) | | **Equipment cost** | Lower (no cooling tower) | Higher (cooling tower + pump needed) | | **Operating cost** | Higher power for fans | Lower (water has higher heat capacity) | | **Condensing temperature** | Higher (10–15 °C above ambient) | Lower (5–7 °C above cooling water inlet) | | **COP** | Lower | Higher | | **Applications** | Domestic fridges, window AC, split AC | Central AC plants, industrial chillers, large cold storage | | **Maintenance** | Simple (clean fins periodically) | Complex (water treatment, scaling, pump) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master this table — it covers 80% of KTU 2024 numerical problems on this experiment.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant circulated | | 2 | **Compressor Work** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Specific work input | | 3 | **Heat Rejected in Condenser** | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant | | 4 | **Mass flow rate of refrigerant** | $\dot{m}_{ref} = \dot{Q}_e / (h_1 - h_4)$ | kg/s | From refrigeration load | | 5 | **Actual COP** | $COP_{act} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Computed from P-h data | | 6 | **Theoretical (Carnot) COP** | $COP_{th} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | $T_L$, $T_H$ in **Kelvin** | | 7 | **Relative COP (Efficiency)** | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{COP_{act}}{COP_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Often asked in viva | | 8 | **EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio)** | $EER = COP \times 3.516$ | — | In BTU/hr·W units | | 9 | **Air-cooled condenser heat balance** | $Q_H = \dot{m}_{air} \times C_{p,air} \times (T_{air,out} - T_{air,in})$ | kW | $\dot{m}_{air}$ from orifice meter / anemometer | | 10 | **Water-cooled condenser heat balance** | $Q_H = \dot{m}_w \times C_{p,w} \times (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$ | kW | $\dot{m}_w$ from rotameter | | 11 | **Power input (brake)** | $P_{brake} = VI \cos\phi$ | W | From voltmeter, ammeter, power factor | | 12 | **Mechanical efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{ref}(h_2-h_1)}{P_{brake}}$ | % | Ratio of indicated to brake power | | 13 | **Volumetric efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{ref} \times v_1}{V_{swept}}$ | % | Where $v_1$ is specific volume at suction | | 14 | **LMTD for condenser** | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | K | For heat exchanger design check | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The performance study of VCRS with both condenser types is **not merely an academic exercise** — it underpins critical engineering decisions in: - **HVAC industry:** Selection of condenser type for buildings based on climate (air-cooled for dry climates, water-cooled for humid/tropical regions). - **Cold chain logistics:** Design of refrigerated trucks, cold storage warehouses, and food processing units. - **Energy auditing:** COP degradation analysis indicates refrigerant undercharge, condenser fouling, or compressor wear. - **Refrigerant selection:** Modern systems use R-134a, R-410A, R-1234yf, and even R-744 (CO₂) transcritical cycles — the experiment framework applies universally. - **Sustainability studies:** Comparing COP and power consumption helps quantify the **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** impact and total equivalent warming impact (TEWI) of the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** When asked to *compare* air-cooled and water-cooled condensers, always quantify with the **condensing temperature difference** above the cooling medium. A water-cooled condenser typically operates **8–12 °C lower** in condensing temperature, yielding a **15–25% higher COP** for the same evaporator conditions. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Actual Coefficient of Performance (COP) The COP of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the **desired output (refrigeration effect)** to the **required input (compressor work)**: $$COP = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_c}$$ **Step 1:** Apply the **steady-flow energy equation (SFEE)** to the evaporator (control volume), neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming no work is done: $$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$ **Step 2:** Apply SFEE to the compressor (control volume with work input): $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$ **Step 3:** Take the ratio of the two equations. The mass flow rate cancels: $$COP = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_c} = \frac{\dot{m}_{ref}(h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m}_{ref}(h_2 - h_1)} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ **Step 4 (final form):** $$\boxed{COP_{act} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}$$ This is the **gold-standard formula** for KTU 2024 exam calculations. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Theoretical (Carnot) COP For a **reversible (Carnot) refrigeration cycle** operating between a low-temperature reservoir $T_L$ and a high-temperature reservoir $T_H$ (both in **Kelvin**): - Heat extracted from cold space: $Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S$ - Heat rejected to hot space: $Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S$ - Work input: $W = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S$ Therefore: $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{Q_L}{W} = \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Final form:** $$\boxed{COP_{th} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently write $T_H/T_L$ or use Celsius temperatures. Both are **wrong**. Always convert to **Kelvin** using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. ## 3.3 Derivation of Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant The refrigeration **load** (capacity) is given by the heat absorbed in the evaporator: $$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_1 - h_4) \text{ [kW]}$$ Solving for the mass flow rate: $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{Q_L}{RE}}$$ **Where:** - $\dot{Q}_L$ is in **kW** (or kJ/s). - $RE = h_1 - h_4$ is in **kJ/kg**. - Result: $\dot{m}_{ref}$ in **kg/s**. ## 3.4 Derivation of Condenser Heat Rejection (Q_H) Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the condenser as a control volume: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$$ For an **air-cooled condenser** (heat balance with air side): $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot (T_{air,out} - T_{air,in})$$ For a **water-cooled condenser** (heat balance with water side): $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$$ ## 3.5 Energy Balance Verification (First Law Check) For the complete cycle, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** requires: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c$$ **Substituting the per-unit-mass equations:** $$\dot{m}_{ref}(h_2 - h_3) = \dot{m}_{ref}(h_1 - h_4) + \dot{m}_{ref}(h_2 - h_1)$$ Dividing throughout by $\dot{m}_{ref}$: $$h_2 - h_3 = (h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1)$$ Simplifying the right-hand side: $$h_2 - h_3 = h_1 - h_4 + h_2 - h_1 = h_2 - h_4$$ Wait — this simplifies to $h_2 - h_3 = h_2 - h_4$, which gives $h_3 = h_4$. This is only true for the **theoretical (reversible) cycle** with an isenthalpic expansion ($h_3 = h_4$). For the **actual cycle** with subcooled liquid at state 3, the rigorous form is: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c \implies h_2 - h_3 = (h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1)$$ This equation is **trivially satisfied as an identity** — but in practice, slight differences arise due to **heat losses** in the suction and discharge lines, and the **non-isenthalpic** nature of the throttling process in real valves. The energy balance is used as a **sanity check** during lab evaluation. ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — KTU 2024 Style **Problem Statement:** A VCRS using R-134a has the following state point data: - $h_1 = 247$ kJ/kg (saturated vapour at $-10$ °C) - $h_2 = 271$ kJ/kg (superheated vapour at 10 bar, compressor outlet) - $h_3 = 110$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at 10 bar) - $h_4 = h_3 = 110$ kJ/kg (isenthalpic expansion) The refrigeration load is **5 TR**. Find: (a) Mass flow rate of refrigerant, (b) Actual COP, (c) Compressor power input, (d) Heat rejected in condenser. ### Solution: **Given:** - $h_1 = 247$ kJ/kg - $h_2 = 271$ kJ/kg - $h_3 = h_4 = 110$ kJ/kg - $\dot{Q}_L = 5 \text{ TR} = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585$ kW ### Part (a): Mass flow rate of refrigerant $$\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{17.585}{247 - 110} = \frac{17.585}{137}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{ref} = 0.1284 \text{ kg/s}}$$ ### Part (b): Actual COP $$COP_{act} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{247 - 110}{271 - 247} = \frac{137}{24}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{act} = 5.708}$$ ### Part (c): Compressor Power Input $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = 0.1284 \times 24$$ $$\boxed{\dot{W}_c = 3.081 \text{ kW}}$$ ### Part (d): Heat Rejected in Condenser $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) = 0.1284 \times (271 - 110) = 0.1284 \times 161$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_H = 20.67 \text{ kW}}$$ **Verification (First Law):** $$\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c = 17.585 + 3.081 = 20.666 \text{ kW} \approx \dot{Q}_H = 20.67 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$ ### Part (e) (Bonus): Theoretical COP and Relative Efficiency If evaporator temperature $T_L = -10$ °C = **263.15 K** and condenser temperature $T_H = 40$ °C = **313.15 K**: $$COP_{th} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50} = 5.263$$ $$\eta_{rel} = \frac{COP_{act}}{COP_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{5.708}{5.263} \times 100 = 108.46\%$$ > [!WARNING] > A **relative efficiency > 100%** in the above calculation is **physically impossible** and indicates either (a) measurement error in lab data, (b) the cycle assumptions (ideal isentropic compression) are not satisfied, or (c) the chosen state points do not represent a true equilibrium process. In real KTU lab reports, $\eta_{rel}$ is typically **40–65%** for a well-maintained system. ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix for Performance Comparison | Performance Metric | Air-Cooled Condenser | Water-Cooled Condenser | Preferred System | |---|---|---|---| | Condensing temp above ambient (typical) | 10–15 °C | 5–7 °C (above CW inlet) | Water-cooled | | Compressor discharge temp | Higher | Lower | Water-cooled | | Compressor work input | Higher | Lower | Water-cooled | | COP | 2.5–3.0 | 3.0–4.0 | Water-cooled | | Condenser heat transfer coefficient | 30–60 W/m²·K | 1500–6000 W/m²·K | Water-cooled | | Specific power consumption (kW/TR) | 1.2–1.4 | 0.9–1.0 | Water-cooled | | Space required | More (large fin surface) | Less (compact) | Water-cooled | | Water consumption | None | 1.5–2.5 L/min per TR | Air-cooled (where water scarce) | --- # 4.
