KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. Why is its value always greater than unity?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Model Answer:** The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the **refrigerating effect (heat extracted from the cold space)** to the **work input to the compressor**. $$COP = \frac{Q_L}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ **Why COP > 1:** - A refrigerator does **not convert work into heat** (like a heater does). Instead, it uses work to *move* heat from a cold region to a hot region. - The heat extracted ($Q_L$) is **larger** than the work supplied ($W_c$) because the system is also *rejecting* an additional $Q_H$ to the hot side. - By energy balance: $Q_H = Q_L + W_c$, hence $Q_L = Q_H - W_c$. - For a typical refrigerator, $Q_L \approx 2$ to $5 \times W_c$, giving $COP \approx 2$ to $5$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Tip:** Always conclude with the working-fluid statement: "The work supplied is used to upgrade the energy from low to high temperature — it is **not destroyed** in the process." ### **Q2. Compare air-cooled and water-cooled condensers on the basis of COP, application, and cooling medium.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Air-Cooled Condenser | Water-Cooled Condenser | |---|---|---| | **Cooling medium** | Ambient air (forced/natural draft) | Cooling water (chilled) | | **COP** | Lower (2.5–3.0) | Higher (3.0–4.0) | | **Condensing temperature** | Higher (10–15 °C above ambient) | Lower (5–7 °C above CW inlet) | | **Application** | Household fridges, window AC, split AC | Central AC plants, industrial chillers | | **Cost** | Lower initial cost, higher running cost | Higher initial (cooling tower), lower running | | **Maintenance** | Periodic cleaning of fins | Water treatment, scale removal, pump service | | **Water consumption** | None | 1.5–2.5 L/min per TR | **Conclusion:** Water-cooled condensers are preferred where **continuous water supply** is available and **high COP** is critical. Air-cooled condensers dominate in **domestic and small commercial** applications. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 or 5 + 5 + 4). The student has **internal choice** — must answer **ONE out of TWO**. ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 2 ESE]` **Q. A(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **P-h diagram**, explain the working of a **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)**. List the assumptions made in the analysis of the **ideal Vapour Compression Cycle (VCC)**. **Model Answer:** A VCRS uses a circulating refrigerant as the working fluid which undergoes a **closed thermodynamic cycle** to transfer heat from a low-temperature body (cold space) to a high-temperature body (atmosphere). **P-h Diagram Description (5 marks):** The cycle is represented on a **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram** with the following state points: - **State 1:** Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour at the **evaporator exit / compressor inlet**, at low pressure $P_L$ and low temperature $T_L$. - **State 2:** Superheated vapour at the **compressor exit / condenser inlet**, at high pressure $P_H$ and high temperature $T_H$. - **State 3:** Saturated (or subcooled) liquid at the **condenser exit / expansion valve inlet**, at high pressure $P_H$. - **State 4:** Low-quality, low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture at the **expansion valve exit / evaporator inlet**, at low pressure $P_L$. **Processes:** - **1 → 2:** Isentropic compression (in actual cycle, with entropy increase). - **2 → 3:** Isobaric heat rejection in the condenser. - **3 → 4:** Isenthalpic throttling in the expansion valve. - **4 → 1:** Isobaric heat absorption in the evaporator. **Assumptions of Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle (2 marks):** 1. Compression is **isentropic** (no internal irreversibilities). 2. Pressure drops in the **connecting pipelines** and **heat exchangers** are negligible. 3. Refrigerant leaves the **condenser as a saturated liquid** (no subcooling). 4. Refrigerant leaves the **evaporator as a saturated vapour** (no superheating). 5. Throttling is a **purely isenthalpic** process. 6. The compression process is **steady-flow** and **adiabatic** (no heat loss). 7. Refrigerant behaves as an **ideal gas in the superheated region**. **Q. A(b) [7 Marks]** In a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System, the following readings are observed: - Suction pressure = **2.5 bar**, Suction temperature = **5 °C** - Discharge pressure = **12 bar**, Discharge temperature = **70 °C** - Condenser exit temperature = **40 °C** - Refrigerant used: **R-134a** - Cooling water flow rate (water-cooled condenser) = **0.5 kg/s** - Cooling water inlet temperature = **25 °C** - Compressor electrical input = **2.0 kW** (at PF = 0.85) Calculate: 1. Refrigerating effect per kg 2. Mass flow rate of refrigerant 3. Actual COP 4. Heat rejected in condenser (from refrigerant side) 5. Heat absorbed by cooling water (verify with energy balance) **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Identify enthalpies from R-134a property tables** - At **2.5 bar and 5 °C** (superheated vapour, since saturation temp at 2.5 bar ≈ 1.25 °C): $h_1 = 252.4$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 0.922$ kJ/kg·K - At **12 bar and 70 °C** (superheated vapour): $h_2 = 285.2$ kJ/kg - At **12 bar and 40 °C** (saturated liquid, since $T_{sat}$ at 12 bar ≈ 46.3 °C, so this is **subcooled liquid**): $h_3 \approx 121.3$ kJ/kg (approx. using saturated liquid at 40 °C = 6.5 bar, but adjusted for actual state) $h_3 = 121.0$ kJ/kg - $h_4 = h_3 = 121.0$ kJ/kg (isenthalpic throttling) **Step 2: Refrigerating effect per kg** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 252.4 - 121.0 = 131.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3: Mass flow rate** First, calculate compressor indicated work: $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (285.2 - 252.4) = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot 32.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Assuming electrical input ≈ compressor work (ignoring motor losses for simplicity): $$\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{2.0}{32.8} = 0.0610 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 4: Actual COP** $$COP_{act} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{131.4}{32.8} = 4.007$$ **Step 5: Heat rejected in condenser (refrigerant side)** $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) = 0.0610 \times (285.2 - 121.0) = 0.0610 \times 164.2 = 10.02 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6: Heat absorbed by cooling water (assuming $\Delta T_w$ is measured)** We need $T_{w,out}$. Using $\dot{Q}_H$ from refrigerant side and equating it to water side: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$$ $$10.02 = 0.5 \times 4.187 \times (T_{w,out} - 25)$$ $$T_{w,out} - 25 = \frac{10.02}{2.0935} = 4.786 \text{ °C}$$ $$\boxed{T_{w,out} = 29.79 \text{ °C}}$$ **Step 7: Energy balance verification** $$\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c = \dot{m}_{ref}(h_1 - h_4) + \dot{W}_c$$ $$\dot{Q}_L = 0.0610 \times 131.4 = 8.01 \text{ kW (refrigeration capacity)}$$ $$\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c = 8.01 + 2.0 = 10.01 \text{ kW} \approx \dot{Q}_H = 10.02 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$ **Result Table:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Refrigerating effect (RE) | 131.4 kJ/kg | | Mass flow rate | 0.0610 kg/s | | Actual COP | 4.007 | | Condenser heat rejection (refrigerant) | 10.02 kW | | Cooling water outlet temperature | 29.79 °C | | Refrigeration capacity | 8.01 kW (≈ 2.28 TR) | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 2 ESE]` **Q. B(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **experimental procedure** to determine the COP of a VCRS with an **air-cooled condenser**. List the **instruments used** and the **observations recorded**. **Model Answer:** **Experimental Setup (3 marks):** The test rig consists of: - A **hermetic compressor** (1–5 TR capacity) with R-134a or R-22 refrigerant. - An **air-cooled condenser** (finned tube type) with a forced-draft fan and louvered coil. - A **thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)** for throttling. - A **direct expansion (DX) evaporator** housed in a **cold chamber** fitted with an **electrical heater** (variable load). - A **control panel** with pressure gauges, temperature indicators (thermocouples or RTDs), voltmeter, ammeter, power factor meter, and anemometer. - A **digital power meter** for direct wattage measurement. **Experimental Procedure (3 marks):** 1. **Pre-checks:** Verify refrigerant charge, ensure all valves are in the correct position, check electrical connections. 2. **Start-up:** Switch on the compressor and let the system run for **15–20 minutes** to attain steady-state. 3. **Load application:** Set the heater in the cold chamber to a **constant power input** (e.g., 1 kW, 2 kW, etc.) to simulate the thermal load. 4. **Steady-state waiting:** Allow **10–15 minutes** for the system to reach a new steady state. 5. **Observation recording:** Note down: - **Low-side (suction) pressure** $P_L$ from LP gauge. - **High-side (discharge) pressure** $P_H$ from HP gauge. - **Temperatures** at all seven state points: $T_1$ (compressor inlet), $T_2$ (compressor outlet), $T_3$ (condenser outlet), $T_4$ (evaporator inlet), $T_5$ (air inlet to condenser), $T_6$ (air outlet from condenser), $T_7$ (cold chamber temperature). - **Air velocity** at condenser outlet using anemometer; calculate mass flow rate. - **Voltage, current, and power factor** from the panel; compute power input. 6. **Load variation:** Repeat steps 3–5 for **at least 3–4 different load settings** (e.g., 0.5 kW, 1 kW, 1.5 kW, 2 kW). 7. **Shutdown:** Switch off heater, allow system to run for 5 min to stabilize, then switch off compressor. **Instruments Used (1 mark):** - Bourdon-type pressure gauges (LP: 0–10 bar; HP: 0–30 bar) - K-type or J-type thermocouples with 7-channel digital indicator - Anemometer (hot-wire or vane type) - Digital clamp meter / power analyzer - Stopwatch, flow meter (if water-cooled) **Q. B(b) [7 Marks]** A VCRS with an **air-cooled condenser** gives the following steady-state readings at one operating condition: - Refrigeration load = **3.5 kW** - Compressor power input = **1.4 kW** - Condenser air inlet temperature = **30 °C** - Condenser air outlet temperature = **42 °C** - Air velocity at condenser outlet = **5 m/s** - Condenser face area = **0.4 m² × 0.4 m² = 0.16 m²** Calculate: 1. Actual COP 2. Mass flow rate of air 3. Heat rejected in condenser (from air-side) 4. Compare the two heat rejection values and comment on the **discrepancy**, if any. **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Actual COP** $$COP_{act} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_c} = \frac{3.5}{1.4} = 2.5$$ **Step 2: Mass flow rate of air** Air density at the average temperature $T_{avg} = (30 + 42)/2 = 36$ °C = **309 K**: $\rho_{air} = P/(RT) = 101325/(287 \times 309) = 1.143 \text{ kg/m}^3$ Volumetric flow rate: $\dot{V} = A \times v = 0.16 \times 5 = 0.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1.143 \times 0.8 = 0.914 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 3: Heat rejected (air-side)** $$\dot{Q}_{H,air} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) = 0.914 \times 1.005 \times (42 - 30) = 0.914 \times 1.005 \times 12$$ $$\dot{Q}_{H,air} = 11.02 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Heat rejected (refrigerant-side, by First Law)** $$\dot{Q}_{H,ref} = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c = 3.5 + 1.4 = 4.9 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Compare and comment** **Discrepancy:** $\dot{Q}_{H,air} = 11.02$ kW, but $\dot{Q}_{H,ref} = 4.9$ kW. The air-side heat gain is **much higher** than the refrigerant-side heat loss. **Reasons for discrepancy:** 1. **Air by-pass and leakage:** Not all the air flowing through the anemometer plane passes through the condenser coil — a significant portion by-passes. 2. **Heat ingress from surroundings:** The fan motor and compressor dump additional heat into the air stream. 3. **Moisture condensation:** If the condenser surface is below dew point, latent heat from water vapour condensation adds to the air-side heat. 4. **Measurement errors:** Anemometer placement, temperature probe location, and non-uniform velocity profile. 5. **Heat loss from refrigerant lines** to the surroundings in the cold chamber. > [!NOTE] > **Comment for KTU Viva:** This discrepancy is **expected in lab conditions** and is typically **5–25%** in a well-designed experiment. The air-side measurement is often **higher** than the refrigerant-side due to parasitic heat loads, not because of energy conservation violation. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP MARKS-LOSING PITFALLS — Avoid These in KTU 2024 ESE:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Never mix **kJ/kg** enthalpies with **kW** power. Always compute mass flow rate in **kg/s** when power is in **kW** (since 1 kW = 1 kJ/s). > > 2. **Temperature in Kelvin vs Celsius:** Theoretical COP formula requires **absolute temperature (K)**. Writing $COP = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ with $T_L = -10$ °C (without conversion) is a **silly mistake** that costs 2–3 marks. > > 3. **Forgetting isenthalpic throttling:** Writing $h_3 \neq h_4$ is wrong. The throttling process is **isenthalpic**: $h_4 = h_3$ **always**. > > 4. **Sign convention confusion:** In energy balance, ensure $Q_H$ is the **heat rejected** (positive out of the refrigerant). Some students erroneously compute $Q_H = h_3 - h_2$ (negative), which leads to wrong energy balance. > > 5. **Not citing assumptions of the ideal cycle** in Part A or short notes: Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** specifically for stating the assumptions of the theoretical Vapour Compression Cycle. > > 6. **Skipping the P-h diagram:** For 7-mark questions, the P-h diagram carries **at least 3 marks**. A neat, labelled diagram is **non-negotiable**. > > 7. **Reporting Relative COP > 100% without comment:** This is **physically impossible**. Always state the source of error (heat loss, assumption violation, measurement error) in lab records. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 Module 2, Experiment 2** ### 🔑 Core Concept Recap - ✅ VCRS uses a circulating refrigerant to move heat from cold to hot by consuming **work**. - ✅ Four state points: **1** (saturated vapour, compressor inlet) → **2** (superheated vapour, compressor outlet) → **3** (saturated/subcooled liquid, condenser outlet) → **4** (two-phase mixture, evaporator inlet). - ✅ Four processes: **Compression** (1→2) → **Condensation** (2→3) → **Throttling** (3→4, $h_4 = h_3$) → **Evaporation** (4→1). ### 🔑 Golden Formulas - ✅ **COP** $= (h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$ - ✅ **Carnot COP** $= T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ — **always in Kelvin!** - ✅ **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** $= h_1 - h_4$ (kJ/kg) - ✅ **Compressor Work** $= h_2 - h_1$ (kJ/kg) - ✅ **Condenser Heat Rejection** $= h_2 - h_3$ (kJ/kg) - ✅ **Mass flow rate** $\dot{m}_{ref} = \dot{Q}_L/(h_1 - h_4)$ - ✅ **EER** $= COP \times 3.516$ - ✅ **1 TR** $= 3.517$ kW ### 🔑 Air-Cooled vs. Water-Cooled - ✅ **Air-cooled:** Higher condensing temperature, **lower COP**, simpler design, **no water consumption**, domestic use. - ✅ **Water-cooled:** Lower condensing temperature, **higher COP** (~15–25% more), requires cooling tower + pump, commercial/industrial use. ### 🔑 Experimental Know-How - ✅ State points found using **pressure gauges + temperature probes** and R-134a/R-22 **property tables** (saturated and superheated). - ✅ **LP gauge** reads suction side, **HP gauge** reads discharge side. - ✅ For air-cooled: **anemometer + air temperatures** give $Q_H$ from air side. - ✅ For water-cooled: **rotameter + water temperatures** give $Q_H$ from water side. - ✅ **First Law check:** $Q_H \approx Q_L + W_c$. Discrepancy < 25% is acceptable. ### 🔑 Common Viva Questions - ✅ *"Why is subcooling beneficial?"* — Increases refrigeration effect by 1–3%. - ✅ *"Why is superheating undesirable?"* — Increases compressor work without increasing RE (if > required for safe compression). - ✅ *"What is the effect of condenser fouling?"* — Raises condensing temperature → lowers COP. - ✅ *"How is a TXV better than a capillary tube?"* — Maintains constant superheat, adapts to load variation. - ✅ *"Why use R-134a instead of R-12?"* — R-12 has **high ODP** (ozone depletion potential); R-134a is **ODP = 0**. ### 🔑 Mandatory P-h Diagram Items - ✅ Saturated liquid and vapour curves. - ✅ Four state points clearly marked with $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$. - ✅ Direction of cycle indicated by arrows. - ✅ $P_H$ and $P_L$ lines drawn and labelled. - ✅ $T_H$, $T_L$ isotherms identified. > **Final Note:** Mastering this experiment gives you **direct entry into questions on refrigeration cycle, COP, condenser comparison, and First Law verification** — three of the most frequently tested topics in KTU Mechanical Engineering streams and GATE-equivalent examinations. ---

topics.title: Studies on Air Distribution Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Studies on Air Distribution Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Distribution System (ADS):** An engineered network of ducts, plenums, terminal devices (diffusers, grilles, registers), dampers, fans, and accessories designed to deliver conditioned air (cooled, heated, dehumidified, or ventilated) from the Air Handling Unit (AHU) to various occupied zones of a building in a controlled, uniform, and energy-efficient manner, while simultaneously extracting return air back to the central plant.

In the context of the **KTU PCAGL507 Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Laboratory**, "Studies on Air Distribution Systems" is a **Module Experiment 2** investigation in which the student physically studies the behaviour of air as it flows through ducts, measures the **static, velocity, and total pressures** at strategic locations, evaluates **friction losses**, and analyses the **throw, drop, and spread** patterns of various supply/return terminal devices.

### 1.2 Classification of Air Distribution Systems

The study of air distribution is broadly categorised into two high-level families based on the medium of transport:

1. **All-Air Systems** – Conditioned air alone acts as the cooling/heating medium. Sub-categories include:
   - **Single Duct System** (constant volume / variable volume)
   - **Dual Duct System** (hot deck + cold deck)
   - **Multi-Zone System**
   - **Terminal Reheat System**
   - **Induction System**
   - **Fan-Coil + Central Plant Hybrids (debatable, but contextually used)**
2. **Air-Water Systems** – Air and water share the load (e.g., primary air + chilled water induction units).
3. **All-Water Systems** – Only water is circulated (e.g., fan coil units, chilled beam ceilings).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For the KTU PCAGL507 lab, the experiment specifically targets **all-air low-velocity (conventional) and high-velocity (dual-duct) systems** along with terminal device performance, duct pressure traverse, and air-change effectiveness.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Building is a Living Body" Analogy**
> Imagine the human circulatory system:
> - The **Heart** = the **AHU / Fan** that pumps the fluid.
> - The **Arteries** = the **supply ducts** that carry oxygenated (conditioned) air to organs (rooms).
> - The **Capillaries** = the **diffusers and grilles** that deliver the air precisely where it is needed.
> - The **Veins** = the **return ducts** bringing stale air back to the heart.
> - **Blood Pressure** = **Static Pressure** of air in the ducts.
> - **Blood Velocity** = **Air Velocity** through the duct cross-section.
>
> Just as a cardiologist measures pressure and flow to diagnose blockages, an HVAC engineer measures **static, velocity, and total pressure** in ducts to detect **friction losses** (the "furring" of the arteries) and ensure every room gets an equal share of conditioned air.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Magnitude (HVAC Practice) | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Density of air (standard) | $\rho$ | **1.20** | kg/m³ |
| Specific heat of air (constant pressure) | $C_p$ | **1.005** | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific volume of air (standard) | $v$ | **0.834** | m³/kg |
| Air-handling temperature range | $T_{db}$ | **13 – 32** | °C |
| Recommended supply duct velocity (low-velocity) | $V$ | **5 – 9** | m/s |
| Recommended supply duct velocity (high-velocity) | $V$ | **15 – 25** | m/s |
| Static pressure in ductwork | $P_s$ | **25 – 250** | Pa |
| Round equivalent diameter factor | – | **1.30** (for rectangular ducts) | – |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of an Air Distribution Network

The flow of air through a duct system follows a strict energy balance governed by the **modified Bernoulli equation** plus the cumulative **friction losses** and **dynamic losses** at every fitting, bend, and terminal.

**Step 1 — Air Generation:** The AHU blower or a fan (centrifugal / axial) imparts **total pressure** $P_t$ to the air. This total pressure is split into:
- **Static Pressure ($P_s$)**: The potential energy per unit volume; the pressure the air exerts on duct walls.
- **Velocity Pressure ($P_v$)**: The kinetic energy per unit volume; dependent on air velocity.

**Step 2 — Transport through Mains and Branches:** As air travels, friction between air and duct walls, plus turbulence at fittings, converts total pressure into **heat (lost energy)**, gradually decreasing the available pressure at each downstream tap.

**Step 3 — Terminal Discharge:** Diffusers and grilles convert the remaining velocity pressure back into static pressure (pressure recovery), projecting the air into the conditioned space at a designed **throw** and **drop**.

**Step 4 — Return Path:** Return ducts collect the air at lower static pressure (often slightly negative to ensure flow), returning it to the AHU mixing box.

**Step 5 — Re-conditioning:** The AHU filters, cools/heats, dehumidifies, and re-energises the air, and the cycle restarts.

### 2.2 The Three Fundamental Air Pressures

The relationship between the three pressures measured during the lab experiment is:

$$P_t = P_s + P_v$$

where:
- $P_t$ = **Total Pressure** (Pa)
- $P_s$ = **Static Pressure** (Pa)
- $P_v$ = **Velocity Pressure** (Pa) = $\dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Highlight:** A **Pitot-static tube** is inserted into the duct to read $P_t$ and $P_s$ simultaneously. The difference (which the instrument gives directly) is $P_v$, and the air velocity is then back-calculated as $V = \sqrt{2 P_v / \rho}$.

### 2.3 Friction Loss in Ducts (Darcy–Weisbach Equation)

Friction loss in a straight duct of length $L$ and hydraulic diameter $D_h$ carrying air at velocity $V$ is given by:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

For rectangular ducts of dimensions $a \times b$ (in mm), the equivalent hydraulic diameter is:

$$D_h = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$$

In HVAC practice, friction loss is more conveniently expressed **per unit length** and **per unit volume flow rate**, leading to tabulated values in design charts (e.g., the ASHRAE/IDEC friction charts).

### 2.4 Duct Sizing Methods

There are two principal engineering methods to size a duct network:

**Method A — Equal Friction Method (Static Pressure Balance):**
Each section of duct is sized so that the **frictional resistance per unit length is the same** throughout. The total pressure drop across the longest run sets the fan static pressure. Simple, but downstream branches may have inadequate air.

**Method B — Static Regain Method (used in high-velocity systems):**
The static pressure **lost in friction is partially recovered** at each branch take-off, equalising the static pressure at the entrance of every branch. Preferred for large commercial installations to balance air without dampers.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Velocity from velocity pressure | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_v}{\rho}}$ | m/s | Use $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³ |
| 2 | Total pressure | $P_t = P_s + P_v$ | Pa | Pitot-static reading difference |
| 3 | Volume flow rate (duct) | $\dot{V} = A \cdot V$ | m³/s | $A$ = duct cross-section area |
| 4 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$ | kg/s | Used for cooling load checks |
| 5 | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D_h} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Pa | For straight duct sections |
| 6 | Hydraulic diameter (rect. duct) | $D_h = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$ | m | $a, b$ in metres |
| 7 | Round equivalent of rectangular | $D_{eq} = 1.30 \left[\dfrac{(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}\right]$ | m | For friction-chart lookup |
| 8 | Specific fan power | $SFP = \dfrac{P_{fan}}{\dot{V}}$ | W/m³/s | Energy metric, KTU/IS/IGBC focus |
| 9 | Air changes per hour | $ACH = \dfrac{\dot{V} \times 3600}{V_{room}}$ | 1/h | For ventilation adequacy |
| 10 | Throw of a diffuser | $T \approx 0.5 \cdot V_{neck} \cdot K$ | m | Empirical; $K$ = 0.5 to 0.7 |
| 11 | Effective cooling load from air | $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ | kW | Sensible cooling via distribution |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Air distribution studies form the **backbone of energy-efficient HVAC design**:
- A poorly designed duct system can waste **30–50 %** of chiller energy through fan power and leakage.
- Properly designed low-velocity systems ensure **draft-free comfort** (air speed at occupant level < **0.25 m/s**).
- **LEED / GRIHA / ECBC (India)** certification credits are awarded for high SFP reduction and proper air balancing.
- In industrial cleanrooms, uniform air distribution ensures **ISO Class 5/7/8** particulate compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Velocity from Velocity Pressure

The kinetic energy per unit volume of a moving fluid equals the **velocity pressure** $P_v$. For an incompressible fluid (air at low speeds):

$$P_v = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

Rearranging for $V$:

$$V^2 = \frac{2 P_v}{\rho} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V = \sqrt{\frac{2 P_v}{\rho}}$$

For air at standard conditions ($\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³):

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2 P_v}{1.20}} = 1.291 \sqrt{P_v}$$

If a Pitot-static traverse gives $P_v = 18$ Pa, then $V = 1.291 \times \sqrt{18} = 1.291 \times 4.243 = 5.48$ m/s.

### 3.2 Derivation: Volume Flow Rate from Duct Traverse

Air flow in a duct is computed by integrating local velocity over the cross-section. By the **log-linear traverse method (Log-Tchebycheff rule)**, readings are taken at points on a log-linear grid, and an average velocity is obtained:

$$\bar{V} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$

The volumetric flow rate is then:

$$\dot{V} = A \cdot \bar{V}$$

where $A$ is the duct cross-sectional area. If the duct is rectangular $a \times b$:

$$A = a \cdot b$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A 600 mm × 400 mm supply duct gives an average velocity of 8.5 m/s from a Pitot traverse.

Step 1: Convert duct dimensions to metres: $a = 0.6$ m, $b = 0.4$ m.

Step 2: Area $A = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24$ m².

Step 3: Volume flow rate $\dot{V} = 0.24 \times 8.5 = 2.04$ m³/s.

Step 4: Convert to CMM (cubic metres per minute): $\dot{V} = 2.04 \times 60 = 122.4$ m³/min.

### 3.3 Derivation: Friction Loss Across a Straight Duct

For a duct segment, the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** is derived from dimensional analysis of viscous and turbulent wall shear stress:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

For **air**, with $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³, and using the **friction factor** $f$ from the Colebrook equation (or from the smooth-duct Blasius approximation for turbulent flow, $f = 0.079\, Re^{-0.25}$):

$$Re = \frac{V D_h}{\nu}$$

with kinematic viscosity of air $\nu = 1.516 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A 30 m long circular duct of diameter 0.4 m carries air at 10 m/s.

Step 1: Reynolds number $Re = (10 \times 0.4) / 1.516 \times 10^{-5} = 2.639 \times 10^{5}$ (turbulent).

Step 2: Blasius friction factor $f = 0.079 \times (2.639 \times 10^{5})^{-0.25} = 0.079 / 22.66 = 0.00349$.

Step 3: Friction loss $\Delta P_f = 0.00349 \times (30/0.4) \times (1.20 \times 10^2 / 2) = 0.00349 \times 75 \times 60 = 15.7$ Pa.

### 3.4 Derivation: Equal Friction Method — Required Duct Size

Given a target friction rate $\Delta P_f / L$ (e.g., **1.0 Pa/m** for low-velocity systems), the required diameter for a circular duct carrying volume flow $\dot{V}$ is found by combining:

$$\dot{V} = A V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} V$$

$$\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{f}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Simultaneously solving these (or using ASHRAE friction charts) gives $D$.

For a rectangular duct of aspect ratio $\le 3:1$, the **equivalent round duct diameter** is:

$$D_{eq} = 1.30 \, \frac{(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Design a rectangular duct (aspect 2:1) for 0.5 m³/s at 1.0 Pa/m friction rate.

Step 1: Guess $a = 0.4$ m, $b = 0.2$ m. Area $A = 0.08$ m², $V = 0.5/0.08 = 6.25$ m/s.

Step 2: $D_h = (2 \times 0.4 \times 0.2)/(0.4+0.2) = 0.267$ m.

Step 3: $Re = (6.25 \times 0.267)/1.516 \times 10^{-5} = 1.10 \times 10^{5}$. $f \approx 0.0046$.

Step 4: $\Delta P_f / L = 0.0046 / 0.267 \times 1.20 \times 6.25^2 / 2 = 1.01$ Pa/m ✓.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Diffuser Performance Characteristics

| Diffuser Type | Throw (m) | Pressure Drop (Pa) | Sound (NC) | Best Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Ceiling Slot | 1.5 – 4.5 | 5 – 25 | 20 – 30 | Linear perimeter zones |
| Round Ceiling (Cone) | 1.8 – 6.0 | 12 – 50 | 25 – 40 | Open office areas |
| Louvered Face | 2.0 – 6.0 | 15 – 60 | 25 – 40 | High-bay industrial |
| Sill / Floor Grille | 1.0 – 3.0 | 8 – 25 | 20 – 35 | Perimeter heating |
| Swirl Diffuser | 2.5 – 7.5 | 20 – 70 | 25 – 45 | Large open-plan halls |
| Nozzle (high-velocity) | 15 – 60 | 100 – 400 | 35 – 50 | Industrial ventilation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Throw** is the horizontal distance from the diffuser to where the air velocity drops to **0.25 m/s** (terminal velocity for comfort). **Drop** is the vertical distance the cold air descends before stabilising (typically 1.5 – 2.5 m). **Spread** is the radial or lateral spread at the terminal-velocity boundary.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Duct Material Pressure Ratings

| Duct Material | Max Velocity (m/s) | Max Static Pressure (Pa) | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Galvanised Iron (GI) Sheet | 20 | 1500 | Standard commercial |
| Aluminium | 15 | 1000 | Light, corrosion-resistant |
| Fibreglass Reinforced Plastic | 18 | 1200 | Corrosive exhaust |
| Pre-insulated (Phenolic) | 12 | 750 | Energy-efficient |
| Fabric (non-permeable) | 10 | 500 | Cleanroom, low-pressure |
| Flex Duct (limited length) | 8 | 250 | Branch connections only |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Air Distribution System (Experiment Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AHU[AHU / Fan Unit] --> PLE[Plenum Chamber]
    PLE --> MAINS[Main Supply Duct]
    MAINS --> BR1[Branch Duct 1]
    MAINS --> BR2[Branch Duct 2]
    MAINS --> BR3[Branch Duct 3]
    BR1 --> DF1[Ceiling Diffuser 1]
    BR2 --> DF2[Ceiling Diffuser 2]
    BR3 --> DF3[Ceiling Diffuser 3]
    DF1 --> ROOM1[Conditioned Room 1]
    DF2 --> ROOM2[Conditioned Room 2]
    DF3 --> ROOM3[Conditioned Room 3]
    ROOM1 --> RG1[Return Grille 1]
    ROOM2 --> RG2[Return Grille 2]
    ROOM3 --> RG3[Return Grille 3]
    RG1 --> RET[Return Duct Main]
    RG2 --> RET
    RG3 --> RET
    RET --> AHU
    PITOT1[Pitot Static Probe 1] -. Measures .-> MAINS
    PITOT2[Pitot Static Probe 2] -. Measures .-> BR2
    MANO[Inclined Manometer] --- PITOT1
    MANO --- PITOT2
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Pitot-Static Traverse Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DS[Air Stream in Duct] --> PST[Pitot Static Tube Inserted]
    PST --> PT[Total Pressure Tap]
    PST --> PS[Static Pressure Tap]
    PT --> M1[Manometer Limb 1]
    PS --> M2[Manometer Limb 2]
    M1 --> DIFF[Differential Reading PV]
    M2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> CALC[Velocity Calculation]
    CALC --> VOL[Volume Flow Rate]
    VOL --> BAL[Air Balancing Report]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Duct Sizing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Known Volume Flow Q] --> CHOICE{System Type?}
    CHOICE -- Low Velocity --> EFM[Equal Friction Method]
    CHOICE -- High Velocity --> SRM[Static Regain Method]
    EFM --> FR[Set Friction Rate e.g. 1 Pa per m]
    FR --> EQ[Equivalent Round Diameter from Charts]
    SR --> REC[Static Pressure Recovery at Branches]
    REC --> EQ
    EQ --> REC1[Rectangular Duct with Aspect Ratio less than 3 to 1]
    REC1 --> CHECK{Velocity within 5 to 9 m/s?}
    CHECK -- No --> ADJ[Adjust Dimensions]
    ADJ --> CHECK
    CHECK -- Yes --> FINAL[Final Duct Schedule]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Diffuser Performance Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FAN[Centrifugal Blower] --> CL[Calibrated Flow Nozzle or Orifice]
    CL --> FLEX[Flexible Connection]
    FLEX --> PLEN[Test Plenum with Diffuser Mounted]
    PLEN --> DIFF[Diffuser Under Test]
    DIFF --> ROOM[Test Room or Open Floor]
    PLEN --> PITOT[Pitot Probe in Plenum Neck]
    PITOT --> MIC[Micro manometer]
    ANEMO[Anemometer at Throw Distance] -. Measures Terminal Velocity .-> ROOM
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
Define **air distribution system** and list any two common types of supply terminal devices used in HVAC systems.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> An air distribution system is the network of ducts, fans, dampers, and terminal devices used to deliver conditioned air to occupied spaces at the desired temperature, humidity, velocity, and purity. **(2 marks)**
> Two common supply terminal devices are: (i) **ceiling diffusers** (round, square, or slot type) and (ii) **linear bar grilles** / **sill grilles**. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
Differentiate between **static pressure** and **velocity pressure** in an air duct. State one instrument used to measure each.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Static pressure** is the potential energy of the air per unit volume — it is the pressure the air exerts on the duct walls in all directions. **(1 mark)**
> **Velocity pressure** is the kinetic energy of the air per unit volume — it depends on the square of the air velocity ($P_v = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$) and is a directional quantity along the flow axis. **(1 mark)**
> Both pressures are measured together using a **Pitot-static tube** connected to an **inclined manometer**; the difference between total and static pressure gives the velocity pressure. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common error #1:** Students often use $\rho = 1.0$ kg/m³ instead of the correct **1.20 kg/m³** for air at standard conditions. This causes a **5–8 % error** in velocity and flow calculations.
> - **Common error #2:** Forgetting to convert **mm to m** before computing area or hydraulic diameter (e.g., using 400 mm as 400 m). Always unit-convert at Step 1.
> - **Common error #3:** Using **velocity pressure** as **static pressure** in fan selection — these are different quantities. Fan selection uses the **total static pressure** (sum of friction + fitting losses + terminal device pressure).
> - **Common error #4:** Failing to draw a **labelled schematic** of the test rig — KTU examiners award **2 marks** specifically for a clean, labelled diagram in 14-mark questions.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — 14-Mark Long Answer**

**Q. (a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **experimental procedure** to study the performance of an air distribution system in a laboratory, including the measurement of static and velocity pressures, and the calculation of volume flow rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a Pitot-static traverse in a 500 mm × 300 mm rectangular supply duct, the following velocity pressure readings (in Pa) were obtained at the centroid of 9 equal-area sub-rectangles: **6, 8, 9, 7, 11, 12, 9, 8, 10**. Calculate:
(i) The average air velocity. **(2 marks)**
(ii) The volume flow rate in m³/s and CMM. **(3 marks)**
(iii) The mass flow rate, assuming $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution (a):**
1. **Apparatus:** Centrifugal blower, calibrated duct section, Pitot-static tube, inclined micro-manometer, anemometer, tape measure, stopwatch. **(1 mark for diagram)**

2. **Procedure:** **(4 marks)**
   - (i) Set up the duct with the Pitot-static tube inserted perpendicular to flow.
   - (ii) Connect the static and total pressure taps to the two limbs of the inclined manometer.
   - (iii) Start the blower and stabilise flow.
   - (iv) Traverse the duct: read velocity pressure at 9 (or 25 for circular) equal-area points.
   - (v) Record the manometer readings, ambient temperature, and barometric pressure.
   - (vi) At the diffuser face, measure throw and drop with an anemometer.
   - (vii) Tabulate and compute velocity, flow rate, and friction loss.

3. **Calculations:** **(2 marks)**
   - $V_i = \sqrt{2 P_{v,i} / \rho}$ for each point; $\bar{V} = \frac{1}{n} \sum V_i$; $\dot{V} = A \bar{V}$.

**Solution (b):**

Given: $P_v$ values (Pa) = 6, 8, 9, 7, 11, 12, 9, 8, 10. Duct: 500 mm × 300 mm = 0.5 m × 0.3 m. $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³.

**Step (i) — Compute velocity at each point** using $V_i = 1.291 \sqrt{P_{v,i}}$:

$$V_1 = 1.291 \sqrt{6} = 1.291 \times 2.449 = 3.16 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_2 = 1.291 \sqrt{8} = 1.291 \times 2.828 = 3.65 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_3 = 1.291 \sqrt{9} = 1.291 \times 3.000 = 3.87 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_4 = 1.291 \sqrt{7} = 1.291 \times 2.646 = 3.42 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_5 = 1.291 \sqrt{11} = 1.291 \times 3.317 = 4.28 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_6 = 1.291 \sqrt{12} = 1.291 \times 3.464 = 4.47 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_7 = 1.291 \sqrt{9} = 1.291 \times 3.000 = 3.87 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_8 = 1.291 \sqrt{8} = 1.291 \times 2.828 = 3.65 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_9 = 1.291 \sqrt{10} = 1.291 \times 3.162 = 4.08 \text{ m/s}$$

Average velocity:

$$\bar{V} = \frac{3.16 + 3.65 + 3.87 + 3.42 + 4.28 + 4.47 + 3.87 + 3.65 + 4.08}{9}$$

$$\bar{V} = \frac{34.45}{9} = 3.83 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (ii) — Volume flow rate:**

$$A = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\dot{V} = A \bar{V} = 0.15 \times 3.83 = 0.5745 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

In CMM:

$$\dot{V} = 0.5745 \times 60 = 34.47 \text{ CMM}$$

**Step (iii) — Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V} = 1.20 \times 0.5745 = 0.689 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 14-Mark Long Answer**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Equal Friction Method** and **Static Regain Method** of duct sizing, with a comparison table of advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular GI duct of diameter 0.45 m and length 25 m carries air at 12 m/s. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
(i) The Reynolds number, taking kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.516 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. **(2 marks)**
(ii) The friction factor using the Blasius equation $f = 0.079 Re^{-0.25}$. **(2 marks)**
(iii) The frictional pressure loss across the duct. **(3 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution (a):**

1. **Equal Friction Method (EFM):** **(3 marks)**
   - Ducts are sized such that the **frictional pressure drop per unit length is constant** throughout the system (e.g., 1 Pa/m).
   - The **friction rate** is set to a design value based on allowable fan static pressure divided by longest run length.
   - For each branch, the diameter (or equivalent round) is read from friction charts given the volume flow rate.

2. **Static Regain Method (SRM):** **(2 marks)**
   - Used primarily in **high-velocity systems**.
   - Static pressure is **recovered at each branch** by the enlargement of the main as flow decreases.
   - Sizes are chosen so that the **static pressure at each branch entrance is equalised**, reducing or eliminating balancing dampers.

3. **Comparison Table:** **(2 marks)**

| Aspect | Equal Friction Method | Static Regain Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Principle | Constant Pa/m | Constant static pressure at branches |
| Best for | Low-velocity systems | High-velocity systems |
| Balancing | Needs dampers | Mostly self-balancing |
| Duct sizes | Larger toward fan | Larger, more uniform |
| Complexity | Simple | More calculation-intensive |
| Energy | Higher fan power | Lower fan power |

**Solution (b):**

Given: $D = 0.45$ m, $L = 25$ m, $V = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.516 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Step (i) — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{12 \times 0.45}{1.516 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{5.4}{1.516 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.562 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step (ii) — Friction factor (Blasius):**

$$f = 0.079 \cdot Re^{-0.25} = 0.079 \times (3.562 \times 10^{5})^{-0.25}$$

$$(3.562 \times 10^{5})^{0.25} = (356200)^{0.25} = 24.39$$

$$f = \frac{0.079}{24.39} = 0.00324$$

**Step (iii) — Friction loss:**

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = 0.00324 \times \frac{25}{0.45} \times \frac{1.20 \times 12^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.00324 \times 55.56 \times \frac{172.8}{2}$$

$$= 0.00324 \times 55.56 \times 86.4$$

$$= 15.55 \text{ Pa}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PCAGL507 Module Experiment 2**

- ✅ **Air Distribution System (ADS)** = Ducts + Fan + Terminals + Dampers, delivering conditioned air uniformly.
- ✅ **Three pressures:** $P_t = P_s + P_v$, where $P_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$.
- ✅ **Air density (standard):** $\rho = \mathbf{1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3}$; **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mathbf{1.516 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$.
- ✅ **Velocity from velocity pressure:** $V = \sqrt{2P_v/\rho} = 1.291\sqrt{P_v}$ (using $\rho = 1.20$).
- ✅ **Volume flow:** $\dot{V} = A \cdot \bar{V}$ in **m³/s**; multiply by 60 for **CMM**; by 3600 for **m³/h**.
- ✅ **Mass flow:** $\dot{m} = \rho A \bar{V}$ in **kg/s**.
- ✅ **Darcy–Weisbach:** $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D_h} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$; for smooth turbulent air ducts use **Blasius: $f = 0.079 Re^{-0.25}$**.
- ✅ **Rectangular equivalent diameter:** $D_h = 2ab/(a+b)$ (hydraulic); $D_{eq} = 1.30 \dfrac{(ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$ (friction).
- ✅ **Recommended velocities:** Supply ducts **5–9 m/s** (low); **15–25 m/s** (high-velocity); return ducts **4–7 m/s**.
- ✅ **Duct sizing methods:** Equal Friction (constant Pa/m) and Static Regain (constant static pressure).
- ✅ **Diffuser performance parameters:** Throw (m), Drop (m), Spread (m), NC rating, Pressure Drop (Pa), Sound Criteria.
- ✅ **Pitot-static traverse grid:** **9 points** for rectangular, **25 points** for circular ducts (Log-Tchebycheff).
- ✅ **Comfort air velocity at occupant level:** $\le \mathbf{0.25 \text{ m/s}}$ (draft-free zone).
- ✅ **Specific Fan Power (SFP)** is the energy benchmark; ECBC India mandates **SFP $\le$ 2.0 W/(L/s)** for office AHUs.
- ✅ **Air Changes per Hour (ACH):** $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \times 3600}{V_{room}}$; **Office: 6–10 ACH**, **Auditorium: 8–15 ACH**, **Hospital OT: 20–25 ACH**.
- ✅ **Static, Velocity, Total Pressure** measured using **Pitot-static tube + inclined micro-manometer**.
- ✅ **Lab deliverable:** Report should include **schematic diagram (2 marks)**, **procedure (2 marks)**, **observations table (3 marks)**, **calculations (5 marks)**, **results and graphs (2 marks)** as per KTU valuation pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visit to Ice Plant and Cold Storage or Building Energy Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL507 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING LABORATORY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Visit to Ice Plant and Cold Storage or Building Energy Studies ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Industrial Visit to an Ice Plant and Cold Storage #### Formal Academic Definition An **Ice Plant** is an industrial refrigeration facility specifically engineered for the mass production of ice in various geometric forms (block, tube, plate, or flake) using dedicated vapour-compression or absorption refrigeration cycles, typically operating with ammonia (R-717) as the working fluid. A **Cold Storage** (or **Cold Chain Warehouse**) is a temperature-controlled insulated enclosure designed to preserve perishable commodities—frozen foods, dairy, pharmaceuticals, fruits, and vegetables—by maintaining specific temperature and relative humidity regimes dictated by the stored product's biochemical respiration rate and microbial decay kinetics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Reference:** > As per the KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual, Module Experiment 2 mandates a **technical site visit** to either (a) an **Ice Plant and Cold Storage** OR (b) a **Building Energy Studies** site. The student is expected to identify major equipment, trace the refrigerant flow, record operating parameters, and compute performance indices. #### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of your home refrigerator—but scaled up by a factor of **1000× to 10,000×**. Your home fridge keeps food "fresh" for a week; a cold storage keeps food "fresh" for **6 to 24 months**. The ice plant is essentially a giant ice-making machine that exploits water's latent heat of fusion (**334 kJ/kg** or equivalently **80 kcal/kg**) to extract heat. The cold storage is a "thermal fortress" wrapped in thick insulation (PUF, EPS, or Cork) that fights heat ingress 24/7. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants to Remember:** > - Latent heat of fusion of water: **$h_{if}$ = 334 kJ/kg = 80 kcal/kg** > - Latent heat of vaporization of ammonia (R-717) at –15 °C: **$\approx$ 1314 kJ/kg** > - Standard Specific Heat of Water: **$c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/kg·K** > - Standard Density of Water at 4 °C: **$\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³** > - Standard Density of Block Ice: **$\rho_{ice} \approx 917$ kg/m³** ### 1.2 Building Energy Studies (BES) – Alternative Visit #### Formal Academic Definition **Building Energy Studies (BES)** is a systematic, engineering investigation that quantifies a building's energy consumption profile—primarily the HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) load—through measured data acquisition, energy audit protocols (per ASHRAE Level 1, 2, or 3), and thermal load calculations per **ISHRAE / ASHRAE Handbook of Fundamentals**. The objective is to identify energy wastage, propose conservation measures (ECMs), and benchmark the **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** in **kWh/m²/year**. #### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine your building is a person running a marathon. The **HVAC system** is the person's lungs, the **building envelope** (walls, roof, glass) is the skin, and **solar radiation** is the sun's heat pounding on them. BES is the act of strapping a fitness tracker to the building, measuring every breath (kW of cooling) and every drop of sweat (kWh per year), then coaching the building to run leaner. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Standard EPI Benchmarks (ECBC / BEE India):** > - Office Building (Existing): **EPI = 180 to 250 kWh/m²/yr** > - Office Building (ECBC Compliant): **EPI $\leq$ 120 kWh/m²/yr** > - SuperECBC / Net-Zero Target: **EPI $\leq$ 50 kWh/m²/yr** > - Typical Residential Building: **EPI = 50 to 100 kWh/m²/yr** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Ice Plant: Subsystem Operational Logic The ice plant consists of six major sub-systems operating in a closed refrigerant loop: 1. **Compressor Bank:** Reciprocating (most common) or screw compressors. Ammonia compressors typically operate at **discharge temperatures between 70 °C and 90 °C** to avoid superheat-induced oil breakdown. 2. **Condenser:** Either an **evaporative condenser** (CTI-rated) or a shell-and-tube water-cooled condenser. Condensing temperature for ammonia: **$\approx$ 30 °C to 38 °C** (corresponding to saturation pressures of 11.67 bar to 14.50 bar absolute). 3. **High-Pressure Liquid Receiver:** Accumulator for condensed liquid ammonia. 4. **Expansion Device:** Float-type high-pressure float valve or thermostatic expansion valve (TXV). Ammonia plants predominantly use **automatic float valves** for stable evaporator feeding. 5. **Evaporator (Ice-Making Sub-system):** This is the unique component—**can ice, plate ice, tube ice, or block ice** generators. 6. **Refrigerant Piping Network:** Steel Schedule 40/80 black pipe with welded joints (ammonia systems avoid copper due to corrosion). #### Why Each Step Matters (Operational Logic) - **Why ammonia (R-717)?** Because of its excellent thermodynamic properties: high latent heat (**1314 kJ/kg at –15 °C**), zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential), zero GWP (Global Warming Potential), and low cost (~₹100/kg). The only drawback is its **mild toxicity (B2L classification)** and pungent odour, which requires leak-detection sensors. - **Why evaporative condensers?** They save 60–70 % of condensing water compared to shell-and-tube condensers and operate at lower condensing temperatures (lower compressor work). > [!NOTE] > **Ice Manufacturing Variants (KTU High-Yield):** > - **Can Ice:** 25 kg, 50 kg, 75 kg, 150 kg blocks; freezing time **8 to 12 hours per can**; harvest via hot gas defrost. > - **Tube Ice:** Hollow cylindrical 29 mm OD × 50 mm long; continuous production; harvest via hot gas + mechanical cutter. > - **Plate Ice:** Flat 10 mm thick plates on the surface of a refrigerated plate; harvest via hot gas; harvest cycle 8–12 min. > - **Flake Ice:** Irregular flakes, 1–2 mm thick; used in fisheries and concrete cooling. ### 2.2 Cold Storage: Thermal Load Composition The total refrigeration load of a cold storage $Q_{total}$ has **five** principal components: $$ Q_{total} = Q_{transmission} + Q_{product} + Q_{respiration} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{auxiliary} $$ Where: - $Q_{transmission}$: Heat gain through walls, roof, and floor (conduction). - $Q_{product}$: Heat to cool the product from intake temperature to storage temperature + latent heat of freezing (for frozen goods). - $Q_{respiration}$: Heat generated by living produce (fruits/vegetables) through cellular respiration. - $Q_{infiltration}$: Heat due to door openings, air curtains, and personnel entry. - $Q_{auxiliary}$: Heat from lights, fans, defrost heaters, and personnel. ### 2.3 Building Energy Studies: Load Calculation Hierarchy Per **ASHRAE Handbook of Fundamentals (Chapter 17, 18)** and **ISHRAE SP41**, the cooling load $Q_{cooling}$ is computed as: $$ Q_{cooling} = Q_{conduction,\,envelope} + Q_{solar,\,glass} + Q_{ventilation} + Q_{internal} + Q_{misc} $$ The energy consumption is then: $$ E_{annual} = \frac{Q_{cooling,\,peak} \times EFLH_{cooling}}{\eta_{HVAC}} $$ Where **EFLH = Equivalent Full-Load Hours** (a function of climate zone; for Kerala, **EFLH$_{cooling} \approx 1800$ to 2400 hours/year**). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE viva and lab report questions. | # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Numerical Form | Units | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Heat of fusion of water | $h_{if}$ | 334 | kJ/kg | Block ice plant load | | 2 | Latent heat of R-717 (–15 °C) | $h_{fg,NH_3}$ | 1314 | kJ/kg | Plant COP computation | | 3 | COP of refrigeration | $COP$ | $\frac{RE}{W_{comp}}$ | dimensionless | ESE direct question | | 4 | Block ice freezing time | $t_f$ | $\frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot h_{if}}{U \cdot \Delta T}$ | hours | Plant design | | 5 | Transmission load (wall) | $Q_w$ | $U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Cold storage design | | 6 | U-value of PUF insulation | $U_{PUF}$ | $\frac{k_{PUF}}{t_{PUF}} = \frac{0.022}{0.10}$ | 0.22 W/m²·K | Standard 100 mm PUF | | 7 | Sensible heat product load | $Q_{sensible}$ | $m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | Chilled storage | | 8 | Latent heat product load | $Q_{latent}$ | $m \cdot h_{if}$ | kJ | Frozen storage | | 9 | Respiration heat (fruits) | $Q_{resp}$ | $m \cdot q_{resp}$ | W | Potato: 0.012 W/kg; Apple: 0.010 W/kg | | 10 | Energy Performance Index | $EPI$ | $\frac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | kWh/m²/yr | BES core metric | | 11 | Infiltration air heat | $Q_{inf}$ | $\dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Cold storage door | | 12 | Tons of Refrigeration | $TR$ | $\frac{Q}{3.517}$ (when Q in kW) | 1 TR = 3.517 kW | Industry standard | | 13 | NH₃ discharge temp limit | $T_{dis}$ | $\le$ 90 | °C | Ammonia plant safety | | 14 | Cold storage floor area rule | $A$ | $\frac{\text{Storage tonnage} \times 1000}{\rho_{prod} \times h_{stack}}$ | m² | Capacity planning | | 15 | Walk-in cooler U-value | $U_{cooler}$ | 0.25 to 0.35 | W/m²·K | Standard reference | > [!NOTE] > **Note on units in the table:** All separators are written as commas (`,`) rather than vertical pipes (`\vert`) to remain safe in markdown table syntax. The reader should interpret values as per column heading. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ice Plants** in India (Kerala) supply block ice to the **fisheries sector** (Ernakulam, Kollam, Kozhikode coastal belts) and to **concrete cooling** in mass construction projects (e.g., Kochi Metro piling). - **Cold Storages** form the backbone of India's **₹13 lakh crore food processing industry**, with the FCI (Food Corporation of India) operating over 600+ cold chain facilities. - **Building Energy Studies** are now mandatory for buildings exceeding **100 kW connected load** under the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) 2017 / 2023** notified by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Block Ice Freezing Time (Classical Plank Equation) Consider a rectangular ice block of thickness $2a$ (or one-dimensional half-thickness $a$, since cooling occurs from both faces) being frozen in a metal can. The Plank equation for phase-change freezing time is: $$ t_f = \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot h_{if}}{T_{f} - T_{air}} \left[ \frac{a^2}{2 k_{ice}} + \frac{a}{h_{air}} \right] $$ Where: - $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³ - $h_{if} = 334{,}000$ J/kg - $T_{f} = 0$ °C (freezing point of water) - $T_{air}$ = ambient air temperature of the cold room (typically –10 °C for an ice plant cold room) - $a$ = half-thickness of the ice block (m) - $k_{ice} = 2.22$ W/m·K (thermal conductivity of ice) - $h_{air}$ = convective heat transfer coefficient on the cold-room side ($\approx 10$ W/m²·K for still air, 25 W/m²·K for forced air) > [!NOTE] > **The Plank Equation assumes:** > 1. Constant material properties. > 2. Negligible sensible heat contribution (good assumption because $h_{if} \gg c_p \Delta T$). > 3. One-dimensional heat flow. > 4. Constant surface temperature at $T_{air}$. #### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style 14-Mark Question) **Problem:** A block ice plant freezes 25 kg blocks of ice in cans of internal dimensions 250 mm × 250 mm × 500 mm. The cold room air temperature is **–10 °C**. The convective heat transfer coefficient on the air side is **$h_{air} = 12$ W/m²·K**. Determine the freezing time per block. **Step 1 — Compute the half-thickness $a$:** The heat flows primarily from the **top and bottom** faces (the lateral faces contribute < 5 % for a tall can). The dominant dimension is therefore the **height** of 500 mm, but cooling is symmetric from both top and bottom → $a = 0.250$ m. **Step 2 — Identify all constants:** $$ \rho_{ice} = 917 \text{ kg/m³}, \quad h_{if} = 334{,}000 \text{ J/kg} $$ $$ T_{f} = 0 \text{ °C}, \quad T_{air} = -10 \text{ °C} $$ $$ \Delta T = T_{f} - T_{air} = 0 - (-10) = 10 \text{ K} $$ $$ k_{ice} = 2.22 \text{ W/m·K}, \quad h_{air} = 12 \text{ W/m²·K} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the Plank equation:** $$ t_f = \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot h_{if}}{\Delta T} \left[ \frac{a^2}{2 k_{ice}} + \frac{a}{h_{air}} \right] $$ **Step 4 — Internal conduction term:** $$ \frac{a^2}{2 k_{ice}} = \frac{(0.250)^2}{2 \times 2.22} = \frac{0.0625}{4.44} = 0.01408 \text{ m²·K/W} $$ **Step 5 — External convection term:** $$ \frac{a}{h_{air}} = \frac{0.250}{12} = 0.02083 \text{ m²·K/W} $$ **Step 6 — Sum of thermal resistances (per unit area):** $$ R_{total} = 0.01408 + 0.02083 = 0.03491 \text{ m²·K/W} $$ **Step 7 — Pre-factor:** $$ \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot h_{if}}{\Delta T} = \frac{917 \times 334{,}000}{10} = 30{,}627{,}800 \text{ J/(m³·K)} $$ **Step 8 — Final multiplication:** $$ t_f = 30{,}627{,}800 \times 0.03491 $$ $$ t_f = 1{,}069{,}221 \text{ s} $$ **Step 9 — Convert to hours:** $$ t_f = \frac{1{,}069{,}221}{3600} = 296.95 \text{ hours} \approx 297 \text{ hours} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Engineer's Reality Check:** A 297-hour freezing time is unrealistic for a commercial plant. This indicates that the dominant cooling should be from the **lateral walls** (smaller thickness ≈ 0.125 m), giving $a = 0.125$ m, which would yield $t_f \approx 80$ to 110 hours—matching the actual industrial cycle of 8 to 12 hours only when **forced brine circulation** is used (where $h_{air}$ becomes $h_{brine} \approx 200$–500 W/m²·K). **This is the key conceptual trap** in KTU viva questions. ### 3.2 Derivation: COP of a Vapour Compression Plant For a saturated liquid + saturated vapour cycle (theoretical Carnot-equivalent): $$ COP_{ref} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} $$ The **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** is the enthalpy difference across the evaporator: $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_{g}(T_{evap}) - h_{f}(T_{cond}) $$ And the **Compressor Work** is: $$ W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1 $$ Where state 1 = compressor inlet (saturated vapour), state 2 = compressor outlet (superheated vapour), state 3 = condenser outlet (saturated liquid), state 4 = evaporator inlet (after isenthalpic expansion). ### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Performance Index (EPI) $$ EPI = \frac{E_{annual} \text{ [kWh/yr]}}{A_{floor} \text{ [m²]}} $$ **Worked Example:** A 500 m² office building consumes 80,000 kWh/year of total electricity, of which 55 % is HVAC. $$ E_{annual,\,HVAC} = 0.55 \times 80{,}000 = 44{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr} $$ $$ EPI = \frac{44{,}000}{500} = 88 \text{ kWh/m²/yr} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** This is below the ECBC 2023 target of 120 kWh/m²/yr, indicating the building is **ECBC-compliant**. The remaining 45 % energy is for lighting, plug loads, and elevators. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** > *List the major components of an ammonia-based ice plant and state the function of each.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Compressor** — Raises refrigerant pressure from low (1.5 to 3 bar) to high (10 to 14 bar) [1 mark]. 2. **Condenser** — Rejects latent heat of condensation to the atmosphere or cooling water [1 mark]. 3. **Expansion Valve (Float Valve)** — Reduces pressure to evaporator pressure, causing partial flash evaporation [0.5 mark]. 4. **Evaporator (Ice Can/Plate)** — Absorbs heat from water, freezing it into ice [0.5 mark]. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > *Define Energy Performance Index (EPI) and give two typical values for a Kerala office building.* **Model Answer:** The **EPI** is the annual energy consumption of a building divided by its conditioned floor area, expressed in **kWh/m²/yr** [2 marks]. Typical values for a Kerala office building: **Existing = 180 to 250 kWh/m²/yr**; **ECBC-compliant = 90 to 120 kWh/m²/yr** [1 mark]. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Choice A]** > *(a)* With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the layout of an industrial **block ice plant** using ammonia (R-717) as the refrigerant. Explain the function of the float valve and the hot-gas defrost line. **[7 Marks]** > *(b)* A cold storage room has internal dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 5 m and is maintained at **–20 °C**. The walls are constructed with **100 mm PUF** ($k = 0.022$ W/m·K) sandwiched between two layers of **GI sheet and RCC** (combined non-insulation resistance is negligible). The outside ambient temperature is **+35 °C**. Compute the **steady-state heat transmission load** through the walls, roof, and floor. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution to (a):** - **Block diagram** (similar to Section 4.1): marks awarded for correctly identifying **compressor → condenser → receiver → float valve → ice-can evaporator → accumulator → compressor** [3 marks]. - **Float valve function:** Maintains a constant liquid level in the evaporator (ice can) regardless of load variation. It opens when liquid level falls and closes when it rises, ensuring only saturated liquid enters the evaporator [2 marks]. - **Hot-gas defrost line:** During ice harvest, hot discharge gas from the compressor is bypassed directly to the evaporator cans. This melts the thin film of ice (1–2 mm) adhering to the can walls, allowing the ice block to slide out by gravity [2 marks]. **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Compute U-value of the 100 mm PUF wall:** $$ U = \frac{k_{PUF}}{t_{PUF}} = \frac{0.022}{0.100} = 0.22 \text{ W/m²·K} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\Delta T$:** $$ \Delta T = 35 - (-20) = 55 \text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the surface areas:** - Two long walls: $2 \times (10 \times 5) = 100$ m² - Two short walls: $2 \times (8 \times 5) = 80$ m² - Roof: $10 \times 8 = 80$ m² - Floor: $10 \times 8 = 80$ m² - **Total $A = 100 + 80 + 80 + 80 = 340$ m²** **Step 4 — Heat transmission load:** $$ Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.22 \times 340 \times 55 $$ $$ Q = 4{,}114 \text{ W} \approx 4.11 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 5 — Express in TR:** $$ TR = \frac{4.11}{3.517} \approx 1.17 \text{ TR} $$ **Result:** The transmission load is **4.11 kW or 1.17 TR** [7 marks: 1 for U, 1 for ΔT, 2 for areas, 2 for final, 1 for unit conversion]. --- > **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Choice B — Internal Choice]** > *(a)* Discuss the **five components of the total refrigeration load** of a cold storage facility and explain how the **respiration heat** of fresh produce (fruits and vegetables) is estimated. **[7 Marks]** > *(b)* A **500 m² office building** in Thiruvananthapuram has the following measured data: total annual electricity consumption = **120,000 kWh/yr**, of which **HVAC = 55 %, lighting = 25 %, plug loads = 15 %, elevators = 5 %**. Compute the **EPI** for the HVAC subsystem. State whether the building satisfies the **ECBC 2023** benchmark of **120 kWh/m²/yr** for HVAC. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution to (a):** The five load components [5 × 1 = 5 marks]: 1. **$Q_{transmission}$** — heat through walls, roof, floor. 2. **$Q_{product}$** — sensible + latent cooling of incoming product. 3. **$Q_{respiration}$** — heat from biological respiration of living produce. 4. **$Q_{infiltration}$** — heat from door openings and air exchange. 5. **$Q_{auxiliary}$** — heat from lights, fans, defrost, personnel. **Respiration heat estimation** [2 marks]: $$ Q_{respiration} = m_{produce} \times q_{resp} $$ Where $q_{resp}$ is the **specific respiration rate** (W/kg) tabulated by ASHRAE or ISHS publications as a function of produce type and storage temperature. Examples: Potato at 5 °C → $q_{resp} \approx 0.010$ W/kg; Banana at 13 °C → $q_{resp} \approx 0.060$ W/kg. The total respiration load scales linearly with the **mass of stored produce** and is **non-negligible for chilled (above 0 °C) storage** but becomes zero for frozen storage (–18 °C) as microbial activity ceases. **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — HVAC energy per year:** $$ E_{HVAC} = 0.55 \times 120{,}000 = 66{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr} $$ **Step 2 — EPI computation:** $$ EPI = \frac{E_{HVAC}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{66{,}000}{500} = 132 \text{ kWh/m²/yr} $$ **Step 3 — Benchmark comparison:** Since **132 > 120 kWh/m²/yr**, the building **does not satisfy** the ECBC 2023 HVAC benchmark by a margin of 12 kWh/m²/yr. **Step 4 — Recommended ECMs:** Upgrade chiller COP from baseline 2.8 to 4.0; install VFDs on AHU fans; apply reflective roof coating; replace single-glass windows with double-glazed low-E units. [7 marks: 2 for step 1, 2 for step 2, 1 for step 3, 2 for ECMs] > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Mixing **W with kW** in the same equation. Always normalize units first. > 2. **$\Delta T$ Sign Error:** In cold storage, $\Delta T = T_{outside} - T_{inside}$ (always **positive**). Students often compute the reverse. > 3. **EPI Scope Trap:** EPI should be computed **only for HVAC energy** in ECBC context, not total electrical energy. Splitting the percentage is essential. > 4. **Respiration Load Trap:** For **frozen storage (–18 °C)**, respiration load is **zero** (microbial activity ceases). Students wrongly add it. > 5. **Ammonia Toxicity Awareness:** Always mention **B2L classification**, **OEL = 25 ppm**, and the **leak detection sensor requirement** in viva questions about ammonia plants. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Ice plant refrigerant:** **R-717 (Ammonia)** is the industry default; **discharge temperature must be $\le$ 90 °C**; **condensing pressure = 10 to 14 bar**. - [ ] **Block ice freezing:** Plank equation is the **standard tool**; freezing time scales as **$a^2$** (conduction-limited). - [ ] **Hot-gas defrost:** Bypasses the condenser, sending hot compressor discharge directly to the evaporator for ice harvest. - [ ] **Five cold storage loads:** Transmission + Product + Respiration + Infiltration + Auxiliary. - [ ] **PUF insulation:** 100 mm PUF gives **U = 0.22 W/m²·K** — the **standard reference** for cold storage design. - [ ] **Frozen storage temperature:** **–18 °C to –25 °C** (perishable preservation); **chilled storage:** 0 °C to 4 °C. - [ ] **Respiration heat:** Non-zero for **chilled storage only**; **zero for frozen storage** below –10 °C. - [ ] **EPI formula:** Always **EPI = Annual HVAC Energy / Floor Area**, expressed in **kWh/m²/yr**. - [ ] **ECBC 2023 benchmark for HVAC:** $\le$ **120 kWh/m²/yr** for office buildings in Kerala. - [ ] **EPI computation workflow:** Site survey → data logging → load calculation (CLTD/RTS) → simulation (EnergyPlus) → baseline EPI → ECMs → M&V. - [ ] **One TR = 3.517 kW** — memorize this conversion; it appears in **almost every KTU cooling load question**. - [ ] **Tropical Kerala climate:** Ambient design DB = **34 to 36 °C**; WBT = **26 to 28 °C**; EFLH$_{cooling} \approx 1800$ to 2400 hours/year. - [ ] **Viva-ready fact:** Ammonia has **ODP = 0, GWP = 0**, but **toxicity class B2L** with **OEL = 25 ppm (8-hour TWA)**. - [ ] **Energy audit levels (per ASHRAE):** Level 1 (Walk-through), Level 2 (Detailed), Level 3 (Investment-grade). > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip (Lab Report):** When writing your lab record for Module Experiment 2, **always include:** (1) a hand-drawn process flow diagram of the visited plant, (2) a **tabulated data sheet** of observed pressures/temperatures, (3) a **COP calculation** using observed values, and (4) a **conclusion paragraph** identifying **two energy-saving recommendations**. Examiners reward **observation depth** and **calculation rigor** equally.
